title: Mixtral
tldr: Mistral's open-weight mixture-of-experts models routing each token to 2 of 8 experts, cutting inference cost while matching larger dense models.
plain_english: Mixtral is a family of open-source language models that use a technique called mixture-of-experts (MoE): instead of running all parameters on every token, the model picks 2 of 8 specialized sub-networks to process each piece of input. Think of it like a team where only the relevant experts speak up per task, not everyone. You get model quality comparable to a 70-billion-parameter model but only pay (in compute) for running about 13 billion parameters at once.
how_it_works: Mixtral 8×7B and 8×22B stack transformer layers with a gating mechanism that routes each token independently to 2 out of 8 experts per layer. Total parameters stay in memory (46B and 141B respectively), but only the active experts—roughly 13B and 39B per forward pass—perform computation. Each expert is a feed-forward network; the router learns which experts handle which token patterns. Output from the 2 selected experts is combined and passed to the next layer. This design keeps throughput high while maintaining model expressiveness.
why_it_matters: Mixtral proved that open-weight MoE at scale is production-ready, kicking off an MoE renaissance. Organizations can now run model quality approaching 70B-parameter dense models on hardware that fits 13–20B models, dramatically reducing infrastructure cost and latency for inference. It directly enabled follow-ups like DeepSeek-V3 and influenced Llama 4's architecture, making MoE a standard open-source strategy.
example: A team running Mixtral 8×7B on an A100 GPU achieves similar quality to Llama 2 70B inference but with 4–5× less memory and 2–3× faster token generation. They use vLLM for batched inference, serving 10–15 concurrent requests where a 70B model would handle 2–3. The trade-off: slightly higher latency per expert selection, but batch throughput more than compensates.
pitfalls: Assuming 'total parameters' is the inference cost; only active parameters per forward pass count toward compute and latency.
Routing imbalance: if the gating network learns to route most tokens to 1–2 experts, you waste compute and lose the MoE benefit.
Not all inference frameworks handle MoE efficiently; vLLM and llama.cpp optimize it well, but some backends serialize expert dispatch and negate speed gains.
Token-level routing breaks some training tricks (e.g., gradient checkpointing across experts) and complicates multi-GPU sharding.
when_use: Use Mixtral when you need dense-model quality but have memory or latency constraints, or when you're serving many concurrent requests with limited GPU VRAM.
when_avoid: Avoid Mixtral if your workload is single-batch latency-critical (routing overhead adds ~5–10ms) or if your inference framework doesn't optimize expert dispatch.

faq.q: What is the difference between Mixtral 8×7B and 8×22B?
faq.a: 8×7B has 8 experts of 7B each (46B total, ~13B active per token); 8×22B has larger experts (141B total, ~39B active). 8×22B is more capable but needs more VRAM and compute per token.

faq.q: Is Mixtral faster than Llama 2 70B?
faq.a: Yes, significantly faster for throughput and latency. Mixtral uses ~13B active parameters vs. Llama 2's 70B, so you get 5–10× more tokens/second per GPU at similar quality.

faq.q: Can I run Mixtral on a single consumer GPU?
faq.a: 8×7B fits on an RTX 4090 or A6000 (48GB VRAM), though quantization (4-bit, 8-bit) makes it faster. 8×22B requires 80GB+ or multi-GPU setup.

faq.q: How does Mixtral handle expert imbalance?
faq.a: The gating network learns routing naturally, but load balancing losses during training (auxiliary loss) prevent all tokens from routing to the same experts. If routing collapses, you lose the MoE benefit.

faq.q: Does Mixtral support fine-tuning?
faq.a: Yes, via LoRA or QLoRA on Hugging Face Transformers, though expert routing is typically frozen. Full fine-tuning requires careful gradient handling across expert layers.

title: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
tldr: Architecture that activates only a subset of parameters per token, delivering large-model quality at smaller compute cost.
plain_english: Instead of running every part of a neural network on every input, an MoE model has multiple specialist subnetworks ('experts') and a router that picks which ones to use. Think of a hospital with 46 specialists on staff but only 2 or 3 treating each patient. You store all their expertise in memory, but activate only what you need per request, cutting inference time roughly in half compared to a dense model of the same total size.
how_it_works: An MoE model contains multiple independent expert networks (e.g., 8 experts in Mixtral) plus a learned router that decides which top-K experts handle each token (typically K=2). The router outputs a probability distribution over experts for each position; you activate only the highest-scoring ones. All expert parameters stay in VRAM—sparsity is only in computation, not memory. During inference, you sum the outputs of active experts weighted by router scores. Training requires balancing expert load; imbalanced routing leaves some experts unused and wastes capacity.
why_it_matters: MoE lets you match 70B dense-model quality with ~13B active parameters during inference, roughly 2.5× faster per token. This bridges the efficiency gap that kept open-source deployment expensive. Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, and Llama variants prove MoE scales: you get frontier performance at operating costs that fit on consumer hardware.
example: Mistral deployed Mixtral 8×7B (46B total, ~13B active) and achieved performance near Llama 2 70B while cutting inference latency by half. A team running 1,000 inference requests per day saves ~$200/month in cloud compute versus a dense 46B model, with no quality regression. The router silently steers language tokens to linguistics experts and code tokens to programming experts.
pitfalls: MoE does NOT reduce VRAM—all 46B parameters must fit in memory even if only 13B compute per token; you need the same GPU memory as a dense 46B model.
Load imbalance causes dead experts: if the router always picks the same 2 experts, others waste storage and training doesn't use full capacity.
Batch-size-1 inference shows dramatic speedup, but large batches see shrinking gains because different tokens route to different experts, forcing compute to wait for slowest expert.
Training MoE requires auxiliary loss terms to encourage balanced routing; without them, convergence is slow or unstable.
when_use: Use MoE when you need inference speed on a fixed VRAM budget, operate at scale where compute cost matters more than latency variance, or deploy a model to consumer hardware without sacrificing quality.
when_avoid: Avoid MoE for single-token, ultra-low-latency applications (speculative decoding is simpler), when VRAM is scarce (all experts still fit), or if you cannot tolerate routing overhead and variance across requests.

faq.q: Does mixture of experts reduce VRAM requirements?
faq.a: No. All expert parameters must stay in memory even if only 2 out of 8 are active per token. You need the same VRAM as a dense model of the same total parameter count.

faq.q: Why is Mixtral 8×7B faster than a 46B dense model?
faq.a: Because only ~13B parameters compute per token instead of all 46B. The router picks 2 of 8 experts, cutting computation roughly 2.5× while storing the same knowledge.

faq.q: What happens if the router always picks the same experts?
faq.a: You get load imbalance: unused experts waste memory, training doesn't benefit from full capacity, and you're effectively running a smaller model. Auxiliary loss during training prevents this.

faq.q: Does MoE inference get slower in large batches?
faq.a: Yes. Different tokens route to different experts, so batches stall waiting for the slowest expert group. Batch-size-1 shows maximum speedup; large batches see diminishing returns.

faq.q: Can I use MoE on CPU?
faq.a: Technically yes, but routing overhead and memory bandwidth make it impractical. MoE is designed for GPU; sparse computation gains vanish without parallel hardware.

title: Nomic Embed
tldr: Open-weights text embedding model with reproducible training, open data, and MTEB-competitive performance.
plain_english: Nomic Embed is a text embedding model where you get the weights, the training data, and the code that built it—all public and auditable. Unlike most embedding models where the training pipeline is proprietary, Nomic publishes everything so you can reproduce results or modify them for your use case. It performs as well as closed competitors on standard benchmarks.
how_it_works: Nomic Embed transforms text into dense vectors (numerical representations) that capture semantic meaning. The v1.5+ versions add Matryoshka dimensionality truncation, letting you keep only the most important vector dimensions and reduce memory usage without retraining. Training uses contrastive learning on public data (the Nomic dataset released alongside the model). You load it via Sentence Transformers or HuggingFace and call it like any embedding API. The open training code (in the Nomic GitHub repo) documents every hyperparameter, loss function, and preprocessing step.
why_it_matters: For teams building RAG systems or semantic search, Nomic Embed removes the audit-trail risk: you know exactly what data trained it, can inspect biases in the training set, and can fine-tune it privately without vendor lock-in. Regulatory and compliance teams prefer open-source embeddings over black-box commercial alternatives.
example: A healthcare startup building a documentation search system for patient records chooses Nomic Embed because their compliance team requires full transparency on model training data. They verify that the open training data contains no PHI, run it on-premises, and later fine-tune it on domain-specific medical synonyms using the same open training pipeline.
pitfalls: Assuming open-weights means open-source—Nomic Embed publishes weights and code but uses a commercial license; verify terms before redistributing.
Using matryoshka truncation without benchmarking on your data; optimal dimensions vary by retrieval task.
Treating MTEB benchmark performance as a proxy for RAG quality; benchmark diversity ≠ real-world retrieval recall on your corpus.
Forgetting that Nomic v1 is multilingual in later versions only; v1.0 is English-focused.
when_use: Use Nomic Embed when you need audit-ready embeddings, must self-host without vendor dependency, or require fine-tuning on proprietary data.
when_avoid: Avoid Nomic Embed if you need real-time managed inference with SLA guarantees or prefer the simplicity of a third-party API (OpenAI, Cohere, Jina).

faq.q: Is Nomic Embed free to use?
faq.a: Yes, the weights and code are free, but check the license (currently commercial-friendly but not fully open-source). Inference is free once downloaded; no API costs.

faq.q: How does Nomic Embed compare to OpenAI embeddings?
faq.a: Nomic Embed matches OpenAI on MTEB benchmarks and costs nothing to run, but you must self-host. OpenAI's API is easier for managed inference and supports larger text lengths (8k tokens vs. 8k for Nomic).

faq.q: Can I use Nomic Embed for non-English text?
faq.a: v1.5 and later versions support multilingual embeddings. v1.0 is English-only. Check the model card for supported languages.

faq.q: What is Matryoshka truncation in Nomic Embed?
faq.a: It lets you keep only the first N dimensions of the vector (e.g., 256 instead of 768) without retraining, cutting memory use. MTEB scores drop slightly but remain competitive if dimensions are chosen carefully.

faq.q: Do I need GPU to run Nomic Embed?
faq.a: No. CPU inference works fine for most workloads, though GPU speeds it up significantly. Batch processing on CPU is practical for offline indexing.

title: OpenAI o1 / o3 (Reasoning Models)
tldr: Models that spend compute at inference time on hidden reasoning before answering, trading latency and cost for accuracy on hard tasks.
plain_english: Instead of answering immediately, o1 and o3 take time to think through a problem step-by-step before responding—like a student working through a proof on scratch paper rather than guessing. That internal thinking is hidden from you; you only see the final answer. But the extra thinking dramatically improves performance on math, code, and science questions.
how_it_works: During training, these models learned to generate long chains of internal reasoning tokens via reinforcement learning, then produce an answer. At inference, the model allocates a compute budget (millions of tokens) to think before outputting its response. The thinking tokens are not shown to the user but influence the final answer. o3 extends this with tool use and higher compute budgets, enabling the model to call functions or refine its reasoning mid-stream. The tradeoff is steep: a single query can consume 10–100x more tokens than a standard chat model.
why_it_matters: o1/o3 demonstrate that test-time compute—spending more resources per query rather than only during training—is a new scaling axis. This has shifted how labs approach model design: Anthropic (Claude Thinking), DeepSeek (R1), and others have shipped similar models. It shows diminishing returns on pretraining alone and opens new product and pricing models (paying more for harder queries).
example: A researcher uses o1 to debug a complex PyTorch distributed training bug. The model spends ~2 minutes reasoning through distributed synchronization patterns, memory layout, and gradient flow before proposing a fix. A standard model would guess in seconds but miss the root cause. The cost per query is 5–10x higher, but the answer quality justifies it for the one-time debugging task.
pitfalls: Assuming all queries benefit equally—simple factual questions waste compute on unnecessary thinking; reserve o1/o3 for hard reasoning tasks.
Forgetting that thinking tokens are opaque; you cannot inspect or steer the reasoning process, making debugging model errors harder.
Underestimating latency impact—inference time can exceed 30 seconds for complex queries, breaking real-time chat workflows.
Treating thinking as a silver bullet for alignment—more reasoning does not eliminate hallucination or jailbreak vulnerabilities.
when_use: Use o1/o3 when you face hard math, code, science, or logical reasoning tasks where a correct answer justifies higher latency and cost, and when the query is complex enough that hidden reasoning adds measurable value.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need sub-second latency, are answering factual lookups or retrieval questions, have tight token budgets, or the task does not require multi-step reasoning.

faq.q: What is the difference between o1 and o3?
faq.a: o1 (Sept 2024) introduced hidden chain-of-thought reasoning. o3 (2025) added agentic tool use, higher compute budgets, and stronger performance on graduate-level benchmarks like ARC-AGI. Both use the same core mechanism; o3 is larger and more capable.

faq.q: Can I see what the model is thinking?
faq.a: No. The thinking tokens are hidden from you; the API returns only the final answer. You cannot inspect or debug the reasoning process.

faq.q: How much more expensive is o1 than GPT-4?
faq.a: A typical o1 query costs 5–10x more in tokens than GPT-4 because it uses millions of hidden reasoning tokens. Exact pricing depends on your input and the model's reasoning budget.

faq.q: Does o1 work better for coding than writing?
faq.a: Yes. o1 shows the most gains on competition math, code debugging, and formal reasoning. Writing and summarization see smaller improvements and may not justify the cost.

faq.q: Is o1 faster or slower than GPT-4?
faq.a: Slower. Inference latency often exceeds 10–30 seconds because the model must complete its internal reasoning chain before responding. Use it when accuracy matters more than speed.

title: Parameters (Model Size)
tldr: Learned weights in a neural network; parameter count determines model capability, memory use, and inference speed.
plain_english: Parameters are the internal numbers a model learned during training—like a student's knowledge after studying. A 7B model has 7 billion parameters. More parameters let the model recognize more patterns, but also demand more GPU memory and slower inference.
how_it_works: During training, parameters (weights and biases) are initialized randomly, then adjusted via backpropagation to reduce prediction error. Each parameter contributes to the model's decisions. A larger parameter count increases the model's capacity to store learned patterns, but requires proportionally more VRAM (roughly 2 bytes per parameter in float16). Common sizes: 1B–3B (mobile/edge devices), 7B–13B (consumer GPUs like RTX 4090), 70B+ (high-end enterprise GPUs or quantized for smaller devices).
why_it_matters: Parameter count is your primary constraint when selecting a model for your hardware. A 70B model cannot run on 8GB VRAM without aggressive quantization. Understanding parameter budgets prevents wasted deployment attempts and helps you trade off model capability against inference latency and cost.
example: A team using an RTX 4090 (24GB VRAM) can fit a 13B model in full precision or a 70B model with 4-bit quantization. The same team on a MacBook Air (16GB unified memory) would choose a 7B model quantized to 5–6 bits to avoid swapping to disk.
pitfalls: Assuming parameter count alone predicts capability—architecture (attention heads, hidden dimension) and training data quality matter equally.
Forgetting overhead: activations during inference consume 5–10× the base parameter memory, not just 2 bytes per parameter.
Underestimating quantization gains—4-bit quantization reduces memory by ~75% with <5% quality loss on most tasks.
Conflating inference and training memory—training a 7B model requires 10–20× more VRAM than running inference.
when_use: Use parameter count as your first filter when choosing a model for a target GPU, when comparing inference speed across similar architectures, or when calculating minimum hardware specs.
when_avoid: Avoid using parameter count alone to judge model quality—two 7B models trained on different data or architectures will perform very differently. Don't rely on it to estimate training time or memory during fine-tuning.

faq.q: What do parameters actually do in a neural network?
faq.a: Parameters (weights and biases) are the learned values that transform input data into output predictions. Each parameter contributes a weighted sum to the model's decisions. During training, parameters are adjusted to minimize error.

faq.q: How much VRAM do I need for a 7B parameter model?
faq.a: Roughly 14–16GB for inference in float16 precision (2 bytes per parameter). With 4-bit quantization, you'll need ~3–4GB. Add 20–50% headroom for activations and KV cache, depending on batch size and context length.

faq.q: Is a 13B model always better than a 7B model?
faq.a: Not necessarily. A 13B model has more capacity but trains slower and uses more memory. A smaller model trained on higher-quality data often outperforms a larger model trained on noisy data.

faq.q: Why do some models have the same parameter count but different sizes?
faq.a: Different quantization levels and formats (float32, float16, int8, int4) produce different file sizes. A 7B model in float32 is ~28GB; in int4 with safetensors, it's ~3.5GB.

faq.q: How do parameters affect inference speed?
faq.a: More parameters mean more multiplication operations, so inference is slower. On the same GPU, a 70B model will generate tokens 5–10× slower than a 7B model, all else equal.

title: Phi (Small Language Model Family)
tldr: Microsoft's small models (3.8B–14B) that match larger ones through curated training data instead of scale.
plain_english: Phi is Microsoft's line of small language models that punch above their weight by using carefully selected, high-quality training data. Instead of scaling up to 70B parameters to get strong reasoning, Phi reaches similar performance at 3.8B or 7B by training on textbook-quality examples and synthetic data from better models. Think of it as learning from the best textbooks rather than the entire internet.
how_it_works: Phi models start with a base architecture similar to other transformers, then train on curated datasets: Phi-2 used web-filtered and synthetic reasoning examples; Phi-3 added instruction tuning for multi-task chat; Phi-4 prioritized reasoning by generating synthetic problem-solving examples from a frontier model. The key is data selection—removing low-signal content and synthetic augmentation replaces raw token volume. Smaller context windows (4K–128K) and fewer parameters reduce inference latency and memory footprint while maintaining benchmark performance.
why_it_matters: Phi reframed small-model viability. You can now deploy models under 14B with chat-quality reasoning on edge devices, mobile hardware, or latency-sensitive APIs. This shifts the tradeoff from 'pick between tiny and slow' to 'pick between capable and efficient.' For teams optimizing for inference cost or on-device deployment, Phi is a top-tier contender.
example: A financial services company building a mobile app for customer Q&A uses Phi-3 (7B) instead of a 70B model. The 7B fits on-device, responds in ~500ms, and handles 80% of support queries correctly. Fallback to a larger model or human agent handles the remaining cases—cutting API costs by 90% while improving latency from 3s to 0.5s.
pitfalls: Phi's benchmark gains don't always translate to conversational helpfulness; curated data may leave gaps in casual or creative tasks.
Synthetic data quality depends on the teacher model—if you distill from a weaker base, Phi inherits those blind spots.
Smaller context windows (Phi-3 uses 4K–128K) can truncate long documents without explicit handling.
Phi models are optimized for English reasoning; non-English or domain-specific tasks may degrade faster than scaling to larger models.
when_use: Use Phi when you need reasoning and chat quality below 14B parameters, target latency under 1s, or deploy to edge/mobile; benchmark performance on your task first.
when_avoid: Avoid Phi when you need multilingual fluency, very long context (>128K), creative writing, or when a task hasn't worked on similarly-sized models in your testing.

faq.q: What is the difference between Phi-3 and Phi-4?
faq.a: Phi-3 is a general-purpose chat model (3.8B, 7B, 14B) tuned for multi-task instruction following. Phi-4 (2024–2025) focuses on reasoning and uses synthetic data from a frontier teacher, trading some generalist ability for stronger logic and math.

faq.q: Can I run Phi on my laptop or phone?
faq.a: Yes. Phi-3 7B quantized to 4-bit fits on most modern phones and laptops. You'll need ~4GB VRAM; tools like Ollama, MLX, or ONNX Runtime make this easy.

faq.q: How does Phi compare to Llama?
faq.a: Llama is a family covering 8B–70B+ with broad availability and community tooling. Phi is smaller (3.8B–14B) with higher curated-data investment, so Phi often matches Llama's quality at smaller sizes but may underperform on tasks outside its training distribution.

faq.q: Does Phi work for my specific domain?
faq.a: Phi is trained on general reasoning tasks and English chat. Fine-tune on your domain data if performance lags; distillation from a larger domain model often works better than starting from Phi.

faq.q: Why is Phi so small but still capable?
faq.a: High-quality training data (textbook examples, synthetic reasoning, filtering noise) teaches models to generalize better. Phi proves that data quality can offset much of the loss from reducing parameters, though it still has limits at very small sizes.

title: Qwen (Model Family)
tldr: Alibaba's open-weights LLM family spanning 1B–70B+ parameters, particularly strong for multilingual and Chinese-language tasks.
plain_english: Qwen is a family of language models released by Alibaba that you can download and run yourself. Unlike closed models, you get the full weights and can fine-tune them for your own use case. The family includes different sizes—from tiny models under 1 billion parameters to massive ones over 70 billion—so you pick one that fits your hardware and latency budget.
how_it_works: Alibaba releases multiple model variants: dense transformers (one forward pass per token) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) variants that route tokens to different sub-networks for efficiency. Each generation—Qwen, Qwen2, Qwen2.5, Qwen3—improves on benchmarks for math, code, and agentic reasoning. Models come with vision-language versions (processing images), audio support, and domain-specific math models. You download weights from Hugging Face, load them into a framework like vLLM or Ollama, and run inference locally or on your infrastructure.
why_it_matters: Qwen competes with Llama and Mistral as a top-tier open-weights option. For teams deploying multilingual systems—especially those serving Chinese, Japanese, or other Asian languages—Qwen often outperforms English-first models at comparable parameter counts. You avoid vendor lock-in and licensing restrictions.
example: A Chinese e-commerce company building a customer-support chatbot uses Qwen2.5 7B instead of GPT-4o because it handles Chinese nuance better, costs less per inference token when self-hosted, and the permissive license lets them fine-tune on internal conversations without legal friction. They deploy it via vLLM on their own GPUs.
pitfalls: Assuming all Qwen sizes are equally multilingual—smaller models (1–7B) lose some non-English capability; you need 14B+ for robust cross-language work.
Mixing up Qwen2.5 benchmarks with older Qwen versions; comparisons to GPT-4o apply mainly to 32B and 72B sizes, not 7B.
Running vision or audio variants without checking VRAM requirements; Qwen-VL and audio models need significantly more memory than text-only.
Not updating chat templates when upgrading Qwen generations; template changes between versions can degrade output quality if you use an old format.
when_use: Use Qwen when you need a permissive open-weights model, serve non-English or multilingual users, want to fine-tune on proprietary data, or need specialized variants (code, math, vision).
when_avoid: Avoid Qwen if you need state-of-the-art English-only reasoning (Qwen trails Llama 3.1 70B and GPT-4o in pure reasoning), require official commercial support with SLAs, or lack infrastructure to host and monitor models.

faq.q: What is the difference between Qwen2 and Qwen2.5?
faq.a: Qwen2.5 (late 2024) improves math, code, and agentic performance over Qwen2, and matches GPT-4o mini on many benchmarks at smaller model sizes. Use Qwen2.5 for new deployments.

faq.q: Can I use Qwen commercially without paying Alibaba?
faq.a: Yes, Qwen weights use permissive licenses (primarily Apache 2.0 or similar). You can use them in commercial products without royalties, though you must respect the license terms.

faq.q: Is Qwen better than Llama for Chinese?
faq.a: Qwen generally outperforms Llama on Chinese-language benchmarks, especially at smaller model sizes (7–14B). For English-dominant use cases, Llama is often competitive or better.

faq.q: What hardware do I need to run Qwen?
faq.a: Qwen 7B fits on a single A100 or H100 with 16–40GB VRAM depending on precision. Smaller 1–2B models run on consumer GPUs or CPUs. Use quantization (GGUF, AWQ) to reduce memory footprint.

faq.q: Does Qwen support function calling and tool use?
faq.a: Yes, Qwen2.5 and newer versions support structured tool calls via chat templates. Integration with agents like AutoGen or CrewAI is well-tested.

title: Qwen Coder
tldr: Alibaba's code-focused Qwen variant for repo-level tasks, multilingual programming, and agentic coding workflows.
plain_english: Qwen Coder is a version of Alibaba's Qwen language model trained specifically to write, understand, and debug code. Think of it like a general translator who then spent years specializing in technical documentation—it keeps broad knowledge but excels at programming. It comes in multiple sizes (0.5B to 32B parameters) and ranks near the top of open-source code benchmarks.
how_it_works: Qwen Coder starts with the base Qwen model, then applies code-specific post-training on repositories, code documentation, and reasoning-focused programming tasks. During inference, you give it a code snippet, natural-language instruction, or multi-file context; it returns completions, bug fixes, or explanations. It handles Chinese-language code comments and documentation better than most English-first models. All variants ship under Apache-2.0 license, allowing commercial use.
why_it_matters: Open-source code models reduce dependency on proprietary APIs (like GitHub Copilot) and let teams run inference locally or on private infrastructure. Qwen Coder competes directly with DeepSeek-Coder and rivals proprietary models on many benchmarks, making it practical for teams building code assistants, IDE plugins, or internal development tools.
example: A startup building an internal code-review bot runs Qwen2.5-Coder-32B on a single A100 GPU. Engineers paste a pull request; the model flags logic bugs and suggests refactoring in under 2 seconds. Because it's open-weights and Apache-2.0 licensed, the startup doesn't pay per inference or expose code to third parties.
pitfalls: Smaller variants (0.5B–1.8B) underperform on complex multi-file refactoring; use 7B+ for production agentic code tasks.
Training was code-heavy but not adversarial; it can still generate insecure patterns (SQL injection, buffer overflows) without guardrails.
Context window is fixed per variant; repo-level tasks may need chunking or retrieval augmentation for large codebases.
Multilingual advantage applies mostly to Chinese; English-centric code is handled equally well by CodeLlama or DeepSeek-Coder.
when_use: Use Qwen Coder when you need open-weights code generation, can run inference on-device or on-prem, work with mixed Chinese/English code, or want to avoid per-token API costs.
when_avoid: Avoid if you need real-time IDE autocomplete at sub-100ms latency, require CUDA-free deployment, or prioritize non-English programming languages outside of Chinese.

faq.q: How does Qwen Coder differ from the base Qwen model?
faq.a: Base Qwen is general-purpose; Qwen Coder applies additional training on code repositories and programming tasks, making it far better at code generation, debugging, and repo-level reasoning. General instruction-following is retained.

faq.q: Can I run Qwen Coder on my local machine?
faq.a: Yes. Variants up to 7B parameters fit on consumer GPUs (8GB VRAM); 32B requires 80GB+ (A100/H100). Use GGUF quantization via Ollama or llama.cpp to reduce memory footprint by 50–75%.

faq.q: Is Qwen Coder better than CodeLlama?
faq.a: On many recent code benchmarks (BigCodeBench, HumanEval), Qwen2.5-Coder 7B/32B outperforms CodeLlama 7B/34B. CodeLlama may be more stable in older production setups, but Qwen Coder is the safer choice for new projects.

faq.q: Can I use Qwen Coder for code generation in production?
faq.a: Yes, all variants are Apache-2.0 licensed. You can self-host, fine-tune, or embed in products without paying per token or sharing code with third parties.

faq.q: Does Qwen Coder understand Chinese code comments?
faq.a: Yes, it was trained on bilingual code corpora and handles Chinese variable names, docstrings, and comments significantly better than English-only models.

title: Reasoning Model (Category)
tldr: LLM trained to produce extended internal reasoning chains before answering, excelling at math and coding.
plain_english: A reasoning model is an LLM that thinks aloud before answering—like showing your work on a math problem instead of just writing the final answer. It's trained differently than standard chat models, using reinforcement learning on tasks with verifiable correct answers. The tradeoff: it costs more and takes longer, but solves harder analytical problems.
how_it_works: During training, reasoning models learn to generate long chains of thought on problems with clear right and wrong answers (mathematics, code verification, logic puzzles). Reinforcement learning rewards correct reasoning steps, not just correct final answers. At inference time, the model spends compute generating internal scratchpad text before producing its answer. This "test-time compute" means each query takes longer and consumes more tokens, but performance on difficult tasks improves significantly compared to single-shot attempts.
why_it_matters: Hard analytical work—formal verification, competition math, security audits—often fails on standard models because they skip reasoning steps. Reasoning models change the cost/quality tradeoff for these tasks. Knowing when to route to a reasoning model versus a faster general-purpose model is critical for product latency and budget management.
example: A researcher verifying a mathematical proof would use o1 or DeepSeek-R1 instead of Claude or GPT-4. The reasoning model takes 30 seconds per proof and catches logical gaps a standard model would miss in 1 second. For customer service chatbots, you'd avoid reasoning models entirely because the overhead isn't worth the accuracy gain on simple questions.
pitfalls: Reasoning models are slower and cost 5–10× more per token—using them on simple queries wastes budget.
Internal reasoning is opaque; you see only the final answer unless the model outputs its scratchpad.
Some models' general conversation quality suffers because training optimizes for analytical tasks, not chitchat.
Latency can exceed 30 seconds for complex problems; unsuitable for real-time applications.
when_use: Use reasoning models for math, formal verification, security analysis, or scientific problem-solving where correctness matters more than speed.
when_avoid: Avoid on customer service, summarization, or interactive chatbots where latency is critical and analytical correctness is secondary.

faq.q: What's the difference between a reasoning model and a regular LLM?
faq.a: Reasoning models generate long internal thought chains before answering; regular LLMs produce answers directly. Reasoning models are trained with reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks, making them stronger at analytical work but slower and more expensive.

faq.q: Can I see the reasoning a reasoning model produces?
faq.a: It depends on the model. Some expose the reasoning tokens (OpenAI o1 can output thinking in API mode), others hide it. Exposing reasoning makes it slower but more interpretable.

faq.q: How much slower are reasoning models?
faq.a: Expect 5–30 second latencies for complex problems versus 1–2 seconds for standard models. Cost per query is also 5–10× higher due to longer token sequences.

faq.q: Do reasoning models work for non-analytical tasks like writing or customer service?
faq.a: They work, but inefficiently. Since training optimizes for analytical correctness, they often perform worse than general-purpose models on creative or conversational tasks while costing more.

faq.q: Which reasoning models exist today?
faq.a: OpenAI o1/o3/o4, Anthropic's extended-thinking mode, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ, and Gemini 2 Thinking are current examples. Availability and capabilities evolve rapidly.

title: RoBERTa
tldr: BERT retrained on more data, larger batches, longer steps, and without next-sentence prediction—consistently outperforms BERT on text classification.
plain_english: RoBERTa is BERT with a better training recipe. Instead of inventing a new architecture, researchers (Liu et al., 2019) kept BERT's structure but changed how they trained it: more data, bigger batches, longer training time, and removing a task that didn't help. It's like using the same oven but baking longer at a better temperature.
how_it_works: RoBERTa uses BERT's transformer encoder unchanged. The key differences are in pretraining: it removes the next-sentence-prediction (NSP) objective that BERT used, trains for more steps on larger batches (256K sequences vs. BERT's 250K), and uses a larger training corpus (160GB of text vs. BERT's 16GB). The model learns bidirectional context through masked language modeling alone. After pretraining, you fine-tune it on downstream tasks like text classification, NER, or question-answering by adding task-specific layers.
why_it_matters: For production NLP systems, RoBERTa gives you better baseline performance than BERT without retraining from scratch. On GLUE benchmark tasks, RoBERTa scores 88.9% versus BERT's 81.5%. This matters when your budget or timeline forces you to pick a pretrained model rather than train your own—RoBERTa usually requires less downstream fine-tuning data to reach target accuracy.
example: A customer support team building a ticket classifier starts with RoBERTa instead of BERT. After fine-tuning on just 2,000 labeled tickets, RoBERTa reaches 94% accuracy on routing (billing vs. technical vs. product). The same setup with BERT requires 3,500 labeled tickets to match that performance, saving the team weeks of annotation work.
pitfalls: Assuming longer training always helps—RoBERTa's gains come from the combination of more data, bigger batches, and longer steps, not any single factor.
Using RoBERTa for languages it wasn't trained on; the English version won't transfer well to languages in its pretraining corpus without continued pretraining.
Fine-tuning too aggressively on small datasets (under 500 examples) can cause RoBERTa to overfit faster than BERT due to its stronger learned representations.
Not removing the NSP token or padding; RoBERTa doesn't use NSP, so including it in prompts wastes context window.
when_use: Use RoBERTa when you need a strong pretrained encoder for classification, NER, or semantic similarity tasks and have limited fine-tuning data (under 10K labeled examples).
when_avoid: Avoid RoBERTa when you need generation (use decoder models like GPT or T5), working with non-English text without multilingual variants, or when inference latency is critical and model size matters (RoBERTa is 355M parameters, same as BERT-large).

faq.q: Is RoBERTa the same as BERT?
faq.a: No. RoBERTa uses BERT's architecture but improves the pretraining recipe by adding more data, larger batches, longer training, and removing the NSP task. The architecture is identical; the training method is different.

faq.q: Should I always use RoBERTa instead of BERT?
faq.a: Usually yes for classification tasks—RoBERTa scores consistently higher on GLUE. But BERT is slightly smaller and trains faster if you have compute constraints. The choice depends on your accuracy target and training budget.

faq.q: Can I use RoBERTa for question-answering or text generation?
faq.a: RoBERTa works well for reading-comprehension question-answering (like SQuAD) because it's an encoder. It cannot generate text; use encoder-decoder models like T5 or decoder-only models like GPT for generation.

faq.q: How long does it take to fine-tune RoBERTa?
faq.a: On a single GPU, fine-tuning RoBERTa on a typical classification task (2–3 epochs) takes 10–30 minutes depending on dataset size. With distributed training, it's faster; with very large datasets, several hours.

faq.q: Does RoBERTa work for non-English languages?
faq.a: The base RoBERTa is English-only. Use XLM-RoBERTa if you need multilingual support; it covers 100+ languages and was trained on comparable data.

title: RWKV
tldr: RNN-transformer hybrid that trains efficiently in parallel but runs inference in linear time with constant memory.
plain_english: RWKV reformulates the attention mechanism as a linear recurrence—think of it as a rolling computation that tracks state instead of comparing every token to every other token. At training time, you unroll this recurrence in parallel like a transformer for speed. At inference, you compute it step-by-step like an RNN, using constant memory per new token instead of growing a cache.
how_it_works: RWKV replaces standard multi-head attention with a gated linear attention mechanism parameterized by time-decay weights. During training, the model unfolds this recurrence across the full sequence and backpropagates in parallel—matching transformer throughput. During inference, you maintain a fixed-size state vector and update it recurrently as each token arrives, avoiding the quadratic KV cache growth of standard transformers. The recurrence is gated so the model learns what information to forget and what to retain over long sequences.
why_it_matters: RWKV cuts memory cost per token from O(n) to O(1) during generation, enabling long-context and edge deployment where transformer KV cache is prohibitive. It trains at competitive speed with transformers while keeping inference costs predictable, making it viable for resource-constrained or latency-critical systems.
example: A team building a long-context document Q&A system for embedded devices might deploy a RWKV-7B model. Standard transformer inference on a 128K context would require gigabytes of GPU memory for the KV cache; RWKV needs only the fixed hidden-state size regardless of context length. This trades off some attention expressiveness for linear scaling, but benchmarks show quality within 2–5% of similarly-sized transformers on standard tasks.
pitfalls: RWKV trades some expressiveness for linear scaling—it cannot attend to arbitrary tokens in the past with the same precision as full attention, which may hurt tasks requiring tight long-range dependency tracking.
Community-driven development means fewer industrial-scale optimizations and fewer ready-made fine-tuning recipes compared to LLaMA or Mistral ecosystems.
Training code and inference tooling (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM) have less mature support, requiring more DIY engineering.
Early versions (RWKV-3, RWKV-4) lagged transformer baselines; later versions (RWKV-6, RWKV-7) are more competitive but still less widely evaluated than mainstream models.
when_use: Use RWKV when you need predictable inference memory, long context windows on limited hardware, or streaming generation with constant latency per token.
when_avoid: Avoid RWKV if you require state-of-the-art accuracy on complex reasoning benchmarks, have deep integrations with closed-source infrastructure (OpenAI, Anthropic APIs), or lack expertise to debug unfamiliar model architectures.

faq.q: How is RWKV different from Mamba?
faq.a: Both are state-space alternatives to transformers, but RWKV uses gated linear attention while Mamba uses selective state-space models with scan operations. RWKV trains in parallel like a transformer; Mamba does not parallelize training as easily. They compete on similar use cases but have different architectural philosophies.

faq.q: Can RWKV do in-context learning like transformers?
faq.a: Yes, RWKV demonstrates in-context learning on few-shot tasks, though typically 2–5% weaker than comparably-sized transformers. The linear attention mechanism limits how sharply it can select context, but the gating mechanism allows learning task-relevant state updates.

faq.q: Does RWKV really have O(1) memory during inference?
faq.a: Yes for the KV cache—the hidden state is constant-size. Total memory also includes model weights and embeddings, which scale with parameter count. But per-token memory cost is constant, unlike transformers where KV cache grows linearly with context.

faq.q: Is RWKV production-ready?
faq.a: RWKV-7 models are stable and open-weight, but production tooling is younger. Deployment is viable for teams comfortable with less battle-tested inference stacks and willing to use community-maintained frameworks like ollama or custom TensorFlow/PyTorch code.

faq.q: Can I fine-tune RWKV models?
faq.a: Yes—RWKV supports LoRA, full fine-tuning, and instruction-tuning via projects like Axolotl. Fine-tuning recipes are less documented than for LLaMA, so you may need to adapt transformer workflows.

title: SAM (Segment Anything Model)
tldr: Meta's foundation model that generates precise object masks from point, box, or mask prompts—zero-shot.
plain_english: SAM turns image segmentation into a point-and-click task. You give it a hint (a point on an object, a bounding box, or a rough outline), and it automatically draws the mask around that object—without needing training data for your specific objects. Think of it as a smart scissors tool that understands what you're trying to select.
how_it_works: SAM was trained on 1.1B masks across 11M images using a simple prompt encoder and a fast mask decoder. You provide a visual prompt—a point coordinate, a box, a free-form mask, or text—and the model outputs a segmentation mask for that region. SAM 2 (July 2024) added temporal memory, allowing it to track objects across video frames. The model uses a vision transformer backbone to process the image and a lightweight decoder to generate masks in near-real-time.
why_it_matters: Segmentation was previously a supervised task requiring annotated data for each new domain. SAM made it zero-shot and practical, becoming the foundation layer for image-editing tools, medical imaging pipelines, and robotic perception systems. It eliminated the need to train or fine-tune segmentation models for new use cases.
example: A medical imaging team uses SAM to annotate tumor boundaries in CT scans. Instead of manually drawing masks, radiologists click once on a tumor, and SAM generates a precise mask in milliseconds. The team then refines the mask with additional clicks if needed. This cuts annotation time from minutes to seconds per image, speeding up dataset creation for downstream AI models.
pitfalls: SAM produces only one mask per prompt and may struggle with partially occluded or very small objects—expect to iterate with multiple prompts.
The model works best with clear, visually distinct objects; cluttered scenes or ambiguous boundaries often require refinement.
SAM 2's temporal tracking can drift on fast motion or extreme lighting changes—always validate output on real video data.
Relying on auto-generated masks without human review in safety-critical domains (medical, autonomous systems) introduces silent errors.
when_use: Use SAM when you need fast, zero-shot segmentation for new object classes, when annotation budgets are tight, or when building interactive editing tools.
when_avoid: Avoid SAM when you need pixel-perfect accuracy in safety-critical applications without human review, or when working with highly specialized domains where domain-specific fine-tuning is cheaper than labeling iterations.

faq.q: What's the difference between SAM and SAM 2?
faq.a: SAM (April 2023) works on static images. SAM 2 (July 2024) adds temporal memory to track objects across video frames without re-prompting every frame, making it 10–100× faster on video sequences.

faq.q: Can SAM work without any prompt?
faq.a: No. SAM is a prompt-based model—it requires at least one visual cue (point, box, mask, or text) to generate a segmentation. It doesn't segment objects automatically without user guidance.

faq.q: Is SAM faster than manual segmentation?
faq.a: Yes. One prompt typically produces a mask in <100ms. For annotation tasks, SAM reduces time from minutes per image to seconds, though you'll often refine masks with follow-up clicks.

faq.q: Does SAM require fine-tuning for new domains?
faq.a: No. SAM generalizes zero-shot across domains—medical, aerial, industrial imagery all work out-of-the-box. Fine-tuning can improve results on very specialized domains but isn't required.

faq.q: Can I use SAM for real-time applications?
faq.a: Yes, but plan around latency. CPU inference is 1–2 seconds; GPU is <100ms. SAM 2 adds memory overhead on video, so benchmark on your hardware.

title: SigLIP
tldr: Google's vision encoder that replaces CLIP's softmax loss with sigmoid, enabling better zero-shot performance at smaller batch sizes.
plain_english: SigLIP swaps how CLIP learns to match images and text. Instead of comparing each image-text pair against all others in a batch (which requires huge batches to work well), SigLIP compares each pair independently. This makes it work better with smaller batches and produces stronger embeddings for identifying what's in an image without retraining.
how_it_works: SigLIP applies a sigmoid activation function to each image-text similarity score independently, rather than CLIP's softmax across the entire batch. During training, the loss treats each pair as its own classification problem: does this image match this text? Yes or no. This decouples performance from batch size. At inference, you encode an image and text separately into embeddings, then compute cosine similarity to rank matches or perform zero-shot classification. The resulting embeddings transfer better to downstream tasks because they aren't optimized for a specific batch distribution.
why_it_matters: SigLIP replaced CLIP as the default vision tower in production VLMs like Gemma and LLaVA-Next because it trains faster, scales to smaller batches, and produces higher-quality embeddings. Teams using smaller GPUs or preferring shorter training runs can now achieve competitive vision-language performance without the hardware constraints CLIP imposed.
example: A computer vision team fine-tuning a VLM on 4 A100s would struggle with CLIP because maintaining effective contrastive learning required batch sizes of 32,768+. With SigLIP, they use batch size 1,024 and reach the same downstream accuracy on image captioning and visual Q&A tasks, cutting training time by 40% and enabling iteration on their data and prompts.
pitfalls: SigLIP embeddings are not directly interchangeable with CLIP embeddings; models trained to consume CLIP outputs may not perform well with SigLIP without retraining.
Smaller batch sizes mean less gradient noise, which can lead to overfitting on small datasets; you still need adequate data diversity even if batch-size requirements drop.

The sigmoid formulation makes per-pair weighting harder; if you need to down-weight noisy pairs (as in contrastive learning with hard negatives), CLIP's softmax formulation may be clearer to instrument.
SigLIP's independence between pairs removes the global ranking signal that softmax provides; convergence is different and may require adjusted learning rates.
when_use: Use SigLIP when training vision-language models on GPUs with limited memory, when you need faster convergence, or when building on modern VLM baselines like LLaVA-Next or Gemma that already integrate it.
when_avoid: Avoid SigLIP if you have an existing pipeline tightly coupled to CLIP embeddings, or if you need the global-ranking properties of softmax contrastive loss for specific hard-negative mining strategies.

faq.q: What is the difference between SigLIP and CLIP?
faq.a: SigLIP replaces CLIP's softmax loss (which compares a pair against all negatives in the batch) with sigmoid loss (which treats each pair independently). This removes the dependency on very large batch sizes and improves embedding quality.

faq.q: Can I use SigLIP as a drop-in replacement for CLIP?
faq.a: Not directly. The embeddings are not identical, and models trained on CLIP embeddings will need retraining or fine-tuning to work well with SigLIP. New projects should use SigLIP; existing CLIP pipelines benefit from gradual migration.

faq.q: Does SigLIP work with small batch sizes?
faq.a: Yes. SigLIP's sigmoid formulation makes it effective at batch sizes of 1,024 or smaller, whereas CLIP requires 32,768+ to be stable. This is a key advantage for resource-constrained training.

faq.q: Which vision-language models use SigLIP?
faq.a: LLaVA-Next, Gemma 2-Vision, and Qwen VL series use SigLIP as their vision encoder. It has become the standard in modern open-source VLMs.

faq.q: Do I need to fine-tune SigLIP for my task?
faq.a: No; SigLIP zero-shot performance is strong enough for many tasks. Fine-tune only if you have domain-specific images or text that differ significantly from training data.

title: Sora (Video Generation)
tldr: OpenAI's text-to-video diffusion model that generates multi-second HD video clips from text prompts.
plain_english: Sora takes a text description and turns it into a video. Think of it like describing a scene to an artist, except the artist works pixel-by-pixel across time to create motion and consistency. It handles different aspect ratios and lengths, so you can ask for a square TikTok clip or a widescreen shot without retraining.
how_it_works: Sora breaks video into spacetime patches—small chunks across space and time—and represents them in a compressed latent space. During generation, it starts with noise and iteratively refines it using a diffusion transformer, predicting which patches come next based on your text prompt. It was trained on a mix of videos and images at varying resolutions and durations, which lets it generalize to new aspect ratios and lengths at inference time. The model outputs video at HD resolution with consistent subjects and camera movement across multiple seconds.
why_it_matters: Sora established the quality bar for AI video generation. Competitors now benchmark against it, pushing the entire field forward. For creators and studios, it opens automated video synthesis at scale; for enterprises, it reduces production timelines and costs for promotional or training content.
example: A product marketing team writes: 'A sleek phone rotating on a white surface, product reflected clearly in the background.' Sora generates a 10-second 1080p clip with smooth rotation, consistent lighting, and no flickering. They use it as a rough draft for their e-commerce site, then refine with human editors—saving weeks of 3D rendering or live shooting.
pitfalls: Sora still struggles with fine-grained physics (water flowing, cloth folding) and long temporal consistency beyond ~60 seconds—subjects can drift or vanish.
Text prompts require specificity: vague descriptions like 'a scene' generate incoherent motion; you must describe camera movement, lighting, and object behavior explicitly.
Generation is slow (minutes per clip) and access is closed—you cannot run it locally or fine-tune it, limiting reproducibility and customization.
Generated video can hallucinate details not mentioned in the prompt, especially in background regions, requiring post-production cleanup.
when_use: Use Sora when you need quick visual exploration of ideas, rapid prototyping of marketing assets, or automated background/B-roll generation at scale without human filming.
when_avoid: Avoid Sora for mission-critical content requiring photorealism, precise physical simulation, or full creative control; use it instead for draft-stage or supplementary clips that humans will edit.

faq.q: How is Sora different from Runway Gen-3 or Kling?
faq.a: Sora generates longer clips (up to ~60 seconds) with higher consistency, but Runway and Kling are faster and open-source-adjacent, offering more user control. Sora's main edge is coherence and quality; competitors trade speed and accessibility.

faq.q: Can you fine-tune Sora on your own videos?
faq.a: No. Sora is closed-access via OpenAI's API only. You cannot download weights, run it locally, or customize it with your own training data.

faq.q: How long does it take to generate a video?
faq.a: Generation typically takes 1–10 minutes for a 5–20 second clip, depending on resolution and batch size. It is not real-time.

faq.q: What aspect ratios and resolutions does Sora support?
faq.a: Sora handles variable aspect ratios (square, widescreen, vertical) and resolutions up to 1920×1080, trained on mixed-resolution data so it adapts without retraining.

faq.q: Does Sora work for animation or only photorealistic video?
faq.a: Sora can generate both stylized and photorealistic outputs depending on your prompt. It is not limited to realism but struggles with extreme style consistency and hand-crafted animation aesthetics.

title: Stable Diffusion
tldr: Open-weight text-to-image model that generates images in a compressed latent space, dramatically cheaper than pixel-space diffusion.
plain_english: Stable Diffusion is an image generator you can run on your own machine. Instead of processing pixels directly (like older methods), it works in a compressed space inside a neural network—imagine editing a blurry thumbnail instead of a full photograph. You type a description, and the model gradually removes noise from random patterns until an image appears.
how_it_works: A VAE encoder compresses your target image into a latent representation. A diffusion UNet learns to denoise this latent space by predicting noise at each step, guided by text embeddings from a CLIP encoder. At inference, you start with random noise and iteratively denoise for 20–50 steps. SDXL added a two-stage refiner (base model + refinement); SD3 switched to a transformer-based (DiT) backbone with rectified flow for faster convergence. The permissive license enabled local tools (Automatic1111, ComfyUI), LoRA fine-tuning, ControlNet spatial control, and countless community extensions.
why_it_matters: Stable Diffusion democratized image generation. Unlike closed-API competitors (DALL·E, Midjourney), you control training data, inference hardware, and output licensing. It powers most open-source image workflows and research prototypes, letting teams avoid per-image costs and API rate limits.
example: A game studio uses Stable Diffusion + ControlNet locally to generate 500 concept-art variations in a weekend, iterating on character designs without cloud bills or API quotas. They fine-tune on their own art style using LoRA (8 GB GPU), then batch-process in ComfyUI at <0.1 USD per image versus $0.02–0.04 per image on commercial APIs.
pitfalls: Latent-space distortion: VAE artifacts (blurriness, color shifts) compound over many denoising steps; test on your target resolution first.
CLIP text-encoder limits: uncommon terms or non-English prompts degrade quality; longer prompts don't always improve results.
Training instability with fine-tuning: LoRA collapse and forgetting happen if learning rates exceed 0.0001; overfitting to small datasets requires regularization.
License ambiguity: Stability AI's license permits commercial use, but training-data disputes (Getty, artists) create legal uncertainty in some jurisdictions.
when_use: Use Stable Diffusion when you need local inference control, low per-image cost, fine-tuning on custom data, or spatial constraints (ControlNet, inpainting).
when_avoid: Avoid it if you require photorealistic human faces at scale, strict brand consistency without extensive training, or real-time interactive generation on consumer GPUs.

faq.q: What is the difference between Stable Diffusion and DALL·E?
faq.a: Stable Diffusion is open-weight and runs locally; DALL·E is closed-source and API-only. SD lets you fine-tune, control inference, and avoid per-image fees; DALL·E offers higher-quality faces and brand consistency out-of-box.

faq.q: Can I use Stable Diffusion commercially?
faq.a: Yes, Stability AI's license permits commercial use of generated images and model weights. However, training-data copyright disputes (Getty, artist collectives) create legal gray areas in some jurisdictions; review your local laws.

faq.q: What GPU do I need to run Stable Diffusion locally?
faq.a: SDXL runs on 8 GB VRAM (RTX 4070, RTX 3070) with quantization; SD 1.5 fits on 4 GB. Inference takes 5–30 seconds per image depending on steps and hardware; CPU-only is feasible but slow (minutes per image).

faq.q: How do I fine-tune Stable Diffusion on my own images?
faq.a: Use LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with tools like Axolotl or Kohya SS. Train on 5–50 representative images at learning rate 0.0001 for 1000–5000 steps; this costs <1 hour on a 24 GB GPU and produces reusable style adapters.

faq.q: Does Stable Diffusion work for text, logos, and hands?
faq.a: Not reliably. SD struggles with legible text, precise logos, and anatomically correct hands. ControlNet (canny, pose) improves structure; specialized models (InstructPix2Pix, text-to-image fine-tunes) help, but expect post-processing or inpainting.

title: StarCoder (Code Model)
tldr: Open-source code LLM trained on permissively licensed GitHub repos; supports fill-in-the-middle completion.
plain_english: StarCoder is a code-focused language model built by BigCode on Apache-2.0 licensed code. Think of it as GitHub's publicly available code distilled into a model you can run locally. Unlike proprietary code assistants, you own the weights and can deploy it on your own hardware.
how_it_works: StarCoder (2023) and StarCoder 2 (2024) are trained on The Stack—a corpus of permissively licensed GitHub repositories filtered for code quality. Models range from 3B to 15B parameters. They support fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mode, where you provide code context before and after a gap, and the model predicts what goes between. Inference runs on consumer GPUs; no cloud dependency required.
why_it_matters: StarCoder is the most permissively licensed serious code model available. Teams building open-source code assistants avoid licensing friction that proprietary models impose. Its local-first design enables on-device completion without telemetry or API calls.
example: A developer team building an open IDE extension uses StarCoder 2 to power code completion. They quantize it to 4-bit precision on a consumer GPU (RTX 4090), achieving ~50 tokens/second latency. The permissive license means they ship it freely without vendor lock-in or commercial restrictions.
pitfalls: StarCoder excels at Python, JavaScript, and C++ but degrades on niche languages—check language-specific benchmarks before deployment.
Fill-in-the-middle quality depends on context window size; sparse or distant context reduces accuracy.
Smaller variants (3B–7B) trade speed for lower code understanding; larger models need more VRAM.
StarCoder 2's multilingual improvements are narrower than Codex on non-English code—test on your specific language first.
when_use: Use StarCoder when you need open-source, locally deployed code completion, avoid vendor lock-in, or operate under strict data privacy requirements.
when_avoid: Avoid StarCoder if you need state-of-the-art reasoning on complex refactoring tasks or proprietary language support; consider proprietary models or fine-tuned variants instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between StarCoder and CodeLlama?
faq.a: StarCoder is trained on GitHub code under permissive licenses; CodeLlama is Meta's proprietary variant trained on code and instruction-tuned. StarCoder is smaller (3B–15B) but fully open; CodeLlama focuses on instruction-following. Choose StarCoder for license clarity and local control.

faq.q: Can I run StarCoder on a CPU?
faq.a: Yes, but it's slow. Quantized variants (4-bit or 8-bit) run on CPU, but GPU is 10–50x faster. For production, target NVIDIA/AMD GPUs or Apple Metal for reasonable latency.

faq.q: Does StarCoder work with any programming language?
faq.a: It works best on Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, C++, and Java. Performance degrades on esoteric or domain-specific languages. Test your language before committing to production.

faq.q: Is StarCoder better than GitHub Copilot?
faq.a: No—Copilot has better instruction-tuning and real-world context from GitHub telemetry. StarCoder trades capability for openness: you control the model, avoid vendor lock-in, and pay no API costs.

faq.q: What is The Stack and why does it matter?
faq.a: The Stack is a curated dataset of 358M permissively licensed GitHub files. It excludes GPL and other restrictive licenses, ensuring you can legally ship and modify StarCoder without legal overhead.

title: State Space Model
tldr: Sequence model using linear recurrence to process tokens in linear time, enabling efficient long-context inference.
plain_english: A state space model treats a sequence like a system that evolves over time. At each step, it updates an internal state based on the current input and uses that state to produce output. Unlike attention, which compares every token to every other token, SSMs only remember a fixed-size hidden state, making them much faster for long documents.
how_it_works: At each position t, the model computes: hidden state h_t = A·h_{t-1} + B·x_t (evolving the state), then output y_t = C·h_t + D·x_t (reading from state). The matrices A, B, C, D are learned during training. S4 (2021) discretized this continuous-time math for discrete tokens; Mamba (2024) made the state transition depend on input (selective), letting the model decide what to forget. Both train in O(n) time and infer in O(1) per token, versus attention's O(n²).
why_it_matters: SSMs offer a real alternative to attention for 100k+ token contexts without quadratic cost. As long-context becomes table-stakes in production, architectures that scale linearly gain practical and economic advantages—lower compute per query, smaller KV cache.
example: A research team at a hedge fund processes quarterly SEC filings (50k–200k tokens each) to extract risk signals. Using a Transformer, inference costs $0.50 per document due to attention overhead. Switching to a Mamba-based model cuts that to $0.08 by eliminating the quadratic KV cache, while maintaining comparable accuracy on financial entity extraction.
pitfalls: Selective SSMs (like Mamba) are newer and less battle-tested than Transformers; production libraries and fine-tuning recipes are still maturing.
SSMs learn different inductive biases than attention—they may underperform on tasks requiring fine-grained token interaction (e.g., precise pronoun resolution).
The learned recurrence is inherently sequential during training unless using specialized parallelization; standard distributed training is harder than with attention.
Hidden state size must be tuned; too small and you lose capacity, too large and you lose the speed advantage.
when_use: Use SSMs when you need linear-time inference on long sequences (>10k tokens), can tolerate a different architectural tradeoff, or need to minimize KV cache overhead in production.
when_avoid: Avoid SSMs if you require state-of-the-art performance on short-context benchmarks, need mature fine-tuning tooling, or rely heavily on attention's ability to do arbitrary token-to-token lookups.

faq.q: How is a state space model different from a Transformer?
faq.a: Transformers use attention (quadratic complexity) to directly compare all token pairs. SSMs use a fixed hidden state (linear complexity) that gets updated sequentially. Transformers are more flexible with token interactions; SSMs are much faster at inference on long sequences.

faq.q: What is the difference between S4 and Mamba?
faq.a: S4 discretizes continuous-time dynamics for all positions the same way. Mamba adds selectivity—each position's state transition learns whether to update or forget based on the current input. Mamba performs better on real tasks because it can adaptively compress information.

faq.q: Can I fine-tune an SSM the same way as a Transformer?
faq.a: Mostly yes, but training is less optimized. Standard data-parallel fine-tuning works. However, advanced techniques like FlashAttention have no SSM equivalent yet, and gradient checkpointing strategies differ due to the sequential nature of recurrence.

faq.q: Do state space models use less memory than Transformers?
faq.a: During inference, yes—no KV cache needed. During training, memory savings are modest because the model still stores activations for backprop through the recurrence. Main advantage is inference memory and latency on long sequences.

faq.q: Are state space models ready for production?
faq.a: Mamba-based models are production-viable for long-context tasks. Inference is fast and mature frameworks exist (vLLM, TGI). Training and fine-tuning tooling lag Transformers, and edge cases with short, task-specific benchmarks may still favor Transformers.

title: T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)
tldr: Google's encoder-decoder model that treats all NLP tasks as text-in, text-out generation.
plain_english: T5 unifies different NLP jobs—classification, translation, summarization—under one format: you feed in text and get text back. Instead of building separate models for each task, you train one model on a "fill-the-blank" objective (mask parts of text, predict what's missing). Think of it like a universal translator that learned to solve problems by rewriting text.
how_it_works: T5 uses an encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder reads your input text and builds a representation; the decoder generates output token by token. During pretraining, random text spans are masked with a special token, and the model learns to predict them—this teaches it to understand and generate language. At inference, you prompt it with task-specific prefixes (e.g., "classify: this is great") and it outputs the answer as text. Comes in sizes from 60M to 11B parameters. FLAN-T5 (2022) adds instruction-tuning on top, making it respond better to natural prompts.
why_it_matters: The text-to-text framing lets you solve many tasks with one model and one training loop. For small-scale and structured-output work, T5 often beats decoder-only LLMs in speed and accuracy. Its compact sizes run on modest hardware, making it practical for production when you don't need the scale of GPT-class models.
example: A customer-service team uses FLAN-T5-base (250M params) to classify support tickets and generate summaries. Input: "classify: The app keeps crashing on login." Output: "bug". For summarization: "summarize: A long ticket description" produces a 1-2 sentence summary. The model runs on a single GPU and costs 1/50th of calling a large commercial API, with response times under 200ms.
pitfalls: T5 struggles with very long contexts (2–4k tokens); use dense-retrieval or chunking to feed relevant text only.
Task-specific prefixes matter: "classify:" vs "Classify:" can shift outputs; document your prefix format in production.
At small scale, T5 may hallucinate in zero-shot; few-shot examples or fine-tuning improve reliability.
Encoder-decoder models add latency compared to decoder-only models because both encoder and decoder must run sequentially.
when_use: Use T5 when you have multiple NLP tasks to solve with one model, need fast inference on consumer hardware, want a small footprint, or can afford the encoder-decoder latency for structured output.
when_avoid: Avoid T5 if you need real-time, ultra-low-latency generation, require handling of 8k+ token contexts without chunking, or are building a general-purpose chat assistant (decoder-only LLMs are better here).

faq.q: What's the difference between T5 and BART?
faq.a: Both are encoder-decoder models, but T5's pretraining (span corruption) differs from BART's (noising + denoising). T5 tends to generalize better across tasks; BART excels at summarization and translation. T5 has more official variants (FLAN-T5).

faq.q: Can I use T5 for question answering?
faq.a: Yes. Prefix your input with "question: <q>; context: <text>" and T5 will generate the answer. However, fine-tuning on a QA dataset (SQuAD, etc.) improves accuracy over zero-shot.

faq.q: How does T5 compare to GPT-3 in cost and speed?
faq.a: T5-base runs on a single GPU and costs nearly nothing to host; GPT-3 requires paid API calls. T5 is 10–100× faster for classification and summarization but lacks the open-ended conversational fluency of large LLMs.

faq.q: Is FLAN-T5 better than base T5?
faq.a: FLAN-T5 is instruction-tuned and stronger at zero-shot tasks and following natural prompts. Base T5 may perform better if you have a large labeled dataset for your specific task and can fine-tune it.

faq.q: What's the training objective 'span corruption'?
faq.a: The model masks random spans of text (e.g., 2–4 tokens) with a special token and learns to predict what was masked. This teaches contextual understanding more efficiently than token-level masking used in BERT.

title: Transformer
tldr: Neural network architecture using self-attention to process sequences in parallel, enabling modern LLMs like GPT and Claude.
plain_english: A Transformer is a machine that reads all parts of a text at once and figures out which pieces matter most to each other. Instead of processing words one-by-one like an assembly line, it compares every word to every other word simultaneously. This made it possible to train the large language models you use today.
how_it_works: A Transformer takes tokens (pieces of text) and converts them into numerical vectors called embeddings. The self-attention mechanism computes a relevance score between every pair of tokens, determining how much each token should influence others. It processes all tokens in parallel across GPUs rather than sequentially, enabling training on billions of parameters. The output is passed through feed-forward layers, then the process repeats across multiple stacked layers. Each layer learns different linguistic patterns—early layers might learn grammar, later layers learn semantics.
why_it_matters: The Transformer enables the scale and speed that created the current AI era. Understanding its architecture helps explain why LLMs have fixed context windows, why longer inputs cost more compute, and why attention works better than older RNNs for language tasks. It's the foundation for every major model released since 2017.
example: When Claude processes a 100K token document, the Transformer's self-attention allows it to find relevant sections instantly rather than re-reading linearly. A researcher at Anthropic can load a research paper into Claude and ask questions about Figure 3 on page 15 without Claude losing track of earlier context—something an older RNN would struggle with due to sequential bottlenecks.
pitfalls: Self-attention costs scale quadratically with context length—a 2x longer document needs 4x more attention computation, making very long sequences memory-hungry.
Position information isn't built in; models rely on positional embeddings or RoPE encoding, which can degrade when extrapolating beyond training context lengths.
Transformers excel at pattern matching but don't reason causally; they correlate tokens without understanding causation, leading to hallucinations.
The architecture assumes fixed-size input tensors; variable-length sequences require padding or chunking, wasting compute.
when_use: Use Transformers for sequence-to-sequence tasks like language modeling, machine translation, question-answering, and any task where tokens interact long-range; they're the default for text, code, and multimodal models.
when_avoid: Avoid Transformers for tasks with extremely long sequences (>1M tokens) where computational cost outweighs accuracy gains, or streaming scenarios where latency is critical and you need constant-time inference per token.

faq.q: What is the difference between a Transformer and an RNN?
faq.a: RNNs process tokens sequentially, one at a time, making them slow on long sequences. Transformers process all tokens in parallel using self-attention, enabling massive GPU parallelization and training on larger datasets. This parallelism is why Transformers power modern LLMs.

faq.q: Why is self-attention called attention?
faq.a: Self-attention computes weights showing how much each token should 'pay attention to' every other token. These weights determine which context the model prioritizes when generating predictions, mimicking selective focus.

faq.q: Does a Transformer understand the order of words?
faq.a: Not inherently. Transformers use positional encodings (typically RoPE or absolute embeddings) to inject position information. Without them, a Transformer treats 'cat chased dog' the same as 'dog chased cat,' which is why positional embeddings are essential.

faq.q: Why do Transformers need more memory for longer contexts?
faq.a: Self-attention creates a matrix comparing every token to every other token. A 4K context creates a 4K × 4K matrix; a 32K context creates a 32K × 32K matrix. Memory grows quadratically, not linearly.

faq.q: Can Transformers handle real-time streaming?
faq.a: Yes, but with tradeoffs. Streaming Transformers must cache KV pairs and recompute attention at each new token, which is slower than batch inference. For true low-latency streaming, some practitioners use smaller models or speculative decoding.
