title: JSON Schema Enforcement
tldr: Provider-side guarantee that LLM output validates against a JSON schema at decoding time.
plain_english: Instead of hoping the model produces valid JSON and retrying when it fails, you give the provider a schema upfront and they enforce it during generation. The model can only output tokens that produce valid JSON matching your schema—keys, types, everything. Think of it like a guard at the exit checking every ticket before letting anyone through.
how_it_works: The LLM provider uses constrained decoding to mask invalid tokens at each generation step. When you call the API with `response_format: {type: json_schema, schema: {...}}` (OpenAI) or equivalent (Anthropic, others), the schema is parsed and only tokens that keep the output on a valid path are allowed. The model cannot deviate—if your schema requires `name` as a string and `age` as an integer, those constraints are enforced at the token level, not post-hoc. Output is guaranteed valid without retry loops.
why_it_matters: Eliminates the failure mode where you parse JSON and hit `KeyError` or type mismatches. Downstream systems can trust the structure immediately and skip validation code. This saves latency (no retries), reduces error handling overhead, and makes integration with type-safe systems straightforward.
example: A customer support team uses an LLM to extract ticket metadata. They define a schema requiring `{"severity": "low"|"medium"|"high", "category": string, "needs_escalation": boolean}`. With enforcement, every response is valid JSON matching those types—no null surprises, no string-instead-of-boolean. They skip writing retry logic and feed results directly into their ticketing system.
pitfalls: Schemas that are too restrictive can cause the model to refuse valid requests or produce repetitive output—test your enum values against real data.
Not all providers support the same schema subset; OpenAI's implementation differs from Anthropic's, so migration requires schema adjustments.
The model still hallucinates within the schema—enforcement ensures valid structure, not semantic correctness.
Complex nested schemas with many optional fields can cause token overhead during decoding, slightly increasing latency.
when_use: Use this when you need guaranteed structured output for downstream type-safe systems, extraction tasks with fixed fields, or workflows where retry loops are unacceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid when your output structure is highly variable, when the model needs creative freedom, or when you're targeting providers that don't support constrained decoding.

faq.q: Is JSON schema enforcement the same as function calling?
faq.a: No. Function calling lets you specify discrete functions the model can invoke; schema enforcement constrains the raw output format. Function calling is for tool selection; schema enforcement is for structure guarantee.

faq.q: Does schema enforcement slow down generation?
faq.a: Slightly. Constrained decoding adds a small overhead per token to check validity, but you save latency by eliminating retries. Simple schemas have minimal impact; very complex ones are more expensive.

faq.q: Can I use JSON schema enforcement with streaming?
faq.a: Yes, most providers support streaming with schemas. The constraint is applied token-by-token, so output is streamed incrementally while remaining valid.

faq.q: What happens if the schema is impossible to satisfy?
faq.a: The model will generate partial output matching the schema constraints, then stop. Some providers error; others truncate. Test edge cases before production.

meta_title: JSON Schema Enforcement — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Provider-side guarantee that LLM output validates against a JSON schema at decoding time.

schema_jsonld.name: JSON Schema Enforcement
schema_jsonld.description: Provider-side guarantee that LLM output validates against a JSON schema at decoding time.

title: LiteLLM
tldr: Open-source Python SDK that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind a single OpenAI-compatible API.
plain_english: LiteLLM is a translation layer for AI models. Instead of writing separate code for OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Mistral, you write once and swap providers by changing a string. It's like adapting a recipe to work in any kitchen—same steps, different appliances.
how_it_works: LiteLLM intercepts your OpenAI-style API call (model, messages, temperature, etc.) and converts it to the correct provider's format before sending it. When you call `completion()`, LiteLLM maps the request to Claude, GPT, or Mistral using the `model` parameter as a routing key. The response normalizes back to OpenAI format. LiteLLM Proxy mode runs as a server, adding cost tracking, key management, budget enforcement, and observability middleware between your app and providers.
why_it_matters: Provider lock-in costs time and risk. Switching models mid-project means rewriting integrations. LiteLLM lets you swap from GPT-4 to Claude to a local Ollama instance without touching your application code, making cost optimization and disaster recovery automatic decisions rather than rewrites.
example: A startup using GPT-4 for customer support hits rate limits and costs spike. Instead of refactoring, they change one config line to route 30% of traffic to Claude and 20% to Mistral via LiteLLM, cutting costs 40% while testing alternative models. When a provider goes down, they failover automatically.
pitfalls: Assuming all providers return identical behavior—latency, output quality, and reasoning depth vary; test each provider in staging.
Forgetting provider-specific limits—Claude has 200k context, GPT-4o has 128k; routing long documents to the wrong model fails silently.
Over-relying on Proxy mode without monitoring—cost tracking and budgets prevent surprises, but misconfigured rate limits block all traffic.
Mixing old and new OpenAI client versions—LiteLLM compatibility breaks if your codebase uses deprecated SDK patterns.
when_use: Use LiteLLM when you support multiple providers, need cost optimization across models, want to A/B test provider performance, or require fallback routing during outages.
when_avoid: Avoid LiteLLM if you're locked into a single proprietary API (Bloomberg terminal) or need sub-millisecond latency where translation overhead matters.

faq.q: Can I use LiteLLM with Ollama or local models?
faq.a: Yes. LiteLLM wraps Ollama, vLLM, and LM Studio the same way it wraps OpenAI—you point it to your local server URL and reference the model by name.

faq.q: Does LiteLLM add latency?
faq.a: Library mode adds 5–15ms overhead (request parsing and routing). Proxy mode adds 10–30ms. For most applications, this is negligible versus network round-trip time.

faq.q: Can LiteLLM track spending across providers?
faq.a: Yes. LiteLLM Proxy logs input/output tokens per provider and calculates costs in real time. You can set per-user or per-model budgets that block requests when limits are hit.

faq.q: What happens if a provider is down?
faq.a: In library mode, the call fails. In Proxy mode, you can configure fallback routing—if OpenAI fails, automatically retry with Anthropic.

faq.q: Is LiteLLM compatible with LangChain or LlamaIndex?
faq.a: Yes. Both frameworks support custom LLM providers that accept OpenAI-format calls, so LiteLLM works as a drop-in replacement.

meta_title: LiteLLM — Open-Source Multi-Provider LLM Gateway | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Open-source Python SDK that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind a single OpenAI-compatible API.

schema_jsonld.name: LiteLLM
schema_jsonld.description: Open-source Python SDK that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind a single OpenAI-compatible API.

title: LLM Gateway
tldr: Middleware that routes requests across LLM providers, handles retries, caches responses, and tracks costs centrally.
plain_english: An LLM gateway sits between your application and the actual LLM providers—think of it as a traffic controller and accountant combined. Instead of your app talking directly to OpenAI, Anthropic, or Mistral, it talks to the gateway. The gateway forwards requests intelligently, retries failed calls, caches duplicate questions, and logs everything for billing and compliance.
how_it_works: The gateway accepts API calls in a standard format (like OpenAI's chat-completions-api). It parses the request, optionally checks a semantic-caching layer to avoid redundant calls, then routes to the configured provider—or to a fallback if the primary is down. After the provider responds, the gateway logs the call (cost, tokens, latency), applies PII-redaction if configured, and returns the result. Retries happen automatically on rate-limits or transient errors, and you get observability across all models and teams from a single dashboard.
why_it_matters: Direct provider keys scattered across your infrastructure fragment cost tracking, resilience, and compliance. A gateway centralizes these concerns: one place to audit LLM spend, one place to enforce rate-limits, one place to rotate credentials. For teams using multiple providers or serving multiple internal customers, this prevents cost surprises and operational chaos.
example: A startup using Claude for customer support and GPT-4 for code review runs both through a gateway. When Anthropic has an outage, the gateway automatically falls back to a different Claude endpoint or Mistral. The finance team sees exact token costs per feature; the security team sees PII was never logged. Without the gateway, they'd have separate monitoring per provider and no unified fallback.
pitfalls: Adding a gateway introduces network latency; measure time-to-first-token before and after to ensure acceptable impact.
Semantic caching trades freshness for cost savings; use with caution for time-sensitive queries like live news summaries.
Gateway logs can become a compliance liability if not encrypted and retention-limited; treat cached responses as sensitive data.
Fallback chains mask provider issues; monitor which fallbacks are triggered frequently to catch real problems early.
when_use: Use a gateway when you're multi-homing LLMs, need cost visibility across teams, require automatic failover, or must enforce security policies like PII-redaction at scale.
when_avoid: Avoid a gateway for single-provider, latency-critical workloads where sub-10ms overhead is unacceptable, or for one-off prototypes where the operational overhead outweighs the benefit.

faq.q: What's the difference between an LLM gateway and a load balancer?
faq.a: A load balancer distributes traffic across identical backend servers by health and latency. An LLM gateway does that plus provider abstraction, semantic caching, cost accounting, and cross-provider fallback. A gateway is application-aware; a load balancer is agnostic.

faq.q: Does an LLM gateway add latency?
faq.a: Yes, typically 10–50ms for routing, caching checks, and logging. For latency-critical use cases, benchmark your specific gateway and payload size. Semantic caching can recover latency by avoiding downstream calls entirely.

faq.q: Can I use a gateway with local models?
faq.a: Yes. Gateways like LiteLLM and Portkey support local inference servers (Ollama, vLLM, llama-cpp-server) alongside cloud providers. This lets you fall back to local models if cloud APIs fail or become expensive.

faq.q: Will a gateway lock me into a specific provider?
faq.a: No—that's the point. A gateway abstracts away provider-specific APIs. You can swap Claude for Mixtral or GPT-4 by changing a config file, not rewriting application code.

faq.q: How much does adding a gateway cost?
faq.a: Gateways charge per-million tokens or flat monthly (typically $50–500). The ROI comes from semantic-cache hits reducing redundant calls, plus reduced emergency incident costs from automatic failover.

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
tldr: Open standard for AI assistants to connect to external tools and data sources via a single, reusable server interface.
plain_english: Instead of building custom integrations between each AI app and each external service, MCP lets you build one server that speaks a standard language. Any MCP-compatible client—Claude, Cursor, or your own app—can then talk to that server to access tools, databases, or APIs. Think of it like a universal adapter: your server is the plug, the protocol is the standard socket, and any MCP client can connect.
how_it_works: You create an MCP server that wraps access to a resource (GitHub, Notion, a database). The server exposes tools, resources, and prompts via a JSON-RPC interface over stdio or HTTP. An MCP client (like Claude) sends a request following the protocol spec, the server processes it, and returns structured JSON. The client then uses the result in its reasoning or passes it to the user. You define what the server does; the protocol handles how client and server talk.
why_it_matters: MCP is becoming the standard for AI tool integration, reducing duplication and letting teams focus on server logic rather than per-client integration code. If you build for multiple AI platforms or clients, adopting MCP early saves engineering time and makes your service work with future clients automatically.
example: A data team builds an MCP server wrapping their company's Postgres database with parameterized queries for sales, inventory, and customer data. They deploy it once. Claude, Cursor, and their internal Slack bot can all query it without custom code. When they add a new AI client later, it works immediately because it already speaks MCP.
pitfalls: Treating MCP as a replacement for function calling or tool use—it's a protocol layer on top, not an alternative way to invoke functions.
Building a server that exposes too many unguarded operations; always validate and scope permissions on the server side.
Assuming all MCP clients implement the spec identically; test your server against multiple clients to catch spec interpretation gaps.
Forgetting that MCP is stateless per request; maintain session or auth context in your server, not in the protocol messages.
when_use: Use MCP when you're building a service, tool, or resource that multiple AI applications or clients need to access, or when you want to share integrations across a team without rewriting them for each model.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP if you're building a single-use integration between one AI client and one backend; direct API calls or function calling will be simpler. Also avoid if your use case requires real-time bidirectional streaming MCP doesn't currently support.

faq.q: Is MCP the same as function calling?
faq.a: No. Function calling is how a model invokes a tool; MCP is a protocol for how a client (which includes models) discovers and communicates with external servers. MCP can transport function calls, but it's a layer above them.

faq.q: Can I use MCP with models other than Claude?
faq.a: Yes. MCP is model-agnostic. Any AI application or agent framework that implements the MCP client spec can use your MCP server. Adoption is growing across Cursor, Zed, and other tools.

faq.q: Do I need to host an MCP server?
faq.a: You can run it locally, on a server you control, or via a hosted service. The client connects via stdio or HTTP—deployment is up to you. Many teams run MCP servers locally or on internal infrastructure for data access.

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP and REST APIs?
faq.a: REST is a general web architecture; MCP is a specific protocol optimized for AI clients to discover tools, prompts, and resources in a standardized format. MCP is simpler for AI use cases because it bakes in context awareness.

faq.q: Does MCP handle authentication?
faq.a: MCP itself doesn't define auth; that's your server's responsibility. You control auth inside your server logic—use API keys, OAuth, or whatever suits your architecture.

title: MCP Client
tldr: An application that connects to MCP servers and exposes their tools, prompts, and resources to an LLM.
plain_english: An MCP client is a host application—like Claude Desktop or your custom agent—that discovers and connects to MCP servers, then translates what the LLM wants to do (call a tool, read a resource) into server requests. Think of it as a router: the LLM generates intent, the client figures out which server handles it and sends the request there.
how_it_works: The client reads a configuration file listing available MCP servers and their connection details. It establishes connections and queries each server for its capabilities (tools, resources, prompts). When the LLM decides to call a tool, the client routes that request to the correct server, waits for the result, and returns it to the LLM. The client also handles permission prompts, error recovery, and multiplexing requests across many simultaneous servers without blocking.
why_it_matters: Understanding the client/server boundary is essential for building agents that actually work. A client lets you compose heterogeneous services (your internal APIs, third-party data sources, file systems) into a single coherent tool ecosystem. Custom clients unlock bespoke workflows—e.g., a researcher client that auto-routes between academic databases, code clients, and domain-specific servers.
example: A data analyst uses Claude Desktop (the MCP client) configured with three servers: one connecting to PostgreSQL, one to a file browser on their local drive, and one to Slack. When the LLM needs to query the database, the client forwards the SQL to the PostgreSQL server; when it needs to read a CSV, the file server handles it. All three work in the same session without the LLM knowing which server is which.
pitfalls: Assuming the client is a transparent pass-through: clients must handle authentication, retry logic, and capability negotiation; misconfiguration breaks the whole chain.
Mixing server discovery with client logic: hardcoding server locations in your client code makes scaling and testing painful; use configuration files instead.
Ignoring permission and error boundaries: clients should prompt users before exposing sensitive server capabilities and gracefully degrade if a server goes down.
when_use: Use an MCP client when you need to wire multiple tool sources (APIs, databases, file systems, custom services) into an LLM-driven workflow and want them to feel like a unified interface.
when_avoid: Avoid building a custom client if you're testing a single-server integration; use the existing Claude Desktop or Cursor client first. Don't build a client if your workflow only needs one static tool endpoint.

faq.q: What's the difference between an MCP client and an MCP server?
faq.a: A server exposes capabilities (tools, resources, prompts) and waits for requests. A client discovers servers, maintains connections, and routes LLM requests to the right server. Clients are consumers; servers are providers.

faq.q: Can I run multiple MCP servers with one client?
faq.a: Yes. That's the main point. A single client can discover and connect to many servers simultaneously, and the LLM can call tools from any of them in the same session.

faq.q: Do I need to write code to build an MCP client?
faq.a: Not always. Claude Desktop and Cursor are production MCP clients; you configure them via JSON. You only write code if you need a custom client for a specific application or use case.

faq.q: How does an MCP client handle server failures?
faq.a: That depends on the client implementation. Well-designed clients implement timeout limits, retry logic, and graceful degradation so a failed server doesn't crash the entire LLM session.

faq.q: Can an MCP client run on my local machine?
faq.a: Yes. Clients can run locally (Claude Desktop, Cursor) or as a cloud service. Local clients connect to servers via stdio, local sockets, or network calls, depending on the server setup.

title: MCP Prompts
tldr: Reusable prompt templates MCP servers expose to clients, usually surfaced as slash-commands or workflow shortcuts.
plain_english: Think of MCP prompts as recipe templates. Instead of writing the same multi-step instructions every time, an MCP server packages them once—e.g., 'summarize-github-pr'—and your client (Claude, an IDE, a bot) offers it as a ready-to-use option. You fill in a parameter (like the PR number), and the server returns a fully-formed prompt ready to send to an LLM.
how_it_works: An MCP server defines prompts by declaring a name, description, and argument schema. When a client connects, it discovers these prompts and typically exposes them in the UI—slash-commands or menu items. Users invoke a prompt with parameters; the server substitutes those values into the template, optionally fetches referenced resources or calls tools, and returns the rendered prompt. The client then sends it to an LLM or uses it directly in a workflow.
why_it_matters: Prompts let server authors ship end-to-end workflows without requiring users to memorize or copy-paste instructions. A well-stocked prompt catalog drastically improves UX—teams spend less time on prompt engineering and more time on results. Prompts also encode domain knowledge (GitHub PR summaries, SQL debugging, code reviews) in a way that's instantly discoverable.
example: A GitHub MCP server defines a 'summarize-pr' prompt that takes a repository and PR number. When you type /summarize-pr in Claude Desktop, the server fetches the PR details via its tools, inserts them into a summary template, and returns a ready-to-send prompt. Claude then uses that prompt to write the summary—no manual copy-paste required.
pitfalls: Prompts that are too generic or too rigid fail to adapt to the user's actual need; test them against real use cases.
Missing parameter documentation forces users to guess what arguments to pass.
Prompts that reference server tools without declaring those tools will fail silently if the client doesn't support them.
Overloading a single prompt with too many optional parameters makes it harder to discover and use correctly.
when_use: Use MCP prompts when you're building a server that ships with common workflows or domain expertise (code analysis, data summarization, debugging), and you want clients to surface these as one-click actions.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP prompts if your workflows are highly user-specific or require real-time customization; prefer letting users write their own prompts or compose tools directly instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between an MCP prompt and a system prompt?
faq.a: A system prompt is a global instruction set sent to every LLM call. An MCP prompt is a reusable template that a server exposes for clients to invoke on-demand, often parameterized by user input.

faq.q: Can an MCP prompt call server tools?
faq.a: Yes. A prompt template can include calls to server tools, so the server can fetch live data (e.g., pull PR details from GitHub) before rendering the final prompt.

faq.q: How does a client discover MCP prompts?
faq.a: When a client connects to an MCP server, the server advertises its prompts via the MCP protocol. The client then lists them in its UI, usually as slash-commands or menu items.

faq.q: Are MCP prompts just static templates?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Prompts can be dynamic—the server can inspect parameters, fetch resources, and conditionally include sections before returning the rendered template to the client.

faq.q: Do I need to use MCP prompts if I'm building an MCP server?
faq.a: No. Prompts are optional. You can build a server with only tools or resources. Prompts are a convenience layer for common workflows.

title: MCP Resources
tldr: Read-only data exposed by MCP servers to LLMs—files, database rows, APIs—identified by URI.
plain_english: Resources are how an MCP server hands context to an LLM without the LLM having to fetch it manually. Think of them as labeled data packages: your server says "here's a file at this URI" or "here's a database row at that URI," and the client can include them in the conversation. Unlike tools, resources never change data—they're pure reads.
how_it_works: An MCP server declares resources with a URI (e.g., file:///workspace/docs/spec.md or postgres://prod/users/id:42), a MIME type (text/markdown, application/json), and optional metadata. The client lists available resources and lets users or agents pick which ones to attach to a session. The server returns the resource content on request. The LLM receives the context without needing to know how to query files or databases directly.
why_it_matters: Resources are RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) without custom plumbing. Instead of building retrieval logic yourself, you expose what the LLM needs as resources and let the MCP protocol handle the wiring. This reduces boilerplate in every tool that talks to your data.
example: A code review tool server exposes resources: git repo files at file:///repo/src/module.py, PR comments at github://pr/123/comments, and test results at postgres://ci_db/runs/latest. An LLM client picks the PR diff and test results, attaches them to the conversation, and the model reviews code with full context—no manual API calls in the LLM chain.
pitfalls: Treating resources as a substitute for tools: resources are read-only context, not actions. Use tools for writes or side effects.
Exposing resources without URI consistency: clients rely on URIs to deduplicate and cache. Inconsistent naming breaks session state.
Overloading a single resource with too much data: large responses block the client. Break them into smaller, scoped resources instead.
Forgetting MIME type or metadata: clients use these to interpret and prioritize resources. Missing them breaks intelligent resource selection.
when_use: Use resources when you need to hand static or slowly-changing context (docs, schema, logs, database snapshots) to an LLM without custom retrieval code.
when_avoid: Avoid resources if the data changes frequently during a session, requires complex filtering logic, or needs write operations. Use tools instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP resources and MCP tools?
faq.a: Resources are read-only context the server exposes. Tools are actions the LLM can call with side effects. Use resources for data, tools for operations.

faq.q: Can an MCP resource be large?
faq.a: Technically yes, but large resources block the client and waste context. Break them into smaller, scoped resources by entity, time range, or file.

faq.q: How do clients decide which resources to include?
faq.a: Clients typically show users a list of available resources and let them select manually, or agents/tools can request specific resources by URI.

faq.q: Do MCP resources support real-time updates?
faq.a: No. Resources are snapshots. If data changes mid-session, the client must re-fetch. For live data, use tools that query on-demand instead.

faq.q: Can I use a resource URI to access a private database?
faq.a: Yes, if your MCP server has database credentials. The server handles auth; the client just sees the URI. But expose only what the LLM should see.

title: MCP Sampling
tldr: MCP protocol feature that lets a server request LLM text generation from the client without managing its own model.
plain_english: Normally your client (like Claude Desktop) talks to an LLM, then calls tools on a server. MCP sampling flips that: the server asks the client to generate text using the LLM it already has access to. Think of it like asking your assistant to use their own reference library instead of requiring you to hand them one. The client typically shows you a permission prompt before responding.
how_it_works: When an MCP server needs generated text, it sends a sampling request to the client with a prompt, temperature, and other generation parameters. The client shows the user a disclosure prompt (so they know a tool is asking the LLM to generate text), then forwards the request to its LLM. The LLM returns completions, which the client sends back to the server. The server gets the result without storing API keys or bundling a model. This works because the MCP protocol includes a sampling sub-interface alongside tools, resources, and prompts.
why_it_matters: Sampling lets MCP servers implement LLM-powered features—summarization, analysis, generation—without needing per-tool API keys, local model infrastructure, or end-to-end model management. It also preserves privacy: a tool can ask the user's trusted client to generate text without the tool's code seeing the LLM directly. This unlocks a new class of MCP patterns: stateless, composable, LLM-backed tools.
example: A code-review MCP server can ask Claude Desktop to summarize a pull request's diff, extract key concerns, and suggest improvements—all through sampling. The server sends the diff as a prompt; Claude generates the summary using its own context window; the server receives the text and formats it into the review. No API keys, no rate limits to manage per-tool, no extra model downloads.
pitfalls: Assuming sampling is automatic: the user must explicitly approve each sampling request in the client UI, so don't rely on sampling for real-time critical paths.

Treating sampling prompts like tool inputs: sampling requests run through the client's full LLM pipeline (including system prompts and temperature), so results vary; design tool logic to handle variance.
Forgetting the user context: sampling runs with the user's LLM and settings, not a neutral baseline, so the output reflects their model choice (Claude vs. GPT vs. local model).
Overloading sampling for tool control: sampling is for text generation, not for routing decisions; if your tool needs deterministic logic, use function calls and conditional branches, not sampling.
when_use: Use MCP sampling when a server tool needs to generate, summarize, analyze, or transform text and the client has trusted LLM access; sampling is ideal for workflows where the LLM sits between the user and the tool.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP sampling when you need guaranteed deterministic output, sub-100ms latency, or the tool requires a specific model or fine-tune; also avoid if the tool must operate offline or the server cannot handle approval delays.

faq.q: Is MCP sampling the same as MCP tools?
faq.a: No. Tools let a server expose functions that the client or LLM can call. Sampling is the reverse: the server asks the client to run the LLM. Tools are for server-side action; sampling is for LLM-side generation.

faq.q: Do I need API keys to use MCP sampling?
faq.a: No. The server doesn't manage keys—the client does. Sampling works because your client is already authenticated to an LLM (Claude, OpenAI, etc.); the server just borrows that connection.

faq.q: Can I use MCP sampling offline?
faq.a: Only if your client has offline LLM access (e.g., a local model). If the client uses a cloud API, sampling requires internet and authentication to that API.

faq.q: What happens if the user denies a sampling request?
faq.a: The client returns an error to the server. The server must handle rejection gracefully—either fall back to a default response, skip the operation, or ask the user differently.

faq.q: Does sampling work with all MCP clients?
faq.a: Sampling is part of the MCP spec, but not all clients implement it. Desktop clients like Claude Desktop do; simpler clients might not. Check your client's documentation.

title: MCP Server
tldr: A program exposing resources, prompts, and tools over the Model Context Protocol for LLM clients to consume.
plain_english: An MCP server is like a plugin that lets AI applications talk to your tools and data. It listens for requests from Claude, Cursor, or other MCP-compatible clients and responds with information or actions. You run it locally or on a server, and it declares what it can do—fetch files, search databases, call APIs—so clients know what to ask.
how_it_works: You implement the MCP server interface in Python, TypeScript, Kotlin, or another supported language. The server connects to a client over stdio, HTTP+SSE, or another transport and registers its capabilities: resources (data sources), prompts (templates), and tools (functions). When a client calls a method, the server processes the request and returns the result. The client stays in control of the conversation; the server only responds when asked.
why_it_matters: MCP servers are the standard way to give AI applications access to your product's APIs, documents, and workflows without building custom integrations for each AI tool. They reduce friction for users adopting Claude, Cursor, or Cline with your platform.
example: A GitHub MCP server registers tools like 'create-issue' and 'search-repos'. When you ask Claude in Cursor to 'open an issue in my frontend repo with these steps', Claude calls the server's create-issue tool, which constructs the GitHub API call and returns the new issue URL. You write the server once; it works with any MCP-compatible client.
pitfalls: Exposing unsafe operations without auth checks—MCP servers run in the user's environment, so validate all inputs and respect permissions.
Not handling streaming resources efficiently—large datasets can block the client; use pagination or chunked responses.
Inconsistent error messages—clients depend on machine-readable error codes to retry or degrade gracefully.
Forgetting to version your schema—breaking changes to tool arguments will confuse clients expecting the old format.
when_use: Use an MCP server when you want to expose your product's data or actions to multiple AI applications (Claude, Cursor, Cline, etc.) without writing a separate integration for each.
when_avoid: Avoid MCP servers if you need real-time bidirectional communication (use WebSockets instead) or if your use case is a one-off integration with a single client (a direct API may be simpler).

faq.q: What's the difference between an MCP server and an MCP client?
faq.a: A server exposes resources and tools; a client (like Claude Desktop) consumes them. The server runs in your environment or on your infrastructure; the client runs on the user's machine or in their app.

faq.q: Do I need to use an official SDK to write an MCP server?
faq.a: No. Official SDKs for Python and TypeScript simplify implementation, but you can implement the protocol directly if you understand the JSON-RPC message format and transport layer.

faq.q: Can an MCP server run in the cloud?
faq.a: Yes. You can deploy it as a containerized service (Docker, Kubernetes) and expose it over HTTP+SSE or gRPC. Clients connect via a remote endpoint instead of stdio.

faq.q: Is an MCP server secure for exposing sensitive APIs?
faq.a: Only if you implement authentication and authorization. An MCP server is a network service, so use API keys, TLS, and permission checks—don't rely on client-side validation.

faq.q: What transport protocols does MCP support?
faq.a: stdio (local), HTTP+SSE (server-sent events), and HTTP with streaming. stdio is simplest for local tools; HTTP variants work for remote servers.

schema_jsonld.name: MCP Server

title: MCP Tools
tldr: Functions an MCP server exposes to LLMs, enabling side-effecting actions like running SQL or creating issues.
plain_english: Think of MCP tools as the "do something" actions available through an MCP server. While resources let you read data and prompts give you templates, tools let you perform operations—execute queries, write files, post messages. The LLM decides when to call them based on what you're asking it to do.
how_it_works: An MCP server defines tools as JSON Schema objects describing inputs, outputs, and behavior. The client exposes these to the LLM, which can invoke them via tool-use messages. When the tool runs, it sends back structured results (success, error, or data). The LLM sees the outcome and decides next steps—retry, call another tool, or respond to you.
why_it_matters: Tools are how agents interact with external systems. Without them, LLMs can only read and suggest; with them, they can act. Most MCP adoption centers on defining and consuming the right tools for your workflow.
example: A code review bot uses MCP tools to fetch pull requests from GitHub (tool: get_pr), run linters against the diff (tool: lint_code), and post comments back (tool: post_comment). Each tool is described with JSON Schema so the LLM knows what parameters to pass and what to expect back.
pitfalls: Exposing a tool with vague input schema forces the LLM to guess parameters—always write precise JSON Schema with required fields and descriptions.
Tools that take too long to execute (>10s) will timeout in many LLM integrations; break expensive operations into smaller, faster tools.
Returning raw errors without context leaves the LLM unable to recover; wrap tool results with suggested next steps on failure.
Defining too many tools (>20) causes the LLM to forget which ones exist or call them incorrectly; group related operations and keep the set lean.
when_use: Use MCP tools when you need an LLM to perform actions—write data, call APIs, run commands, or modify external systems.
when_avoid: Avoid tools for read-only access (use MCP resources instead) or for reusable instruction templates (use MCP prompts).

faq.q: What's the difference between MCP tools and function calling?
faq.a: Function calling is an LLM capability; MCP tools are the protocol layer for exposing functions over MCP. An MCP server uses function calling under the hood to let clients define and invoke tools.

faq.q: Can an MCP tool return multiple results?
faq.a: Yes. A tool can return structured content with multiple fields or arrays. The LLM sees all of it and can decide what to do next based on the full result.

faq.q: How does the MCP server know if a tool call succeeded?
faq.a: The tool handler in the server returns a result object. You control the structure—include a status field, error message, or data. The client passes this back to the LLM so it can react.

faq.q: What happens if a tool takes too long to run?
faq.a: Most LLM integrations have a timeout (typically 30–60 seconds). If your tool exceeds it, the call fails. Design tools to be fast, or split long operations into separate tools that report progress.

faq.q: Can I make a tool optional—only available in some MCP clients?
faq.a: Yes. Each tool definition is independent. A client only sees and can call tools that the server declares. You can conditionally register tools based on configuration or capabilities.

meta_title: MCP Tools — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Functions an MCP server exposes to LLMs, enabling side-effecting actions like running SQL or creating issues.

schema_jsonld.name: MCP Tools
schema_jsonld.description: Functions an MCP server exposes to LLMs, enabling side-effecting actions like running SQL or creating issues.

title: Messages API (Anthropic)
tldr: Anthropic's native API format for Claude with system prompt as a top-level field and conversation as a messages array.
plain_english: Think of the Messages API as Anthropic's own way of talking to Claude. Instead of mixing your system instructions into a messages list like some other APIs, you put the system prompt in its own box at the top, then send user and assistant messages below. This separation makes it clearer what your standing instructions are versus what's happening in the conversation.
how_it_works: You send a request with three main pieces: a top-level system field (your standing instructions), a messages array (user and assistant turns, always starting with user), and optional parameters like tools or cache_control. Claude responds with an assistant message that can include text, tool_use blocks (for calling functions), or thinking blocks (in extended thinking mode). Tool results come back as tool_result messages in your next request. Prompt caching works here via explicit cache_control markers you place on messages.
why_it_matters: Claude-specific features like prompt caching and extended thinking are only available through the Messages API, not through other bridges. The clear separation of system from conversation reduces mistakes in prompt structure and makes it easier to version and test your instructions independently from your conversation logic.
example: A support team building an internal ticket classifier adds their company style guide as the system prompt, then sends tickets as user messages. Claude responds with tool_use blocks to call a routing function. On the second request, they add cache_control to the system prompt so the 5KB style guide isn't re-processed for every ticket, cutting latency by 40% and API cost by 20%.
pitfalls: Forgetting that messages must alternate user/assistant or end with user—sending two user messages in a row fails.
Mixing static instructions into the messages array instead of using the system field, losing the structure advantage and breaking prompt caching.
Not reading the tool_use response format; tool results must come back as tool_result blocks in the next message, not as plain text.
Assuming the Messages API is compatible with OpenAI Chat Completions clients; it is not—you need Anthropic's SDK or compatible wrappers.
when_use: Use the Messages API when building Claude-native features (prompt caching, extended thinking, computer use), when you need a clean system-versus-conversation boundary, or when you want to avoid SDK abstraction layers.
when_avoid: Avoid the Messages API if you need a single client library to work with both Claude and non-Anthropic models; use a compatibility layer or multi-model gateway instead.

faq.q: Is the Messages API the same as OpenAI's Chat Completions API?
faq.a: No. Both use a messages array, but Anthropic separates the system prompt into its own top-level field, and the tool use format and behavior differ. They are not interchangeable.

faq.q: Can I use the Messages API if I only want to send text, no tools?
faq.a: Yes. Tools are optional. You can use the Messages API for simple text-in, text-out conversations; you just get the system/messages structure.

faq.q: Does prompt caching work automatically on the Messages API?
faq.a: No. You must explicitly mark which messages or system blocks you want cached using the cache_control parameter. Then Claude batches them and caches subsequent requests.

faq.q: What happens if I send two user messages in a row?
faq.a: The API rejects it. Messages must alternate between user and assistant, or end with user. Claude cannot respond to back-to-back user turns.

faq.q: Do I need a different SDK to use the Messages API?
faq.a: You need Anthropic's official SDK or a compatible wrapper. Generic OpenAI-compatible libraries will not work because the request/response shapes differ.

title: OpenAI Batch API
tldr: Submit bulk requests in JSONL format; OpenAI processes them asynchronously at 50% discount within 24 hours.
plain_english: Think of Batch API like sending mail instead of calling someone. You bundle all your requests into one file and hand it off; OpenAI processes them whenever they have capacity and sends back a single file of results. You save 50% on cost by giving up immediate answers.
how_it_works: Prepare a JSONL file where each line contains a custom_id, request endpoint, and request body (e.g., chat completions, embeddings). Post the file to the Batch API endpoint. OpenAI queues your job and processes requests within a 24-hour window when hardware capacity is available. Retrieve the completed batch by polling the status endpoint or setting up a webhook. You download the JSONL results file containing outputs matched to your custom_ids.
why_it_matters: Batch API cuts API costs in half for any offline workload—eval sets, synthetic data generation, backfill operations, or bulk embeddings. If your deadline is flexible, Batch is the standard cost optimization for data pipelines.
example: A research team runs MMLU evaluation on 10,000 prompts. Using chat completions API directly costs $10 at standard rates; Batch API costs $5 for the same job, completing overnight. Another use case: a company backfills 1M embeddings for a vector database in a single batch job instead of streaming requests over weeks.
pitfalls: Forgetting that a custom_id must be unique per request—duplicates will overwrite silently.
Submitting a batch exceeding 100 MB or 10M input tokens; requests are rejected without partial processing.
Assuming JSONL line order matches result order; you must match on custom_id, not position.
Not retrying on transient 5xx errors; the API does not auto-retry failed requests within a batch.
when_use: Use Batch API when you have offline workloads (eval, backfill, synthetic data generation), can wait 24 hours, and want to cut costs in half.
when_avoid: Avoid Batch when you need real-time responses, sub-second latency, or interactive user-facing features.

faq.q: How long does an OpenAI Batch API job actually take?
faq.a: Up to 24 hours. Most complete within 1–2 hours during low-traffic periods. Your actual time depends on queue depth and request complexity. You can poll status or use webhooks to avoid checking manually.

faq.q: Can I use Batch API for vision or fine-tuning?
faq.a: Batch supports chat completions and embeddings endpoints only. Fine-tuning and vision tasks require standard API calls or separate fine-tuning endpoints.

faq.q: What happens if a single request in my batch fails?
faq.a: That request fails independently; others complete normally. OpenAI returns an error object in the results file for the failed custom_id. You must retry failed requests in a new batch or via standard API.

faq.q: Is Batch API cheaper than using Assistants API?
faq.a: Yes. Batch is 50% off standard rates. Assistants API pricing is separate and often higher due to overhead. Use Batch for bulk offline work; use Assistants for multi-turn, stateful conversations.

faq.q: Can I cancel or pause a batch after submission?
faq.a: You cannot pause a batch. If submitted and queued, it will run. You can discard results after it completes, but you pay for the full processing.

title: OpenAPI Tool Binding
tldr: Automatically converts OpenAPI specs into LLM-callable tool definitions, exposing REST APIs to agents instantly.
plain_english: An OpenAPI specification is a machine-readable document that describes your REST API's endpoints, parameters, and responses. Tool binding reads that spec and generates function-calling definitions that an LLM can understand and invoke. Instead of manually writing schemas for each endpoint, your existing API documentation becomes your tool definitions automatically.
how_it_works: You provide an OpenAPI YAML or JSON file to a tool binding system (LangChain, OpenAI's GPTs, Claude's MCP). The system parses the spec and extracts endpoint metadata: HTTP method, path, parameters, request/response schemas, and descriptions. For each endpoint, it generates a function definition with type-checked arguments and return types. When the LLM needs to call an API, it invokes the function by name with structured arguments; the framework handles HTTP serialization and response mapping back to the LLM.
why_it_matters: Manual schema design per tool creates drift: you maintain two definitions (API docs and tool schemas) that diverge over time. Binding eliminates that duplication—changes to your OpenAPI spec immediately propagate to the LLM. This cuts integration time from hours to minutes and reduces bugs from schema mismatches.
example: A Stripe integration: instead of manually defining 20 schemas for payment, customer, and invoice endpoints, you point your agent framework at Stripe's published OpenAPI spec. The framework auto-generates all 20 tools. When Stripe adds a new parameter to the payment endpoint, your agent's schema updates on the next sync—no code changes required.
pitfalls: OpenAPI specs often omit rate limits and error cases; the generated tool can call an endpoint that fails or rate-limits unexpectedly.
Enum fields and strict input validation in OpenAPI don't always map cleanly to LLM outputs; the model may hallucinate values outside the spec.
Nested or deeply structured request bodies can confuse LLMs; simplify schemas or add clear examples in the OpenAPI spec.
Tool descriptions auto-extracted from specs are often too terse; add narrative context in the `description` field for better agent reasoning.
when_use: Use when you have a stable, well-documented REST API and want agents to call it; the API documentation is authoritative and kept current.
when_avoid: Avoid when your OpenAPI spec is incomplete, outdated, or missing critical metadata (like rate limits or deprecations); manual tool design is safer when agent safety is critical.

faq.q: Can I use OpenAPI tool binding with any LLM?
faq.a: Only if the LLM supports function calling (most modern ones do). The binding generates function schemas compatible with OpenAI, Anthropic, and other function-calling APIs, but your framework must pass those schemas to the model correctly.

faq.q: What's the difference between OpenAPI tool binding and writing functions manually?
faq.a: Binding auto-generates schemas from your API docs; manual writing lets you customize descriptions, validation, and structure. Binding is faster but less flexible. Use binding for straightforward CRUD endpoints, manual definitions for complex logic or safety-critical calls.

faq.q: Does my OpenAPI spec have to be public?
faq.a: No. You can bind private or internal specs. The binding system only reads the YAML/JSON file—it doesn't publish it. Store the spec securely alongside your agent code.

faq.q: How do I test an auto-generated tool before deploying it to agents?
faq.a: Call the generated function directly with test inputs, or use a tool-calling harness (LangChain's tool validators, eval frameworks like DeepEval). Check that the generated schemas match your actual API behavior.

faq.q: Will the agent actually understand what a tool does if I use auto-generated descriptions?
faq.a: It depends on the description quality in your OpenAPI spec. Auto-extracted summaries are often bare-bones. Add concrete examples and intent notes (e.g., 'Use this to refund an order, not to modify it') to your spec for better agent reasoning.

title: Function Calling (Parallel)
tldr: LLM emits multiple tool calls in one response, executed concurrently, cutting latency for independent requests.
plain_english: Instead of asking a question, waiting for an answer, then asking another question, you ask three questions at once and get all three answers back together. The model figures out which requests don't depend on each other and tells the system to run them in parallel. You then feed all the results back to the model in a single turn.
how_it_works: When a model detects multiple independent tool calls (e.g., fetch weather for Tokyo, Paris, NYC), it emits all three in a single response object instead of waiting for the first to finish. Your client receives an array of tool calls with separate IDs and executes them concurrently via thread pool, subprocess, or async handlers. Once all results return, you send them back to the model in one message with the same call IDs attached. The model processes the batch and responds accordingly. OpenAI, Anthropic, and most recent LLMs support this via their function-calling or tool-use APIs.
why_it_matters: For agents that gather multiple independent pieces of information—market data, weather, user profiles—parallel calls collapse N sequential round-trips into 1 batch, cutting wall-clock time from seconds to milliseconds. This is the biggest latency win available to tool-using agents without changing model architecture. It moves from request-response chains to true concurrent execution.
example: An expense-reporting agent processes a receipt by calling three tools in parallel: extract_ocr_text (scan the image), lookup_vendor_tax_id (search database), and get_exchange_rate (fetch USD to EUR). All three execute concurrently. Within 200ms, the model gets back text, tax ID, and rate, and can reconcile the expense in the next turn—instead of waiting 200ms per call sequentially.
pitfalls: Calling tools that depend on each other in parallel (e.g., list_files then read_file on the first result) will fail when downstream calls use undefined IDs.
Not deduplicating tool calls: if the model requests weather twice, execute both calls and return both results; the model handles idempotency.
Treating parallel calls as atomic: one tool failure does not rollback others; return partial results with error details so the model can retry or skip.
Assuming all models support parallel calls equally; some older or smaller models may not structure calls correctly; test with your target model.
when_use: Use parallel function calling when your agent fetches multiple independent pieces of data (multi-location lookups, multi-API aggregation, batch retrieval) and latency is a concern.
when_avoid: Avoid parallel calling when tool results have strong dependencies, when execution order matters, or when your deployment cannot handle concurrent I/O.

faq.q: What's the difference between parallel function calling and regular function calling?
faq.a: Regular calling emits one tool request, waits for the result, then emits the next. Parallel calling emits multiple independent requests in one batch. Regular calling takes N × latency; parallel takes 1 × max_latency.

faq.q: Do all LLMs support parallel function calls?
faq.a: No. OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, and newer models do. Smaller or older models may not structure multiple calls in a single response. Check your model's API docs.

faq.q: What happens if one parallel tool call fails?
faq.a: The failure is returned in the result array with that call's ID and an error message. Other results succeed. Your client code must handle partial success and decide whether to retry, skip, or escalate.

faq.q: Can I call dependent tools in parallel?
faq.a: Not safely. If tool B needs the output of tool A, request tool A first, wait for the result, then request tool B. Parallel only works for truly independent calls.

faq.q: How much latency does parallel calling save?
faq.a: If you have 3 tools taking 100ms, 150ms, and 200ms each, sequential takes 450ms; parallel takes 200ms (the slowest). Savings scale with call count and diversity of latencies.

meta_title: Parallel Function Calling — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: LLM emits multiple tool calls in one response, executed concurrently, cutting latency for independent requests.

schema_jsonld.name: Function Calling (Parallel)

title: Prompt Caching (API)
tldr: Reuse computed KV cache from repeated prompt prefixes across API calls to cut cost and latency by 50–90%.
plain_english: When you send the same long context (like a system prompt or document) to an LLM multiple times, the API stores the mathematical computation from that prefix after the first call. On the next call with the same prefix, the model skips recomputing it and jumps straight to generating output. Think of it like memoizing a math problem—you solve it once, cache the answer, and look it up instead of recalculating.
how_it_works: After your first API request, the provider computes the KV cache (the attention weights) for a designated prefix and stores it for 5 minutes to 1 hour. Subsequent requests that start with the same prefix reuse that cached state, skipping the prefill phase. You signal cacheable sections via provider-specific syntax: Anthropic uses cache_control tags, OpenAI caches automatically on repeated requests, Google requires explicit prefix markers. Cost and latency drop proportionally to the cached portion—typically 50–90% savings on the prefix itself.
why_it_matters: For agents making repeated calls, RAG systems with large document contexts, and long-conversation workflows, prompt caching often cuts API costs in half without changing your code logic. It's the difference between a $10 and $5 request when you're re-analyzing the same 50KB document 100 times a day.
example: A customer-support chatbot processes 1000 conversations daily, each starting with a 10KB company knowledge base in the system prompt. Without caching, that's ~10MB of redundant computation per day. With prompt caching enabled via Anthropic's cache_control, the knowledge base is computed once and reused for 5 minutes, cutting that workload to ~50KB total. Cost drops from $50/day to $10/day on that overhead alone.
pitfalls: Cache TTL is short (5 min to 1 hour depending on provider); don't assume persistence across deployments or shifts.
If your prefix changes even slightly (e.g., one extra space or different system prompt version), the cache misses and you lose savings.
Providers count cache writes differently; some charge more for cache-setting than cache-hitting, making small batches less economical.
Not all model versions support caching; check provider docs before architecting around it.
when_use: Use prompt caching when you repeatedly send the same long prefix (≥1KB) within the provider's cache window, or when you're running batch jobs with shared context across many calls.
when_avoid: Avoid it for one-shot requests, constantly changing system prompts, or when your entire prompt fits in a single token—the overhead outweighs savings.

faq.q: How much does prompt caching actually save?
faq.a: Typical savings are 50–90% on the cached prefix alone (both cost and latency). If your cached prefix is 80% of the request, expect ~60% overall savings. Exact numbers depend on provider pricing and your cache hit rate.

faq.q: Is prompt caching the same as semantic caching?
faq.a: No. Prompt caching reuses the exact same token prefix via KV cache. Semantic caching matches similar (not identical) prompts and reuses their outputs. Prompt caching is faster and cheaper but requires exact matches.

faq.q: Do I have to change my code to use prompt caching?
faq.a: It depends on the provider. OpenAI caches automatically on repeated requests. Anthropic requires you to add cache_control tags to your system prompt. Google requires explicit prefix markers. Check your provider's docs.

faq.q: What happens if the cache expires while I'm working?
faq.a: Your next request will miss the cache and recompute the prefix from scratch. Cost and latency jump back to baseline. Cache TTL is typically 5 minutes to 1 hour; design batch jobs to stay within that window if possible.

faq.q: Can I cache the entire conversation history?
faq.a: Yes, if it stays under the provider's cache-write limit. But if users add new messages frequently, only the stable part (system prompt + instructions) benefits. Mark only truly static sections for caching.

title: Rate Limit (LLM APIs)
tldr: Provider caps on request frequency and tokens per minute, tiered by account level; exceeding returns HTTP 429.
plain_english: Rate limits are quotas your API provider enforces to control costs and protect their infrastructure. Think of it like a water meter on your house—you get X gallons per minute; if you exceed it, the flow stops until the next minute starts. For LLM APIs, limits come in two flavors: requests per minute (RPM)—how many API calls you can make—and tokens per minute (TPM)—the total words/tokens across all those calls.
how_it_works: Your API provider tracks incoming requests and tokens in real time. When you hit the limit, the server rejects the next request with HTTP 429 and a `Retry-After` header telling you how long to wait. Production systems handle this by implementing exponential backoff (wait 1s, then 2s, then 4s before retrying), request bucketing (queue pending calls), and multi-key failover (switch to a secondary API key if one is exhausted). Limits are tiered: a free account gets 3 RPM / 90 K TPM; a paid account gets 10k RPM / 2M TPM. New accounts have lower limits regardless of payment tier to prevent abuse.
why_it_matters: Rate limits are the most common cause of production LLM failures and the hardest to debug post-launch. Undersizing your tier costs you dropped requests; over-provisioning costs money. Planning for traffic spikes (e.g., a viral feature launch) requires knowing your provider's ramp-up policy—you can't move from 100 TPM to 10M TPM in one day without approval and lead time.
example: A chatbot startup launches a free tier and gets 50k users in week one. They provisioned for 10k concurrent requests but only have a 3 RPM limit (free tier). Users see timeout errors, and the product team has to request an upgrade in writing, wait 24–48 hours for approval, and manually adjust the limit. A week later, the same startup built exponential backoff into their client SDK and kept a waiting queue; when they hit limits, requests queued and retried automatically instead of failing, hiding the rate limit from users until they could upgrade.
pitfalls: Assuming your limit is fixed—many providers auto-adjust based on usage history and payment; a spike from day 1 won't auto-scale.
Not implementing backoff in your first version; by the time you add it, production is already burning.
Mixing RPM and TPM budgets mentally; a 100-token request counts the same toward TPM as a 1-token request, so RPM limits can hit before TPM.
Forgetting that parallel requests count separately; firing 10 concurrent requests consumes 10 RPM, not 1.
when_use: Use proactive rate-limit handling from day one in any production LLM integration, especially if user-facing or scaling toward concurrent traffic.
when_avoid: Skip complex backoff logic only if testing offline or in a sandbox with no concurrency; always add it before any multi-user deployment.

faq.q: What does HTTP 429 mean?
faq.a: 429 is the HTTP status code for 'Too Many Requests'—your provider's server is telling you to stop sending requests and wait. The response headers include `Retry-After` (seconds to wait) or `x-ratelimit-reset-tokens` (when your token budget refills).

faq.q: How long does a rate limit last?
faq.a: Rate limits reset on a sliding window (usually per minute or per day, depending on the dimension). Once the window passes, your quota refills automatically; you don't need to do anything.

faq.q: Can I request a higher rate limit?
faq.a: Yes, but it varies by provider. OpenAI, Anthropic, and others require you to apply, prove usage, and wait 1–7 days. Some providers (Groq, Together AI) auto-scale for paid accounts. Always check your provider's ramp-up policy before launch.

faq.q: What's the difference between TPM and RPM limits?
faq.a: RPM caps how many API calls you make per minute (e.g., 10,000 requests). TPM caps total tokens across all calls (e.g., 2 million tokens). Both are active; you hit whichever limit first.

faq.q: Does batch API have rate limits?
faq.a: Batch APIs (like OpenAI's batch endpoint) have separate, usually much higher limits—they're designed for non-real-time workloads and don't compete with synchronous API calls for quota.

title: Realtime API (OpenAI)
tldr: WebSocket API for speech-in, speech-out LLM conversations with sub-500ms latency and native interruption.
plain_english: Instead of converting speech to text, sending text to an LLM, then converting back to speech (three separate steps), the Realtime API handles audio directly end-to-end. Your voice goes in, the model listens and speaks back—with you able to interrupt mid-sentence, just like a phone call. The whole round-trip typically takes 300–500 milliseconds.
how_it_works: Your client opens a WebSocket connection to OpenAI's Realtime endpoint and streams raw PCM audio (or µ-law). The model processes speech directly without ASR/LLM/TTS handoffs, returning synthesized audio back in near-real-time. The API manages turn-taking: it detects when you stop speaking, generates a response, and streams it back while you listen. You can interrupt a response mid-stream, triggering an immediate cancel. Function calls, session state, and conversation history persist across turns.
why_it_matters: Voice products built on separate ASR, LLM, and TTS services feel laggy and robotic because latency stacks. Realtime APIs eliminate that bottleneck, making voice agents feel conversational—critical for customer support, accessibility tools, and interactive AI companions. Lower latency also reduces perceived awkwardness in turn-taking.
example: A customer support team deploys a voice agent using Realtime API to handle tier-1 inquiries. A caller says 'I need to reset my password,' the agent interrupts with clarifying questions in real-time, and if the caller talks over the agent, the audio stream resets instantly without awkward silence. End-to-end latency stays under 400ms, so the interaction feels natural—no 2–3 second gaps between human and AI.
pitfalls: Assuming Realtime handles all languages equally; check your use case's language against OpenAI's supported list.
Not handling connection drops gracefully; WebSocket reconnection logic is your responsibility, not the API's.
Forgetting that interruption cancels the entire response stream; if you need partial audio saved, buffer before send.
Treating function calls synchronously; they block the audio stream, so long-running external calls will cause noticeable pauses.
when_use: Use Realtime API when you need sub-500ms voice interactions with interruption, or when your product's core experience is conversational speech rather than request-response text.
when_avoid: Avoid if your use case is batch voice transcription, one-way voice announcements, or if latency under 300ms is not critical to user experience.

faq.q: How is Realtime API different from Whisper + GPT + TTS?
faq.a: Realtime handles audio end-to-end in one connection with native interruption. The three-step pipeline (Whisper transcribes, GPT responds, TTS synthesizes) serializes latency—each hop adds 100–500ms. Realtime skips intermediate steps entirely.

faq.q: Can I use Realtime API with custom voices?
faq.a: You select from OpenAI's preset voices in the session config. You cannot upload custom voice files or clone a speaker's voice with Realtime API.

faq.q: What happens if I talk while the agent is speaking?
faq.a: The API detects your voice and immediately cancels the agent's outgoing audio stream. You then own the turn. If you stop, the model processes your speech and generates a new response.

faq.q: Does Realtime API support function calling?
faq.a: Yes. You define tools in the session config. When the model calls a function, audio output pauses, you execute the function server-side, and send the result back. Audio resumes after.

faq.q: What's the pricing model for Realtime API?
faq.a: OpenAI charges per input and output tokens at published rates (same as Chat API), plus per-minute connection fees for active sessions. Check current pricing on OpenAI's billing page.

meta_title: Realtime API (OpenAI) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: WebSocket API for speech-in, speech-out LLM conversations with sub-500ms latency and native interruption.

schema_jsonld.name: Realtime API (OpenAI)
schema_jsonld.description: WebSocket API for speech-in, speech-out LLM conversations with sub-500ms latency and native interruption.

title: Responses API (OpenAI)
tldr: OpenAI's stateful API for multi-turn conversations, tool use, and reasoning—replaces Chat Completions for complex workflows.
plain_english: Think of Responses API as the successor to Chat Completions. Instead of you managing conversation history manually, the API keeps track of it for you. You tell it what tools (search, code interpreter, file lookup) it can use, and it handles the reasoning and tool calls without you writing extra parsing logic.
how_it_works: You send a request with a system prompt, user message, and available tools. The API maintains conversation state internally—no need to append every previous exchange to your payload. It returns structured reasoning steps (for o1 and reasoning models), tool calls, and final response in one object. You can set reasoning-specific parameters like max_reasoning_tokens. Tool results feed back into the same conversation thread automatically.
why_it_matters: For agentic workflows and reasoning-heavy tasks, Chat Completions becomes verbose: you manually track history, parse tool calls, and reconstruct context. Responses API flattens this into a single stateful interface. If you're building multi-step agents or using o1, this is the recommended entry point. During the transition, you may maintain both APIs depending on model availability and use case.
example: A research team building an autonomous code reviewer sends a GitHub PR to Responses API once, then the API orchestrates tool calls: fetch repository context (file search), run the code interpreter on diffs, and iterate without the team re-packaging context between calls. The conversation state persists server-side.
pitfalls: Assuming Responses API works identically to Chat Completions—tool semantics and reasoning output differ significantly.
Not setting reasoning token budgets when using o1; your cost scales with reasoning tokens, not just output.
Treating Responses API state as persistent across sessions; state is per-request conversation, not long-term memory.
Forgetting that not all OpenAI models support Responses API yet—check the current model matrix before migrating.
when_use: Use Responses API when building multi-turn agents, reasoning workflows, or applications that call tools repeatedly and need server-side conversation state.
when_avoid: Avoid Responses API for simple single-turn completions, compatibility with non-OpenAI models, or when you need fine-grained control over every message.

faq.q: What's the difference between Responses API and Chat Completions API?
faq.a: Responses API manages conversation state server-side and integrates tool calls natively; Chat Completions requires you to reconstruct history and parse tool calls yourself. Responses API is built for multi-step agentic flows.

faq.q: Do I still need the Assistants API if I use Responses API?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Responses API covers most Assistants API use cases (tools, reasoning, state) in a simpler, more direct format. Assistants API remains useful for persistent threads and file management across sessions.

faq.q: Can I use Responses API with GPT-4 and older models?
faq.a: No. Responses API is designed for o1 and newer reasoning models. Check OpenAI's model compatibility matrix for the current list of supported models.

faq.q: How much do reasoning tokens cost in Responses API?
faq.a: Reasoning tokens are priced separately and cost more than regular completion tokens. Set reasoning_effort to 'low', 'medium', or 'high' to control spending, or cap max_reasoning_tokens.

faq.q: Does Responses API save conversation history between requests?
faq.a: No. State persists within a single request conversation. For long-term memory, you must implement external storage or use the Assistants API with persistent threads.

meta_title: Responses API (OpenAI) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: OpenAI's stateful API for multi-turn conversations, tool use, and reasoning—replaces Chat Completions for complex workflows.

schema_jsonld.name: Responses API (OpenAI)

title: Responses API Reasoning (OpenAI)
tldr: OpenAI's API surface for reasoning models, letting you control inference compute and access reasoning summaries.
plain_english: The Responses API is OpenAI's first-class interface for reasoning models like o1 and o3. Think of it as the successor to Chat Completions—designed specifically for tasks that need extended thinking time. You control how much compute the model spends reasoning (minimal to high), decide whether to show or hide the reasoning process, and use tools like web search or code execution within the reasoning loop.
how_it_works: When you call Responses API with a reasoning model, you pass a reasoning.effort parameter (minimal, low, medium, or high) that allocates test-time compute. The model generates both a reasoning phase (internal steps and problem breakdown) and a final response. You can surface the reasoning summary to users or keep it hidden. Built-in tools (web_search, code_interpreter, file_search) work natively during reasoning loops, unlike Chat Completions where tool integration felt bolted-on. The API returns structured outputs with both reasoning tokens and response tokens tracked separately.
why_it_matters: For OpenAI reasoning models, this is the native interface—Chat Completions has incomplete support. You get finer control over inference-time cost and the ability to show or audit reasoning, which matters for transparency and for tasks where intermediate steps guide users (education, debugging, planning).
example: A financial analyst using o3 via Responses API to evaluate a merger with reasoning.effort='high' spends more compute, receives a detailed reasoning summary showing assumptions and risk factors, and uses code_interpreter to validate spreadsheet calculations—all within a single request. They surface the reasoning to stakeholders to justify the recommendation.
pitfalls: Setting reasoning_effort='high' on every request ignores cost; start with 'medium' and scale only for high-value decisions.
Assuming all tools work identically in reasoning loops; web_search timing and code_interpreter sandbox limits affect reasoning phase differently than in Chat Completions.
Hiding reasoning summaries when they would improve user trust; transparency becomes a feature, not a bug.
Mixing reasoning models with non-reasoning models in the same system without accounting for response latency differences.
when_use: Use Responses API Reasoning when you need explicit control over inference compute, must show or audit intermediate reasoning, or require tight tool integration within reasoning loops.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need sub-second latency (reasoning adds latency), have cost-sensitive workloads without clear ROI from extended thinking, or don't need the reasoning process exposed.

faq.q: What's the difference between Responses API and Chat Completions API?
faq.a: Responses API is built for reasoning models with native reasoning.effort control, structured reasoning summaries, and seamless tool integration. Chat Completions has limited reasoning model support and was designed before test-time compute became a first-class concern.

faq.q: How much does reasoning.effort affect cost and latency?
faq.a: Higher reasoning.effort allocates more tokens to the reasoning phase, increasing both cost and latency. 'minimal' is fast and cheap; 'high' can 10x tokens and latency. The tradeoff is accuracy and solution quality.

faq.q: Can I hide the reasoning from users?
faq.a: Yes. The API returns reasoning summaries separately from the final response. You choose whether to display them, log them, or discard them—useful for compliance, UX control, or reducing verbosity.

faq.q: Do tools like web search run inside or outside the reasoning phase?
faq.a: Tools run within the reasoning loop itself. The model reasons, decides to call a tool, uses the result to refine reasoning, then outputs a final answer—tighter integration than Chat Completions allows.

faq.q: Which OpenAI models support Responses API Reasoning?
faq.a: o1, o1-mini, o3, and o3-mini support Responses API natively. Non-reasoning models (GPT-4, GPT-4o) also work via Responses API but without the reasoning.effort parameter.

title: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
tldr: HTTP protocol for streaming server-to-client updates over a persistent connection; how LLM APIs deliver tokens.
plain_english: SSE is like opening a phone line where the server talks and you listen—the connection stays open, and data arrives line-by-line as it's ready. Unlike request-response HTTP, SSE lets the server push updates without you asking again. Every LLM streaming response you get uses SSE under the hood.
how_it_works: You open an HTTP GET connection to an endpoint that returns content-type: text/event-stream. The server sends events in plaintext: each event is a data: field followed by the payload, separated by newlines. Your browser or client reads these lines as they arrive without closing the connection. For LLMs, each event typically contains a JSON chunk with the next token(s). The connection closes only when the server sends it or network failure occurs.
why_it_matters: SSE is simpler than WebSockets (one-way only), works through most proxies, and requires no special authentication beyond HTTP. Understanding it lets you debug streaming APIs, build custom clients without SDKs, and implement reliable reconnection logic. It's the actual transport layer every major LLM provider uses.
example: You call OpenAI's chat completions endpoint with stream: true. OpenAI opens an SSE connection and sends data: {"choices":[{"delta":{"content":"Hello"}}]} followed by data: {"choices":[{"delta":{"content":" world"}}]} line-by-line. Your client reads each line, parses the JSON, and displays tokens as they land. When generation finishes, the server sends a [DONE] marker and closes the connection.
pitfalls: SSE doesn't handle server-to-client backpressure; if your client can't keep up, the browser buffer fills and packets drop.
Reconnection logic isn't automatic—you must implement exponential backoff and track event IDs to resume mid-stream.
Some proxies or firewalls terminate long-lived HTTP connections after ~30 seconds; check your infrastructure.
JSON parsing errors on malformed event data will silently fail unless you add explicit error handlers.
when_use: Use SSE when you need simple, low-latency server-to-client streaming (e.g., LLM tokens, live updates) and don't need bidirectional communication or binary data.
when_avoid: Avoid SSE when you need true bidirectional communication (use WebSockets), must support older browsers without polyfills, or need to stream binary data natively.

faq.q: How is SSE different from WebSockets?
faq.a: SSE is one-way (server → client only) and uses standard HTTP; WebSockets are bidirectional and use a separate protocol. SSE is simpler for streaming; use WebSockets only if you need client-to-server messages without polling.

faq.q: Do I need an SDK to use SSE streaming?
faq.a: No. Any HTTP client library (curl, fetch, httpx) can read SSE by reading lines from the response body. SDKs handle parsing and error recovery, but aren't required for basic streaming.

faq.q: What happens if the SSE connection drops mid-stream?
faq.a: The connection closes and no more data arrives. You must detect the drop and reconnect manually. Use the id field in SSE events and the Last-Event-ID header to resume from the last received event.

faq.q: Can SSE pass through proxies and firewalls?
faq.a: Usually yes, because it's plain HTTP. However, some corporate proxies timeout long-lived connections after 30–60 seconds. If streaming cuts out unexpectedly, check your infrastructure's idle-connection policy.

faq.q: Is SSE the right choice for my chatbot?
faq.a: Yes, if you're streaming text tokens from an LLM. SSE handles this use case well. If users also need to upload files or send binary data, consider combining SSE (for streaming) with a separate upload channel.
