title: Product Quantization (PQ)
tldr: Vector compression that splits vectors into chunks, quantizes each independently, shrinking index size 10–100x.
plain_english: Product Quantization (PQ) treats each vector like a phone number: instead of storing all digits, you store a few short codes that point to pre-computed lookup tables. Each code represents where that chunk of the vector lives in a small 256-item vocabulary. At search time, you compare your query's codes against the lookup tables to approximate distances without ever reconstructing the full vector.
how_it_works: PQ splits a D-dimensional vector into M consecutive sub-vectors. For each sub-vector position, k-means clustering across your entire corpus creates a codebook of 256 centroids. You then encode each vector as M bytes (one byte per sub-vector, indexing into its 256-centroid codebook). At query time, you compute a lookup table of distances between your query's M sub-vectors and each codebook, then sum the lookups to approximate the full-vector distance. Pairing PQ with IVF (Inverted File indexing) enables billion-scale search: IVF partitions the space coarsely, PQ handles fine-grained distances within each partition.
why_it_matters: Storing raw vectors for 1 billion items at 1536 dimensions (float32) needs ~6 TB of RAM. PQ reduces that to 60–600 GB—a 10–100x saving—while keeping recall above 95% for most retrieval tasks. This compression is how production vector databases stay economical at scale.
example: A search engine indexing 2 billion web pages needs dense embeddings for semantic ranking. Storing raw 384-dim vectors costs 3 TB; PQ with M=8 sub-vectors shrinks it to 16 GB. The system runs k-means once offline on a sample, then encodes each page as 8 bytes. At query time, it uses IVF to narrow candidates to 10k pages, then applies PQ lookup tables to rank them, achieving 93% recall in <50ms.
pitfalls: Codebook training on biased data skews quantization: rare query directions get poor centroid coverage. Always sample training data uniformly.

M (number of sub-vectors) trades off compression vs. recall: M=2 compresses 64x but loses 10–15% recall; M=16 loses only 2% but compresses just 4x. Choose M via offline validation on your actual queries.
PQ breaks if vectors are not normalized or have wildly different scales; sub-vectors with high variance overshadow others. Standardize before training codebooks.
Retraining codebooks is expensive: changing M or recomputing k-means on new data requires re-encoding the entire index. Plan for periodic (monthly or quarterly) retraining, not per-query updates.
when_use: Use PQ when you need to index 100M+ vectors on commodity hardware, can afford offline codebook training, and tolerate 2–5% recall loss for 10–100x memory savings.
when_avoid: Avoid PQ if you need <1% recall loss, query patterns are adversarial (far from training distribution), or vectors change rapidly enough that codebook staleness is a problem.

faq.q: How does Product Quantization differ from other quantization methods?
faq.a: PQ divides vectors into independent sub-vectors and quantizes each separately, preserving relative distances within sub-vector groups. Scalar quantization quantizes each dimension individually (worse) and binary quantization rounds to bits (more aggressive but more lossy). PQ offers a middle ground: ~10x compression with acceptable recall.

faq.q: What is IVF-PQ and why combine them?
faq.a: IVF (Inverted File) partitions vectors into clusters; PQ quantizes within each cluster. IVF cuts search space 1000x (coarse), PQ handles fine-grained ranking (fine), so together they scale to billions of vectors while staying fast.

faq.q: Can you update a Product Quantization index without retraining?
faq.a: Adding new vectors is fast: encode them with the existing codebooks in O(M) time. But retraining codebooks requires re-encoding all vectors. FAISS and other libraries support incremental clustering for updates, but full retraining every few months is still common practice.

faq.q: Does Product Quantization work with asymmetric search?
faq.a: Yes. Asymmetric PQ (APQ) encodes database vectors tightly but computes query distances in higher precision, trading slightly slower search for better recall. Most production systems use this variant.

faq.q: What recall drop should I expect with Product Quantization?
faq.a: Typical IVF-PQ systems achieve 92–98% recall of the exhaustive top-100 results, depending on M and data dimensionality. Validate on your own queries offline before deploying.

meta_title: Product Quantization (PQ) — Definition, How It Works & When to Use | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Vector compression that splits vectors into chunks, quantizes each independently, shrinking index size 10–100x.

schema_jsonld.name: Product Quantization (PQ)
schema_jsonld.description: Vector compression that splits vectors into chunks, quantizes each independently, shrinking index size 10–100x.

title: Query Rewriting
tldr: Transform user questions into retriever-friendly queries before lookup to improve RAG hit rates.
plain_english: Users phrase questions in conversational language—'What did we ship last quarter?'—but your retrieval system searches a corpus indexed differently. Query rewriting runs the user's question through an LLM to rephrase it in ways your retriever can actually find. Think of it like translating someone's casual question into library catalog keywords.
how_it_works: You intercept the user's raw query before it hits your retriever. An LLM reformulates it into one or more candidate queries—single rewrites, multiple variants, or generalizations. Variants include: single-rewrite (one improved query), multi-query (generate 3–5 versions), step-back prompting (abstract first, then retrieve), and conversational rewriting (inject prior context). Each variant or the best one goes to your retriever. Higher relevance matches improve downstream ranking and final answers.
why_it_matters: Query wording is often the bottleneck in RAG retrieval quality—mismatched vocabulary costs you relevant documents before ranking even starts. In multi-turn chat, rewriting is frequently the single biggest lever you can pull to fix retrieval failures without reindexing your corpus or tuning your embeddings.
example: A support team uses RAG over internal docs. A user asks 'How do I reset my password?' but your corpus uses 'account credential recovery' and 'authentication reset.' The rewriter detects the intent mismatch and generates 'password reset procedure', 'account access recovery', and 'login credential change'. Multi-query retrieval now finds all three relevant docs instead of zero.
pitfalls: Rewriting adds latency (extra LLM call); measure end-to-end cost vs. retrieval lift before shipping.
Multiple rewrites can retrieve contradictory contexts; implement a reranker to pick the best result.
Rewriter hallucination: it may add specifics ('Q4 2025') that don't exist in the corpus, narrowing retrieval.
Conversational rewriting needs memory management; long chat histories inflate tokens and can drift query intent.
when_use: Use query rewriting when your retriever misses relevant docs due to vocabulary mismatch, you have multi-turn chat where context shifts are common, or you want to improve hit rate without reindexing.
when_avoid: Avoid it if your retriever already has 80%+ recall on a held-out test set, latency is critical (single-digit milliseconds), or your corpus is tiny enough that exhaustive search is already fast.

faq.q: Is query rewriting the same as prompt engineering?
faq.a: No. Prompt engineering shapes instructions to the LLM itself; query rewriting transforms the user's input before retrieval. Query rewriting is a retrieval-specific tactic.

faq.q: How many rewrites should I generate?
faq.a: Start with 3–5 variants. Diminishing returns kick in after 5; measure retrieval recall and latency cost to find your sweet spot.

faq.q: Does query rewriting work with hybrid search?
faq.a: Yes. You can rewrite once and route to both dense and sparse (BM25) retrievers, or rewrite separately for each modality if lexical and semantic intent differ.

faq.q: What's the difference between query rewriting and step-back prompting?
faq.a: Query rewriting keeps the same query scope. Step-back prompting abstracts the query first ('facts about X') then retrieves, trading specificity for broader coverage.

faq.q: Can I rewrite without adding latency?
faq.a: Partially. Batch-rewrite user queries during off-peak ingestion if you can predict common question patterns, or cache rewrites for common queries.

meta_title: Query Rewriting — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Transform user questions into retriever-friendly queries before lookup to improve RAG hit rates.

schema_jsonld.name: Query Rewriting
schema_jsonld.description: Transform user questions into retriever-friendly queries before lookup to improve RAG hit rates.

title: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
tldr: Fetch relevant documents first, then feed them to the LLM so it answers based on your data.
plain_english: An LLM trained on internet text doesn't know your private documents or today's news. RAG solves this by searching your document store for chunks that match the user's question, then stuffing those chunks into the context window alongside the prompt. The LLM reads both the question and the retrieved facts, then answers based on what it actually has.
how_it_works: You store your documents as text chunks in a vector database (typically converted to embeddings first). When a user asks a question, you embed that question using the same method, search the vector database for the most similar chunks, and append those chunks to the prompt before sending it to the LLM. The LLM's context window now contains both the question and the retrieved context, so it can cite your data or synthesize answers from it. The retrieval step happens outside the LLM—you control what documents are searchable.
why_it_matters: RAG is the standard way to build production AI apps that work with proprietary data without retraining the model. It powers customer support bots, internal Q&A tools, and document chat interfaces. It's faster and cheaper than fine-tuning, keeps data fresh without model updates, and lets you see which documents the model cited.
example: A support team uploads 500 product documentation PDFs into a vector database. When a customer asks 'Can I return items after 30 days?', the system retrieves the 3 most relevant PDF chunks about returns, passes them to an LLM, and generates an answer grounded in your actual policy—not the LLM's training data.
pitfalls: Retrieving irrelevant chunks wastes context window space and causes the LLM to ignore good information (lost-in-the-middle problem); validate your retrieval quality separately.
Embedding mismatches—if your corpus uses embeddings trained on code but you query with natural-language embeddings, retrieval fails silently.
Over-relying on chunk size; too-small chunks lose context, too-large chunks dilute signal and waste tokens.
Forgetting that RAG doesn't fix hallucination—if no relevant chunk exists, the LLM still makes things up.
when_use: Use RAG when your data changes frequently, is proprietary or large, or when you need the LLM to cite sources.
when_avoid: Avoid RAG if your task requires the LLM to reason across entire documents at once or if your corpus is so small that fine-tuning a base model is faster.

faq.q: What's the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?
faq.a: RAG retrieves external documents at query time and feeds them into the LLM's context; fine-tuning bakes knowledge into model weights during training. RAG is faster to update (add new docs anytime), fine-tuning is slower but lets the model learn deeper patterns and use fewer tokens.

faq.q: Does RAG work with proprietary data?
faq.a: Yes. Your documents never leave your infrastructure—they stay in your vector database. Only the user's question goes to the LLM (unless you're using a managed service), so proprietary text remains internal.

faq.q: How do I know if my RAG system retrieved the right information?
faq.a: Test retrieval quality separately from LLM quality. Use metrics like NDCG or mean reciprocal rank on a labeled dataset of queries and relevant documents. Common tools: RAGAS, LangSmith, or manual spot-checks on sample queries.

faq.q: Can RAG handle long documents?
faq.a: RAG handles any document length by breaking it into chunks and retrieving only the most relevant ones. If you need reasoning across the entire document, consider hybrid approaches like small-to-big retrieval or re-ranking retrieved chunks.

faq.q: What's the latency cost of RAG?
faq.a: Retrieval typically adds 50–500ms depending on your vector database size and indexing method (HNSW is faster than IVF). For real-time chat, this is usually acceptable; for ultra-low-latency systems, cache or pre-compute common queries.

meta_title: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) — Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Fetch relevant documents first, then feed them to the LLM so it answers based on your data.

schema_jsonld.name: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
schema_jsonld.description: Fetch relevant documents first, then feed them to the LLM so it answers based on your data.

title: RAG Fusion
tldr: RAG pattern that generates multiple query variants, retrieves results for each, and merges them using reciprocal rank fusion.
plain_english: Instead of running a single search query, RAG Fusion asks the LLM to rephrase your question 3–5 different ways. You search the knowledge base for all variants at once, then blend the results so documents appearing in multiple retrieved sets rank higher. It's like asking five different librarians the same question and combining their answer lists—you catch documents the single phrasing would miss.
how_it_works: The pattern operates in three stages: (1) an LLM generates 3–5 semantic variants of the user's query, (2) you run retrieval (BM25, embedding, or hybrid) for each variant independently, fetching the top-k documents per variant, (3) reciprocal rank fusion combines the ranked lists by assigning each document a score based on how high it ranked across all variants. Higher scores bubble documents that appear consistently in multiple result sets. The final merged set becomes your RAG context.
why_it_matters: Single-query retrieval misses relevant documents when phrasing doesn't match your index well. RAG Fusion improves recall—finding more of the documents you actually need—while keeping cost predictable (typically 3–5× the retrieval call count). It works especially well for ambiguous, exploratory, or domain-specific queries where synonym coverage matters.
example: A customer support team uses RAG Fusion on a product manual. User asks 'How do I reset the system?' The LLM generates variants: 'factory reset procedure', 'restore to defaults', 'clear all settings'. Retrieval runs three times. A document about 'hard reset' appears in results for variants 1 and 3, so it scores high enough to reach the final context. A single-query search might have buried it below less relevant matches.
pitfalls: Retrieving more documents per variant (k=20 vs k=5) increases cost linearly without guaranteed recall gains; benchmark your k before deployment.
Poor variant generation (e.g., LLM producing near-duplicate phrasings) wastes compute; use explicit prompting or constrain the LLM's temperature.
RRF weights all variants equally; if one is a much better paraphrase, it still counts the same—consider learned weighting instead.
In small/specialized indexes, variants may retrieve overlapping documents, yielding no recall benefit; test on your actual corpus first.
when_use: Use RAG Fusion when dealing with ambiguous queries, domain-specific terminology, sparse indexes, or when single-query retrieval recall is measurably low and cost for 3–5 extra retrievals is acceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid RAG Fusion when latency is critical (each variant adds retrieval latency), when your index is dense and single-query recall is already >90%, or when budget precludes the N× retrieval cost.

faq.q: Is RAG Fusion the same as multi-query retrieval?
faq.a: RAG Fusion is one implementation of multi-query retrieval. Multi-query retrieval is the broader idea of generating multiple phrasings; RAG Fusion specifically combines it with reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) for merging. Other patterns merge differently (e.g., simple dedup or learned weights).

faq.q: How much does RAG Fusion cost compared to normal RAG?
faq.a: RAG Fusion typically costs 3–5× more in retrieval calls because you run retrieve once per variant. If you use an embedding model it's negligible; if you're calling an API per retrieve, it adds up. Measure latency and cost on your workload before committing.

faq.q: Can I use RAG Fusion with BM25 or only embeddings?
faq.a: RAG Fusion works with any retrieval method—BM25, dense embeddings, sparse retrieval (SPLADE), or hybrid search. The variant generation and RRF merge are retrieval-agnostic. Hybrid retrieval often yields the best results.

faq.q: What happens if my LLM generates bad query variants?
faq.a: Poor variants hurt recall because you miss relevant documents. Use explicit prompting (e.g., 'Generate 5 alternative wordings that change syntax but preserve meaning') or sample-based quality checks. Temperature control helps reduce repetitive variants.

faq.q: Do I need reciprocal rank fusion or can I just merge lists differently?
faq.a: RRF is one merge strategy; others include simple dedup, concatenation with reranking, or learned weighted fusion. RRF is simple and interpretable but not always optimal. Compare against reranking-based fusion on your specific corpus.

meta_title: RAG Fusion — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: RAG Fusion

title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
tldr: Merge ranked lists from multiple retrievers by summing reciprocal ranks, ignoring score scales.
plain_english: RRF is a simple method for combining search results from two or more retrievers—say, keyword search and semantic search—without caring about their score ranges. Instead of trying to normalize scores that might be on completely different scales, RRF just looks at position: if an item ranks first in one retriever and third in another, RRF rewards it accordingly. It's like tallying votes: whoever appears high in the most lists wins.
how_it_works: For each document, RRF computes sum(1/(k+rank_i)) across all retrievers, where k is typically 60 and rank_i is the document's position in retriever i. Documents that don't appear in a retriever are skipped. Results are then ranked by total RRF score. This approach ignores raw scores entirely, so BM25 (sparse) and cosine similarity (dense) can be mixed without normalization tricks. Documents missing from some lists simply accumulate fewer votes.
why_it_matters: RRF is the default fusion method for hybrid search in RAG systems because it's parameter-free, interpretable, and often matches learning-to-rank performance at a fraction of the complexity. It solves the immediate problem of combining heterogeneous retrievers without tuning or training.
example: A RAG pipeline retrieving documents for legal research uses BM25 (exact keyword matches) and a dense embedding model (semantic relevance). Document A ranks #1 in BM25 but #50 in embedding search; Document B ranks #20 in BM25 and #2 in embedding. RRF assigns A a score of 1/(60+1) + 1/(60+50) ≈ 0.0098 and B a score of 1/(60+20) + 1/(60+2) ≈ 0.0259. B rises to the top despite weak keyword match because it's semantically strong.
pitfalls: Setting k too low (e.g., 1) gives extreme weight to rank 1 and near-zero weight to ranks beyond 10; k=60 is empirically robust but adjust if your retriever lists are very short.
Assuming RRF works with scores from retrievers that have internal ties or bucket rankings; it requires strict ordinal ranks, not score clusters.
Not handling missing documents consistently—if one retriever returns 10 results and another returns 100, the smaller list's documents get unfair advantage in RRF voting.
Treating RRF as a final solution rather than an intermediate step; reranking the RRF output with a cross-encoder often yields further gains.
when_use: Use RRF when combining sparse and dense retrieval without labeled ranking data, or when retrievers produce incompatible score scales (e.g., BM25 and cosine similarity, or multiple API providers).
when_avoid: Avoid RRF if you have ground-truth relevance labels for training a learning-to-rank model, or if all retrievers produce calibrated, comparable scores (use weighted score fusion instead).

faq.q: What does RRF stand for?
faq.a: Reciprocal Rank Fusion. It sums reciprocal ranks (1/rank) across multiple result lists to create a unified ranking.

faq.q: How is RRF different from averaging scores?
faq.a: RRF ignores the magnitude of scores and only looks at position in each list, so it doesn't require score normalization. Averaging raw scores fails if one retriever outputs 0–1 and another outputs 0–100.

faq.q: What value should k be?
faq.a: k=60 is the original and widely-used default. It smooths the contribution of lower-ranked items. For very short result lists, k=10–20 may work better; for very long lists, k=100+ is sometimes used.

faq.q: Does RRF require the same number of results from each retriever?
faq.a: No. If one retriever returns 5 results and another returns 100, RRF still works—items not in a retriever's list simply don't vote in that column.

faq.q: Can I use RRF with more than two retrievers?
faq.a: Yes. RRF sums over all retrievers, so it scales to three, four, or more sources. Each missing retriever just contributes zero to that document's vote.

meta_title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: Reranking
tldr: A second retrieval pass using a more accurate model to re-score top candidates from a fast first pass.
plain_english: Reranking is like having two rounds of screening. First, you use a cheap method (embedding similarity) to grab your top 50 candidates quickly. Then, you bring in a smarter inspector (a cross-encoder) to look at each candidate more carefully alongside the original query, and reorder them. It catches nuance the first pass missed.
how_it_works: Your embedding model retrieves top-K results in milliseconds via approximate nearest neighbor search. A reranker then takes these candidates and processes each as a query–document pair, assigning new scores based on deeper semantic matching. You keep the top-M reranked results and discard the rest. This two-stage pipeline trades latency (add 10–50ms) for recall: you keep the speed of embeddings for breadth, but add accuracy where it matters most. Standard flow: retrieve top 50 in 5ms via BM25 or embeddings, rerank to top 10 in 20ms using BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3 or Cohere Rerank.
why_it_matters: If your RAG retrieves the right context 80% of the time but misses the top-ranked answer, reranking often closes that gap with minimal latency overhead. It directly improves recall@K metrics—often 10–25 percentage points—which translates to more accurate citations and fewer hallucinations. For user-facing applications, moving the right answer from position 5 to position 1 is the difference between trust and frustration.
example: A customer support team uses a vector database to find relevant past tickets. Embedding search returns 50 candidates in 3ms, but the most similar ticket often ranks 8th because the embedding model doesn't account for subtle wording differences. Adding BGE Reranker costs 15ms and moves the correct answer to position 1 in 90% of cases, cutting average resolution time from 10 minutes to 3 minutes.
pitfalls: Rerankers require a full forward pass per query–candidate pair; you cannot precompute scores. Each query doubles or triples latency if you rerank carelessly.
Larger rerankers don't always win. BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3 (568M params) often beats 3B-param models on domain-specific data; measure on your actual distribution.
Reranking only helps if your top-K contains the right answer. If embeddings drop the answer outside the top 50, reranking cannot recover it.
Cross-encoders are sensitive to input order and formatting; inconsistent chunking or unusual query phrasing can degrade ranking quality.
when_use: Use reranking when you need higher recall@K, latency budget allows 20–100ms per query, or your embedding model struggles with semantic nuance in your domain.
when_avoid: Avoid reranking if latency is under 10ms SLA, your embedding model already ranks correctly, or you must handle millions of candidates per second.

faq.q: What is the difference between a reranker and an embedding model?
faq.a: Embedding models encode text independently into fixed vectors; rerankers see query and document together and output a relevance score. Embeddings can be precomputed; rerankers cannot.

faq.q: How much does reranking slow down retrieval?
faq.a: Typically 10–50ms per query depending on model size and candidate count. BM25 + rerank is often faster than embedding-only retrieval while ranking better.

faq.q: Can I use reranking without embeddings?
faq.a: Yes. BM25 (sparse retrieval) + reranker is a common hybrid approach. Embeddings are one fast first-pass option; BM25, keyword search, or graph traversal also work.

faq.q: Does reranking help with non-English queries?
faq.a: Depends on the reranker. Multilingual models like BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3 or Cohere Rerank support 100+ languages; monolingual rerankers do not.

faq.q: How many candidates should I rerank?
faq.a: Typically 20–100. Below 20, you lose benefit; above 100, latency grows linearly. Start with top 50 and measure recall vs. latency on your data.

meta_title: Reranking — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: Reranking

title: Retrieval (IR)
tldr: Finding relevant documents from a corpus to answer a query—the first step in RAG pipelines.
plain_english: Retrieval is the process of scoring and ranking documents in your corpus to find the ones most likely to help answer a user's question. Think of it like a library search: you hand the librarian a question, they filter the stacks, and return the top shelves. In RAG systems, a poor retriever breaks everything downstream—no generator can fix garbage input.
how_it_works: A retriever takes a query and a corpus of documents, then scores each document by relevance. Sparse retrievers (BM25, TF-IDF) match keywords and term frequencies. Dense retrievers embed both query and documents as vectors, then compute similarity. Hybrid systems combine both. The retriever returns the top-k ranked results. Evaluation uses recall@k (did the right answer appear?), MRR (rank position), or nDCG (ranking quality). Retrieval quality is the ceiling on RAG quality—a great language model cannot recover from poor retrieval choices.
why_it_matters: Most RAG failures trace to retrieval, not generation. Your language model can only work with documents it receives. Investing time in retrieval quality (better embeddings, reranking, hybrid search) yields faster returns than optimizing the generator. Getting retrieval right is often the single highest-leverage RAG engineering decision.
example: A customer support team uses RAG to answer product questions. They embed 50,000 support articles as vectors. When a user asks 'How do I reset my password?', the retriever searches for semantically similar docs—finding the password-reset guide in top-3 results. A weak retriever might return FAQs about billing instead, causing the generator to hallucinate or refuse. The team adds reranking to their pipeline and watch support ticket volume drop 20%.
pitfalls: Treating retrieval as a solved problem—your embedding model, chunk size, and corpus structure all affect quality; they need tuning.
Ignoring the 'lost in the middle' effect—relevant context sandwiched between irrelevant docs lowers generation quality; rerank or reorder results.
Using only sparse retrieval (BM25) for semantic queries; keywords miss paraphrases and domain-specific language.
Assuming high recall@k means good RAG performance; retrieving 100 documents and ranking the right answer 50th is useless if your generator only reads top-5.
when_use: Use retrieval when you have a corpus larger than context window, need latency under 1 second, or want to ground generation in authoritative documents.
when_avoid: Avoid RAG retrieval if your corpus is tiny (fits in context), your query is generic (needs no grounding), or latency is not a constraint—simple in-context learning may suffice.

faq.q: What's the difference between sparse and dense retrieval?
faq.a: Sparse (BM25, TF-IDF) matches keywords and term frequencies; it's fast and works for exact-match queries but misses paraphrases. Dense (embedding-based) maps documents to vectors and finds semantic similarity; it catches intent better but is slower and requires embedding models. Most production systems use hybrid to get both.

faq.q: How do I evaluate if my retriever is good?
faq.a: Use recall@k (did the correct document appear in top-k?), MRR (mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant result), or nDCG (ranking quality). Run a small eval set of queries with known relevant documents, then measure these metrics. Aim for recall@5 >0.8 for most RAG tasks.

faq.q: Can a good language model fix poor retrieval?
faq.a: No. If the retriever returns irrelevant documents, the generator can only hallucinate or refuse. Retrieval sets the ceiling on RAG quality. Focus retrieval optimization first, then tune the generator.

faq.q: What chunk size should I use for retrieval?
faq.a: There is no universal answer; it depends on your use case and embedding model. Smaller chunks (100 tokens) increase retrieval granularity but may lose context. Larger chunks (500+ tokens) preserve context but dilute relevance signals. Start with 256–512 tokens and measure recall@k on your eval set.

faq.q: How does reranking improve retrieval?
faq.a: Reranking runs a second-stage model (often a cross-encoder) that re-scores the top-k results from the initial retriever. It's slower but more accurate; use it when the first retriever is noisy or when you can afford the latency.

title: Self-Query Retrieval
tldr: LLM extracts semantic queries and metadata filters from natural language, applying structured constraints before vector search.
plain_english: Instead of treating every search as a pure semantic similarity problem, self-query retrieval lets the LLM read your question and decide which structured filters (dates, categories, authors) to apply first. Think of it like telling a librarian "find action movies from the 90s over 2 hours long"—the librarian understands both what you mean by "action movie" and the hard constraints (decade, runtime). The vector database then only searches within matching documents, making results far more precise.
how_it_works: You send a natural-language query to the self-query retriever. The LLM parses it into two outputs: (1) a semantic query describing the content you want (e.g., "action movie"), and (2) a set of structured metadata filters (e.g., {decade: 1990s, runtime_min: 120}). These filters are applied at the vector database layer as a pre-filter, reducing the candidate set before similarity search runs. Only documents matching both the filters and semantic relevance are returned. LangChain and LlamaIndex implement this by defining a schema for your metadata fields, then using the LLM to populate it.
why_it_matters: Metadata filters alone miss semantic intent; pure vector search ignores hard constraints. Self-query retrieval bridges both, letting you build RAG systems that handle date ranges, categories, and other structured constraints without manual query engineering. This is essential when your corpus has rich metadata and users expect precise, constrained results.
example: A fintech documentation system receives: "Show me security audit reports from 2023 or later, at least 50 pages." The self-query retriever extracts semantic query = "security audit" and filters = {year_min: 2023, pages_min: 50}. The vector DB filters to 2023+ docs ≥50 pages, then ranks by relevance. Without self-query, you'd either get off-topic results from 2022 or miss short summaries that mattered, or require users to manually specify filters.
pitfalls: Metadata schema mismatches: If your schema doesn't cover what users actually filter by (e.g., author names), the LLM can't extract them; define schema comprehensively.
Over-filtering: Strict metadata constraints can eliminate all results if the corpus doesn't match. Use optional filters or fallback retrieval if the first pass returns nothing.
LLM parsing errors: The LLM may misinterpret edge cases (e.g., "recent" vs. "2020–2023"); validate extracted filters before querying the vector DB.
Latency overhead: Each query now requires an LLM call to parse; batch or cache common patterns if latency is critical.
when_use: Use self-query retrieval when your corpus has structured metadata (dates, categories, authors) that users naturally reference, and you want results constrained by those fields without manual query engineering.
when_avoid: Avoid when metadata is sparse or unstructured, when users rarely filter by specific fields, or when latency is more critical than precision.

faq.q: What's the difference between self-query retrieval and just metadata filtering?
faq.a: Metadata filtering requires users or engineers to manually specify constraints; self-query retrieval extracts constraints from natural language via the LLM, combining semantic understanding with structured filtering automatically.

faq.q: Does self-query retrieval work with any vector database?
faq.a: Yes, as long as the vector DB supports metadata filtering. LangChain and LlamaIndex abstract over FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, and others; the self-query layer handles LLM parsing, and the framework handles the underlying DB call.

faq.q: What happens if the LLM extracts a filter that doesn't match any documents?
faq.a: You get zero results. Handle this with fallback logic: re-run without strict filters, loosen date ranges, or ask the user to clarify. Some frameworks support optional filters to avoid complete misses.

faq.q: Is self-query retrieval slower than regular vector search?
faq.a: Yes—there's an extra LLM call to parse the query. If latency matters, cache common patterns, batch queries, or use faster LLMs. The speed/precision tradeoff is usually worth it for recall-critical applications.

faq.q: Can I use self-query retrieval without an LLM?
faq.a: No, the whole pattern depends on the LLM to parse natural language into structured filters. For rule-based filtering, use traditional metadata-filtering or hybrid search without the self-query layer.

title: Semantic Caching
tldr: Cache LLM responses by semantic similarity, not exact text match, to reuse answers for similar queries.
plain_english: Instead of caching only when someone asks the exact same question twice, semantic caching recognizes when two questions mean roughly the same thing and returns the cached answer from the first one. You embed each query, store it with its response, and when a new query arrives, you check if it's similar enough to something you've already answered. If yes, you skip the LLM call entirely.
how_it_works: When a query arrives, you embed it and search a vector store of past (embedding, response) pairs using a similarity metric like cosine distance. If the best match scores above a threshold (typically 0.8–0.95), you return the cached response immediately. If not, you run the query through your LLM, embed and store the new pair, and return the live response. The threshold controls the trade-off: higher values reduce false hits but lower your cache rate; lower values increase hits but risk returning wrong answers to subtly different questions.
why_it_matters: Semantic caching cuts LLM costs and latency for high-volume assistants—often achieving 20–40% cache hit rates where exact-match caching would fail. For FAQ-heavy or repetitive workloads, this is the single highest-leverage optimization before scaling infrastructure. Every cache hit saves both API cost and user-facing latency.
example: A customer support chatbot receives 'How do I reset my password?' and 'What's the process to reset my password?' within minutes of each other. The first query hits the LLM and gets cached. The second query embeds to nearly identical vectors, triggers a cache hit above threshold, and returns the same response without a new API call. Over a day, dozens of paraphrased password-reset questions all hit that one cached response, reducing token spend from 1,000+ tokens to near zero.
pitfalls: High vector similarity does not mean semantically identical intent—'How do I delete my account?' and 'How do I close my account?' may embed close together but require different workflows.
Threshold tuning is non-trivial; too low and you return incorrect answers, too high and you miss valid cache hits and waste compute.
Stale cached responses can persist if the underlying knowledge or policy changes; you need invalidation or TTL logic.
Embedding overhead can negate cache savings if you embed every query and use a slow embedding model—use a fast, in-process embedding or cached embeddings.
when_use: Use semantic caching when you have high query volume, many paraphrased questions, and can tolerate slight answer variance across semantically similar queries.
when_avoid: Avoid when answers must be deterministic across identical-intent queries, when embedding latency exceeds LLM cost savings, or when your queries are highly specific and rarely repeated.

faq.q: How is semantic caching different from prompt caching?
faq.a: Prompt caching (e.g., Claude's prefix caching) reuses KV-cache tokens for identical prefixes in a single request or session. Semantic caching matches across different requests by meaning, storing full responses in a separate cache layer. They're complementary: use prompt caching within a conversation, semantic caching across users.

faq.q: What embedding model should I use for semantic caching?
faq.a: Use a small, fast embedding model (e.g., bge-small, all-MiniLM-L6-v2) that runs locally or via a cached service. Avoid large models; the embedding latency will outweigh cache savings. Match your embedding to your domain if possible (e.g., domain-specific MTEB leaders).

faq.q: Can semantic caching work with multi-turn conversations?
faq.a: Yes, but carefully. Cache individual Q&A pairs separately from multi-turn context. A new question in a conversation can hit a cached response from a standalone query if they're semantically close. Store the full conversation context if you need turn-aware behavior.

faq.q: What threshold similarity should I set?
faq.a: Start at 0.85–0.90 and tune based on your false-positive rate (incorrect cache hits). For safety-critical queries, use 0.95+. For FAQ bots, 0.80–0.85 is often aggressive but acceptable. Monitor hit rate vs. response quality continuously.

faq.q: Does semantic caching work for real-time or time-sensitive queries?
faq.a: Not reliably. If the answer changes frequently (stock prices, current weather), cached responses become stale. Add a TTL (time-to-live) or skip caching for time-sensitive queries based on metadata or query intent classification.

meta_title: Semantic Caching — Definition & Implementation Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: Sentence Window Retrieval
tldr: Index sentences individually but retrieve surrounding context windows to balance precision with interpretability.
plain_english: You break a document into sentences and embed each one separately, like filing index cards. But when you retrieve a matching sentence, you return it along with a few sentences before and after—like pulling the whole paragraph instead of just one card. This keeps answers precise while giving the LLM enough neighboring text to understand the context.
how_it_works: Split your source into sentences and embed each as its own vector. Store metadata pointing to a "window"—typically 3–5 sentences before and after each indexed sentence. At query time, find the top-k matching sentences by embedding similarity, then return each match along with its full window. The LLM receives both the targeted answer and surrounding context in one chunk.
why_it_matters: Sentence-level indexing catches specific facts that paragraph chunking misses, reducing false negatives. Windows prevent the LLM from receiving orphaned sentences that lack context for interpretation. This trade-off often outperforms fixed-size chunks on precision-critical tasks like QA over dense legal or technical documents.
example: A financial compliance team indexing loan agreements embeds each sentence (e.g., "Interest rates adjust quarterly.") but retrieves a 4-sentence window around matches. When a query asks about rate adjustments, the LLM gets the target sentence plus the preceding terms and following conditions, enabling accurate summaries without hallucination.
pitfalls: Windows that overlap heavily across sentences create duplicate context in batches, inflating token counts without proportional quality gain.
Sentence boundaries from naive splitting (periods, newlines) fail on abbreviations, decimals, and multi-line tables, corrupting windows.
Setting window size fixed across all documents ignores domain variation—legal docs need wider context than customer reviews.
Retrieving full windows for every match can exceed context length if you return many results; combine with reranking or top-k limits.
when_use: Use when documents are dense, logically structured by sentence (e.g., technical specs, legal text, research papers), and your task requires high recall on specific facts without summarization.
when_avoid: Avoid when documents lack clear sentence structure (code, tables, conversational text), your context window is tight, or you need multi-document synthesis where surrounding context adds noise.

faq.q: How is sentence window retrieval different from paragraph chunking?
faq.a: Paragraph chunking indexes fixed-size blocks. Sentence windows index individual sentences but retrieve wider context, letting you tune indexing precision independently of LLM context size. This often improves recall on narrow facts while reducing irrelevant surrounding text compared to large paragraphs.

faq.q: What window size should I use?
faq.a: Start with 2–3 sentences before and after (total 5–7). Denser, more technical documents benefit from wider windows (4–5). Test on your domain; wider windows reduce hallucination but increase token cost and noise if context is sparse.

faq.q: Does sentence window retrieval work with long documents?
faq.a: Yes, it handles length well—you index sentences individually so scale is linear. But very long documents risk windows that still miss important context if the answer depends on information far away; pair with reranking or document-level filtering for reliability.

faq.q: Can I combine sentence window retrieval with other RAG techniques?
faq.a: Yes. Pair it with reranking to filter noisy windows, semantic caching to avoid redundant retrievals, or graph-based retrieval to link related sentences across documents. Sentence-window indexing is a building block, not a complete solution.

faq.q: What happens if sentences don't split cleanly?
faq.a: Use a tokenizer-aware splitter (spaCy, NLTK) instead of naive regex. For tables, code, or structured data, preprocess into pseudo-sentences or fall back to fixed-size chunks. Garbage splits corrupt windows and hurt retrieval quality.

meta_title: Sentence Window Retrieval — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: Sentence Window Retrieval

title: Small-to-Big Retrieval
tldr: Embed small chunks for precision, return progressively larger context based on token budget.
plain_english: You embed sentences individually so retrieval finds the exact relevant piece. But instead of giving the LLM only that sentence, you return the sentence plus its paragraph, or the full document if tokens allow. Think of it like zooming out on a map: you pinpoint the location at street level, then expand the view to the neighborhood or city as needed.
how_it_works: Build a hierarchy: sentences point to paragraphs, paragraphs point to documents. Embed only the sentences. At retrieval time, match the query against sentence embeddings to find the best candidate. Then return that sentence plus its parent paragraph. If the LLM has remaining token budget, escalate to the full document. LlamaIndex's auto-merging retriever implements this by recursively merging child nodes into parent nodes until hitting the token limit.
why_it_matters: Mixed-density documents waste tokens or lose precision. Small-to-big gives you both: tight retrieval (sentence-level matching) without starving the LLM of context (paragraph or document fallback). This reduces hallucination from incomplete information and improves latency for token-constrained scenarios.
example: A technical documentation site has 50-page API guides with dense reference sections and loose narrative explanations. Small-to-big embeds each sentence, so a query about "rate limiting" finds the exact sentence in the reference section. Retrieval returns that sentence plus its surrounding paragraph. If budget allows, it adds the full section. The LLM gets surgical precision without re-reading the whole guide.
pitfalls: Forgetting to set strict token budgets per tier; you may still exceed limits by always escalating to full documents.
Embedding all granularities wastes storage; only embed the smallest unit (sentence), store pointers to parents.
Using identical thresholds for sentence-to-paragraph and paragraph-to-document escalation; content density varies, so tune each independently.
Not measuring whether returned context actually improves answer quality; the extra context may introduce noise.
when_use: Use small-to-big when your documents have mixed density (dense reference sections, loose narrative), you have strict token budgets, or your retrieval quality suffers from too-small chunks.
when_avoid: Avoid when all documents are uniformly dense, your context window is very large, or your chunks are already semantically coherent at a single granularity.

faq.q: How is small-to-big retrieval different from parent-document retrieval?
faq.a: Parent-document retrieval embeds at one granularity (e.g., sentence) but always returns the parent (e.g., paragraph). Small-to-big embeds at the smallest granularity but escalates the returned context dynamically based on token budget, returning sentence → paragraph → document progressively.

faq.q: Do I need to embed sentences, paragraphs, and documents separately?
faq.a: No. Embed only the smallest unit (sentence). Store hierarchical pointers to parents. At retrieval time, match against the smallest embeddings, then traverse upward to return larger context blocks.

faq.q: Can small-to-big work with variable-length documents?
faq.a: Yes. The token budget controls escalation, not document size. Short documents may return fully; long ones return sentence + paragraph. Tune per-use case.

faq.q: Does this increase retrieval latency?
faq.a: Minimally. Sentence-level embedding search is fast. Parent traversal is metadata lookup. The cost is storage for pointer hierarchies, not retrieval speed.

faq.q: What if my documents don't have natural paragraph breaks?
faq.a: Create synthetic paragraphs using fixed window size (e.g., 3–5 sentences). Small-to-big still works; quality depends on whether your synthetic grouping aligns with semantic boundaries.

meta_title: Small-to-Big Retrieval — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Embed small chunks for precision, return progressively larger context based on token budget.

schema_jsonld.name: Small-to-Big Retrieval

title: Sparse Retrieval
tldr: Token-based retrieval using BM25, TF-IDF, or learned sparse models like SPLADE that match exact keywords.
plain_english: Sparse retrieval treats documents and queries as lists of important words, then finds matches by counting overlapping terms. Unlike dense retrieval, which uses semantic meaning, sparse methods focus on exact keywords—like searching for "Python error" and finding documents with both words. BM25 is the most proven method and has beaten newer approaches on many real tasks.
how_it_works: Sparse retrievers represent queries and documents as high-dimensional vectors with mostly zeros, containing only non-zero values for present tokens. BM25 weighs term matches by frequency and inverse document frequency—common words count less, rare words more. Learned sparse models like SPLADE use an LLM to expand queries and reweight tokens, capturing more semantic signal while staying interpretable. At query time, the system matches on token overlap and ranks by accumulated weights.
why_it_matters: Exact-match precision matters for code, rare terms, and multi-word entities where semantic models fail. Production RAG systems pair sparse and dense retrieval: sparse catches what you typed, dense catches what you meant. Sparse remains interpretable—you can see which terms matched—while dense embeddings are black boxes.
example: A technical support RAG ingests error logs and docs. When a user queries 'OutOfMemoryError in TensorFlow', sparse retrieval immediately returns logs containing both 'OutOfMemoryError' and 'TensorFlow'. Dense-only retrieval might return conceptually similar memory issues in unrelated frameworks. Hybrid search uses both: sparse for precision on known errors, dense for conceptual matches.
pitfalls: Relying on sparse alone loses semantic nuance—'car broke down' and 'vehicle malfunction' have no token overlap.
Learned sparse models (SPLADE) require fine-tuning on your domain; off-the-shelf versions may overfit to training data.
BM25 struggles with synonyms and abbreviations without preprocessing; query expansion helps but adds latency.
Assuming tokenization is free—subword tokenization creates sub-term overlap that inflates false positives.
when_use: Use sparse retrieval when exact keywords matter (code, medical codes, legal clauses), when you need interpretability, or as one layer in a hybrid system alongside dense retrieval.
when_avoid: Avoid sparse-only when your domain has many synonyms or paraphrased concepts, or when rare terms are sparse in your corpus and you need semantic generalization.

faq.q: What's the difference between sparse and dense retrieval?
faq.a: Sparse retrieval matches exact keywords using BM25 or learned term weights. Dense retrieval embeds meaning into vectors and finds semantic similarity. Sparse is interpretable and fast for exact matches; dense catches paraphrases and synonyms.

faq.q: Is BM25 still competitive in 2024?
faq.a: Yes. BM25 remains the baseline on many benchmarks and often outperforms dense retrieval alone on keyword-heavy queries. Hybrid search combining BM25 + dense embedding beats either alone.

faq.q: What's SPLADE and when should I use it?
faq.a: SPLADE uses an LLM to expand terms and assign learned weights, bridging sparse and dense. Use it if you have domain-specific terminology or need better-than-BM25 recall without full dense-embedding cost.

faq.q: Can sparse retrieval handle synonyms?
faq.a: Not natively—'synonyms' and 'similar words' have zero token overlap. You need query expansion, semantic preprocessing, or a hybrid approach with dense embeddings to handle synonym variation.

faq.q: How fast is sparse retrieval?
faq.a: Very fast. BM25 on an indexed corpus is O(log n) and returns results in milliseconds. Dense embedding similarity is also fast but requires vector math; sparse is often 2–10x quicker on large corpora.

meta_title: Sparse Retrieval — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Token-based retrieval using BM25, TF-IDF, or learned sparse models like SPLADE that match exact keywords.

schema_jsonld.name: Sparse Retrieval
schema_jsonld.description: Token-based retrieval using BM25, TF-IDF, or learned sparse models like SPLADE that match exact keywords.

title: SPLADE (Sparse Learned Retrieval)
tldr: Sparse retrieval model using BERT to weight vocabulary terms learned end-to-end, deployable on inverted indexes.
plain_english: SPLADE is like BM25's smarter cousin. Instead of counting word frequency, it trains a neural network to predict which terms matter most for a query—including terms you never typed. The output is still a sparse vector (mostly zeros) that works with traditional search engines like Elasticsearch, so you get learned relevance without buying a vector database.
how_it_works: SPLADE uses a BERT encoder with a regularized output head that produces a sparse weight vector over the vocabulary. During training, the model learns which terms should activate for relevance; at query time, this produces implicit query expansion—the model predicts semantically related terms automatically. The sparse vector is scored using traditional lexical matching on inverted indexes (like Lucene), combining learned semantic understanding with existing infrastructure. Regularization (L1 penalty) keeps the representation sparse, making it efficient to index and retrieve.
why_it_matters: You gain learned semantic retrieval without replacing your Lucene or Elasticsearch backend. SPLADE bridges the gap between traditional sparse systems and modern neural retrieval, cutting deployment complexity and infrastructure costs while improving ranking quality over BM25.
example: A support team uses Elasticsearch to search internal documentation. Switching to SPLADE means their model learns that queries about 'authentication failure' should also match documents mentioning 'login errors' and 'session timeout'—without manual synonym configuration. The sparse vectors fit in the same inverted index, so no new infrastructure is needed.
pitfalls: Sparse output requires careful regularization; too little and vectors bloat; too much and relevant terms get pruned.
Query-time expansion is implicit in weights, not explicit terms, making debugging why a document ranked lowly harder than BM25.
Inference still requires a BERT forward pass per query, so latency is higher than BM25 but lower than reranking the full result set.
Training needs a good relevance dataset; poor labels hurt learning more than they would for unsupervised BM25.
when_use: Use SPLADE when you have Lucene or Elasticsearch, need better relevance than BM25, and want to avoid dense vector infrastructure.
when_avoid: Avoid SPLADE if you need sub-100ms query latency across millions of documents, already run dense retrieval, or lack labeled relevance data for fine-tuning.

faq.q: Is SPLADE the same as BM25?
faq.a: No. BM25 is unsupervised and counts term frequency; SPLADE is learned end-to-end, predicting term importance via neural networks. SPLADE typically ranks better but requires training data and inference compute.

faq.q: Can I use SPLADE without a vector database?
faq.a: Yes. SPLADE outputs sparse vectors that work directly in Elasticsearch or Lucene using inverted indexes. You don't need a separate vector DB, unlike dense retrievers.

faq.q: How much slower is SPLADE than BM25?
faq.a: SPLADE adds one BERT forward pass per query, typically 50–200ms. BM25 is faster at query time, but SPLADE offsets this with better ranking, reducing reranking overhead downstream.

faq.q: What is query expansion in SPLADE?
faq.a: SPLADE implicitly expands queries by assigning learned weights to related terms you didn't type. The model predicts which untyped vocabulary words are semantically relevant and weights them accordingly.

faq.q: Do I need to retrain SPLADE for my domain?
faq.a: Ideally yes—fine-tuning on your domain's relevance labels significantly improves ranking. Generic pretrained SPLADE models exist but will underperform a domain-adapted version.

meta_title: SPLADE (Sparse Learned Retrieval) — Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Sparse retrieval model using BERT to weight vocabulary terms learned end-to-end, deployable on inverted indexes.

schema_jsonld.name: SPLADE (Sparse Learned Retrieval)
schema_jsonld.description: Sparse retrieval model using BERT to weight vocabulary terms learned end-to-end, deployable on inverted indexes.

title: Adversarial Robustness
tldr: A model's ability to resist crafted inputs designed to trigger misclassification or unsafe outputs.
plain_english: Adversarial robustness is your model's defense against intentional attacks. Just as a spam filter learns to catch disguised emails, an LLM needs to resist prompts engineered to bypass safety guardrails—like jailbreak attempts or prompt injections that manipulate it into producing harmful content. No defense is perfect, but layered protections make attacks much harder.
how_it_works: Adversarial attacks come in two forms: gradient-based optimization (like GCG, which appends gibberish tokens to flip model behavior) and semantic attacks (jailbreaks and prompt injections written by humans). Defenses include: adversarial training (retraining on attack examples), input filtering (blocking known attack patterns), output filtering (catching unsafe responses before users see them), random masking (adding noise to inputs), and constitutional AI (training the model to follow safety principles). You layer multiple defenses because no single approach blocks all attacks.
why_it_matters: Any LLM exposed to untrusted input—customer-facing chat, tool-use systems, API endpoints—is a target. A single jailbreak reaching production can cause customer harm, regulatory scrutiny, or erosion of trust. Adversarial robustness shifts the cost of attack from detection to execution: attackers must spend more compute, time, and skill to succeed.
example: A healthcare chatbot trained to refuse diagnosis requests gets hit with a GCG attack appending optimized tokens to prompts. Without robustness testing, the attack succeeds and the bot generates medical advice. With adversarial training on 500 GCG examples and output filtering for medical claims, the attack fails. The team discovers the weakness in red-teaming before launch.
pitfalls: Adversarial training on one attack type (e.g., GCG) may not defend against different attacks (e.g., semantic jailbreaks); diversity of training data matters.
Over-filtering on adversarial examples can reduce model capability—answering legitimate edge-case questions becomes harder, causing over-refusal.
Testing robustness on a fixed set of attacks gives false confidence; attackers adapt faster than you can patch, so treat robustness as a process, not a checkbox.
Assuming a model is robust without red-teaming is the most common failure; published robustness metrics don't transfer to your deployment or threat model.
when_use: Use adversarial robustness testing when deploying customer-facing LLMs, enabling tool-use or code execution, or operating in high-stakes domains (healthcare, finance, law).
when_avoid: Skip formal adversarial robustness if you're prototyping, the model is not exposed to untrusted input, or your threat model doesn't include adversarial attackers.

faq.q: What is the difference between adversarial robustness and safety?
faq.a: Safety is the broad goal: avoiding harmful outputs. Adversarial robustness is one slice—defense against intentional attacks. A model can be safe against random queries but fail under deliberate jailbreak attempts.

faq.q: Can I make a model 100% adversarially robust?
faq.a: No. Perfect robustness is mathematically impossible for general tasks. The goal is to raise the cost of attack high enough that it's impractical for your threat model. Layer defenses and red-team continuously.

faq.q: Is adversarial training the same as RLHF?
faq.a: No. RLHF optimizes for user preference and alignment. Adversarial training specifically exposes the model to attack examples to build defense. They complement each other but serve different goals.

faq.q: How do I test for adversarial robustness?
faq.a: Run red-teaming (humans crafting attacks), use automated tools like GCG or jailbreak datasets, and monitor production logs for new attack patterns. Document what breaks, retrain on it, and test again.

faq.q: Do open-source models have worse adversarial robustness than closed models?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Robustness depends on training data and defense choices, not license. But open-source models are easier to attack because weights are public, enabling gradient-based optimization attacks.

meta_title: Adversarial Robustness — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: A model's ability to resist crafted inputs designed to trigger misclassification or unsafe outputs. Learn how to defend LLMs against jailbreaks and attacks.

schema_jsonld.name: Adversarial Robustness

title: AI Governance
tldr: Formal policies and processes for managing AI systems—inventory, risk assessment, approvals, monitoring, incident response.
plain_english: AI governance is your organization's rule book for AI. It answers: which AI systems do we have, how risky are they, who can deploy them, and what happens when something breaks. Think of it like IT security policy, but specifically designed for machine learning systems instead of databases.
how_it_works: Start with inventory: catalog all AI systems in use, including vendor models and custom deployments. Classify each by risk using a framework like NIST AI RMF—language models handling customer data rank higher than internal tools. Build approval workflows: model cards, dataset reviews, and bias assessments before deployment. Set up continuous monitoring: track model performance drift, detect anomalies, and log access. Create incident response procedures: who escalates, who investigates, how you roll back. Add third-party vendor assessment: audit external APIs and SaaS providers for compliance.
why_it_matters: Without governance, you ship models blindly into production, miss regulatory compliance deadlines, and can't explain decisions when they harm users. Formal governance reduces deployment cycle time by standardizing review, catches risks early, and protects your organization when regulators ask what safeguards you had in place.
example: A financial services company tracks 47 AI systems across fraud detection, credit scoring, and customer churn. They classify credit-scoring models as high-risk because regulatory bodies audit them; fraud detection is medium-risk because false positives cost money. All credit models require internal fairness audits before deployment. Monthly monitoring checks for performance drift on protected attributes. When a drift alert fires, the governance team investigates, potentially rolling back the model while the data science team retrains.
pitfalls: Treating governance as a compliance checkbox instead of a feedback loop—if you don't use findings to improve, audits become theater.
Over-classifying risk and creating approval bottlenecks that make teams bypass governance entirely.
Lacking clear escalation criteria—if every alert triggers a meeting, real problems drown in noise.
Assuming third-party vendor documentation is current; audit regularly, especially for updates.
when_use: Use AI governance whenever your models affect business-critical outcomes, touch regulated domains (finance, healthcare, hiring), or operate on behalf of users who can contest decisions.
when_avoid: Skip heavy governance structures for internal experimental notebooks, A/B testing dashboards, or one-off analyses that never ship to production.

faq.q: What's the difference between AI governance and AI safety?
faq.a: Safety is about preventing harms—bias, hallucinations, jailbreaks. Governance is the organizational structure that enforces safety checks. Safety is the goal; governance is the process.

faq.q: Do I need AI governance if I only use off-the-shelf models like ChatGPT?
faq.a: Yes, but lighter weight. You still need to document which models you use, who accesses them, and compliance implications. Third-party risk assessment is critical.

faq.q: What framework should we use—NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, or something else?
faq.a: Pick based on your regulatory environment. NIST AI RMF is US-focused, free, and widely adopted; ISO 42001 aligns with existing ISO compliance programs. Many orgs blend both.

faq.q: How often should we audit AI systems under governance?
faq.a: Risk-based: high-risk models monthly or after any retraining; medium-risk quarterly; low-risk annually. Trigger audits after any production incident or major data distribution shift.

faq.q: Can a small team implement AI governance or do we need a dedicated officer?
faq.a: Small teams can start with lightweight checklists and shared responsibility; governance scales to a dedicated function once you exceed ~10 production models or hit regulatory pressure.

meta_title: AI Governance — Definition, Frameworks & Implementation | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Formal policies and processes for managing AI systems—inventory, risk assessment, approvals, monitoring, incident response.

schema_jsonld.name: AI Governance
