title: Temperature
tldr: A parameter scaling how much randomness the model applies when picking the next token.
plain_english: Temperature controls whether your model plays it safe or takes risks. At 0, it always picks the most likely next word (like reading a predictable script). At 1.0, it samples words proportionally to their probabilities (normal conversation). Higher values make it more chaotic and unpredictable.
how_it_works: Temperature rescales the probability distribution over possible next tokens before sampling. At temperature 0, the model applies greedy decoding—it selects the single highest-probability token every time, producing deterministic output. At temperature 1.0, probabilities stay unchanged and the model samples stochastically. At temperatures above 1.0, the distribution flattens, making low-probability tokens more likely to be chosen. The math: divide logits by T before softmax. Higher T = flatter, more uniform distribution; lower T = sharper, more concentrated distribution.
why_it_matters: Temperature is one of the most direct controls over output quality and consistency. Set it too low and your model produces robotic repetition; set it too high and you get hallucinations, incoherence, and unreliable behavior. For deterministic tasks (code, math), you want low temperature. For creative or open-ended tasks, higher temperature gives variety without breaking coherence.
example: A customer-support chatbot would use temperature 0.3–0.5 to answer account questions reliably and identically each time. A creative writing assistant for brainstorming might use 0.8–1.0 to generate diverse plot ideas. An API for summarization would lock temperature at 0 to ensure consistency across identical requests.
pitfalls: Setting temperature too high (>1.5) causes the model to ignore probabilities and generate random nonsense; you lose the model's learned knowledge.
Confusing temperature with top-p or top-k; temperature reshapes the entire distribution before any truncation happens.
Using high temperature for code generation or factual tasks; the model will invent plausible-sounding but wrong answers.
Assuming temperature 0 is always safer; it can produce repetitive, evasive, or stilted responses in conversational contexts.
when_use: Use temperature >0.7 for creative writing, brainstorming, and open-ended generation; use 0.3–0.5 for classification, summarization, and factual retrieval; use 0 only when determinism is critical (e.g., reproducible evaluations).
when_avoid: Avoid high temperature (>1.0) for code, math, or factual Q&A; avoid temperature 0 if you need natural, varied conversational tone; avoid changing temperature mid-conversation without resetting context.

faq.q: What is the difference between temperature 0 and temperature 1?
faq.a: Temperature 0 always picks the highest-probability token (deterministic, repetitive). Temperature 1.0 samples proportionally to the original probabilities (stochastic, natural). Temperature 0 is useful for reproducibility; temperature 1.0 gives more natural variation.

faq.q: What temperature should I use for code generation?
faq.a: Use temperature 0–0.3. Code correctness depends on the model following learned patterns closely. High temperature introduces hallucinated syntax and logic errors.

faq.q: Can I use temperature with top-p sampling?
faq.a: Yes. Temperature reshapes the probability distribution first, then top-p truncates it. Using both together gives you control over both diversity and extreme-tail probability cutoff.

faq.q: Why does my model produce the same response every time?
faq.a: You likely have temperature set to 0 or very close to it. Increase temperature to 0.7–1.0 to get varied responses; even then, the prompt and model weights matter more than temperature alone.

faq.q: Is higher temperature always better for creativity?
faq.a: No. Temperature >1.5 often produces incoherent gibberish rather than creative ideas. Aim for 0.7–1.0; creativity comes from the model's training, not from chaos.

meta_title: Temperature — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: A parameter scaling how much randomness the model applies when picking the next token. Control creativity vs. consistency with 0.0–1.0+.

schema_jsonld.name: Temperature
schema_jsonld.description: A parameter scaling how much randomness the model applies when picking the next token.

title: Top-k Sampling
tldr: Sampling strategy that restricts choices to the K highest-probability tokens, ignoring the rest.
plain_english: When a language model generates text, it assigns a probability score to every word it knows. Top-k sampling says: "Only consider the top K most likely options, throw away the rest, then randomly pick one." It's like narrowing a restaurant menu to your 40 favorite dishes instead of reading all 500 options.
how_it_works: At each generation step, the model scores all tokens in its vocabulary. The decoder selects the K tokens with the highest scores, renormalizes their probabilities to sum to 1.0, then samples one token from that restricted distribution. Common K values range from 40 to 100. A small K (e.g., 5) produces repetitive, formulaic output; a large K (e.g., 500) approaches unrestricted sampling and reintroduces rare implausible tokens. Top-k is often combined with top-p (nucleus sampling) in practice: top-k acts as a hard ceiling, top-p adds adaptive cutoff based on cumulative probability.
why_it_matters: Top-k is a foundational decoding method exposed by nearly all LLM inference stacks. Understanding it unlocks why temperature alone doesn't control output quality, how beam search and other strategies differ, and how to avoid pathological behaviors like hallucinating nonsense tokens during low-confidence moments.
example: You're deploying a chatbot on vLLM with `SamplingParams(top_k=50, top_p=0.9, temperature=0.7)`. At each token, the model considers its full 32k-token vocabulary, but the decoder only allows the 50 most-likely candidates through. For a simple factual query, the top 50 almost certainly include the correct answer; for creative storytelling, top-p=0.9 ensures you still get stylistic variation without the decoder dredging up tokens ranked 500th.
pitfalls: Fixed K wastes compute at high-confidence steps (model is certain, K=50 includes implausible tokens) and cuts off alternatives at low-confidence steps (model unsure, K=50 may exclude valid continuations).
Confusing top-k with 'better' or 'safer'—it simply trades diversity for avoidance of tail tokens; use top-p or temperature if you need creative output.
Setting K too small (<10) creates boilerplate repetition; too large (>200) effectively disables the filter and reintroduces rare-token blowups.
Forgetting to renormalize probabilities after filtering; the K tokens' probabilities must be rescaled to sum to 1.0 before sampling.
when_use: Use top-k as a hard cap when you want to prevent rare or nonsensical token hallucinations, especially in risk-averse applications like code generation or medical chatbots, or when combined with top-p for production inference.
when_avoid: Avoid top-k alone for creative writing, brainstorming, or open-ended tasks—use top-p with temperature instead. Do not use if you need deterministic or near-deterministic output; use greedy decoding or beam search.

faq.q: What is the difference between top-k and top-p sampling?
faq.a: Top-k uses a fixed count: pick the K highest-probability tokens. Top-p uses cumulative probability: pick tokens until their probabilities sum to p (e.g., 90%). Top-p adapts to the model's confidence; top-k does not. Both can be combined in one decoder pass.

faq.q: How do I choose the right K value?
faq.a: Start with K=40–50 for general use. Increase to 100+ for creative tasks; decrease to 20–30 for conservative, factual applications. Experiment with your task—there is no universal K.

faq.q: Does top-k sampling prevent hallucinations?
faq.a: Partially. It reduces hallucinations caused by sampling from the tail of the distribution, but it does not prevent the model from confidently generating false information ranked in the top K. Use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) or fine-tuning for stronger hallucination control.

faq.q: Can I use top-k with greedy decoding?
faq.a: No. Greedy decoding always picks the single highest-probability token; there is no sampling or distribution to restrict. Top-k only applies to sampling-based strategies.

faq.q: What happens if K is larger than the vocabulary size?
faq.a: Top-k becomes equivalent to unrestricted sampling. The decoder will consider all tokens, so the filter has no effect. Keep K well below your vocabulary size (typically 30k–100k).

meta_title: Top-k Sampling — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Sampling strategy that restricts choices to the K highest-probability tokens, ignoring the rest.

schema_jsonld.name: Top-k Sampling
schema_jsonld.description: Sampling strategy that restricts choices to the K highest-probability tokens, ignoring the rest.

title: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling
tldr: Sampling from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches threshold p, adapting to the model's confidence.
plain_english: When a model generates text, it assigns probabilities to thousands of possible next tokens. Top-p sampling says: "Keep adding tokens in order of likelihood until you've covered p% of the total probability." If the model is confident (say, 90% sure the next word is 'the'), you might use only 2–3 tokens. If it's uncertain, you might use 50+. This is like asking: "What's the smallest group of likely options that covers 90% of what the model thinks?"
how_it_works: At each generation step, the model outputs a probability distribution over all tokens. You sort tokens by probability (highest first) and cumulatively sum them until the sum exceeds p (e.g., p=0.9). Only tokens in that set can be sampled. You then sample one token uniformly from that set. Top-p is often combined with temperature (which flattens or sharpens the distribution) and a hard top-k ceiling (to prevent rare, low-probability tokens from dominating in uncertain cases). The set size changes dynamically based on the distribution shape.
why_it_matters: Top-p prevents two failure modes: setting a fixed top-k too low produces bland, repetitive text; setting it too high allows incoherent rambling. Top-p adapts automatically—on confident predictions it tightens, on uncertain ones it opens. This is why modern LLMs can be both coherent and creative without hand-tuning k for every use case.
example: OpenAI's GPT-4o uses top_p=1.0 (full distribution) by default, but users tuning for creative writing often set top_p=0.9 with temperature=1.0 to get varied but plausible responses. A customer service chatbot might use top_p=0.8 and temperature=0.5 to be consistent and on-topic. Llama models via Ollama expose the same parameter, letting you match behavior across vendors by tuning the same two knobs.
pitfalls: Setting p=1.0 is not 'maximum creativity'—it includes the entire distribution, including garbage tokens. The creative sweet spot is typically p=0.85–0.95 with temperature ≥0.7.
Top-p and temperature interact multiplicatively. Lowering temperature narrows the distribution first, making top-p act on a smaller set. Adjust one at a time during tuning.
Different model cards ship different defaults (e.g., Llama 3 vs. Mistral). Check the model card and test on your task; don't assume p=0.9 works everywhere.
Top-p doesn't prevent repetition or off-topic drift on its own. Combine it with frequency_penalty or presence_penalty for longer outputs.
when_use: Use top-p when you want creative, varied generation (creative writing, brainstorming) but need the model to adapt automatically; when you're tuning across multiple model vendors and want a consistent knob; when you don't know k in advance.
when_avoid: Avoid top-p if you need deterministic output (use greedy decoding instead). Avoid if your task requires strictly constrained vocabularies (use logit masking). Don't rely on it alone for domain-specific safety—combine with guardrails or constrained decoding.

faq.q: What's the difference between top-p and top-k sampling?
faq.a: Top-k keeps exactly k highest-probability tokens regardless of their probabilities; top-p keeps tokens until cumulative probability reaches p, adapting set size to the distribution shape. Top-p usually produces more natural text because it tightens on confident predictions.

faq.q: Why does top-p interact with temperature?
faq.a: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution before top-p is applied. Lower temperature makes the distribution more peaked, so top-p's set shrinks; higher temperature flattens it, so top-p's set grows. They compound, not replace each other.

faq.q: Is p=0.9 always the right setting?
faq.a: No. p=0.9 is a common default, but it depends on your task, model, and temperature. Creative tasks often use p=0.85–0.95; factual tasks use lower values (p=0.7–0.8). Always test on your own data.

faq.q: Should I use top-p or top-k?
faq.a: Top-p is usually better for general-purpose generation because it adapts. Use top-k only if you need strict, predictable token set sizes or have a specific reason to prefer fixed behavior.

faq.q: Does top-p prevent hallucinations?
faq.a: No. Top-p only affects sampling diversity; it doesn't validate factual correctness. Combine it with prompt engineering, retrieval augmentation, or constrained decoding to reduce hallucination.

meta_title: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Sampling from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches threshold p, adapting to the model's confidence.

schema_jsonld.name: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling

title: Typical Sampling
tldr: Sample tokens whose information content matches the distribution's expected entropy, avoiding predictable and incoherent extremes.
plain_english: Typical sampling filters tokens by how surprising they are, keeping only those that fall within a reasonable surprise range. Think of it like choosing words that feel natural — not obvious (boring) and not bizarre (confusing). It's grounded in information theory: you measure how much information each token carries and only keep tokens near the distribution's average.
how_it_works: At each generation step, compute the negative log probability (information content) for every token in the vocabulary. Calculate the expected entropy of the full distribution. Keep only tokens whose information content falls within a threshold τ of that entropy. Sample uniformly or proportionally from this filtered set. Result: you get tokens that are typical under the model's distribution, avoiding both the highest-probability (redundant) and lowest-probability (nonsensical) tail tokens.
why_it_matters: For creative text generation, typical sampling produces more human-like variation than nucleus sampling without sacrificing coherence. It's principled — rooted in information theory rather than ad-hoc probability thresholds — making it more predictable across different model scales and domains.
example: A story-writing model using typical sampling with τ=1.0 generates: 'The detective entered the room and noticed...' with word choices that feel natural without being clichéd. Top-p sampling might skip rarer but fitting words; greedy decoding would pick the most obvious continuation every time. Typical sampling balances surprise and sense.
pitfalls: Setting τ too tight excludes useful variation; too loose reintroduces incoherence — requires empirical tuning per use case.
Entropy varies by token position and model state, so a fixed τ may behave differently early vs. late in generation.
Computational cost is higher than top-k or top-p because you calculate log-probs for the entire vocabulary.
The threshold τ is not directly comparable across models with different entropy scales.
when_use: Use typical sampling for creative writing, storytelling, or any task where you want natural variation without sacrificing coherence and where the cost of computing full-vocabulary entropy is acceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid when latency is critical, when you need deterministic output, or for tasks like code generation where you want either greedy correctness or tight nucleus sampling control.

faq.q: How is typical sampling different from top-p sampling?
faq.a: Top-p keeps cumulative probability mass below a threshold; typical sampling keeps tokens within an entropy range. Typical sampling is theory-driven and often produces different stylistic outputs, especially for creative tasks.

faq.q: What should I set the tau threshold to?
faq.a: Start with τ = 1.0 (within one unit of expected entropy) and adjust based on output quality. Smaller τ favors coherence; larger τ increases diversity. No universal default — tune empirically.

faq.q: Does typical sampling work with all model types?
faq.a: Yes, but entropy calculations assume a well-calibrated probability distribution. For quantized or distilled models, verify that log-probs are still reliable before deploying.

faq.q: Is typical sampling slower than top-p?
faq.a: Yes. Typical sampling requires computing negative log probabilities for all tokens and calculating full entropy, while top-p only needs cumulative sorting of a subset.

faq.q: Can I combine typical sampling with other decoding techniques?
faq.a: You can apply temperature before typical sampling to shift the entropy distribution. Combining with other filters (like logit bias) is possible but requires careful tuning.

meta_title: Typical Sampling — Information-Theory Decoding Strategy | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Sample tokens within the distribution's expected entropy range. Avoids boring and incoherent extremes for natural, coherent text generation.

title: 3D Parallelism
tldr: Combines data, tensor, and pipeline parallelism to train models with trillions of parameters across GPU clusters.
plain_english: Imagine you're distributing a massive computation across a grid of machines. 3D parallelism splits the work three ways: some GPUs handle different batches of data (data parallelism), others split individual matrix operations (tensor parallelism), and still others work on different layers sequentially (pipeline parallelism). It's like assembling a car on three separate assembly lines running in parallel.
how_it_works: You arrange GPUs in a 3D grid. Within each node, tensor parallelism shards matrix multiplications across GPUs connected by fast NVLink, eliminating memory bottlenecks on a single GPU. Pipeline parallelism distributes model layers across nodes linked by InfiniBand, hiding latency by computing different micro-batches on different layers simultaneously. Data parallelism replicates this entire 3D setup across multiple clusters, each processing different input batches. Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed coordinate these three axes automatically, handling communication patterns and memory allocation.
why_it_matters: Training models beyond a few hundred billion parameters requires 3D parallelism—single-axis parallelism hits communication bottlenecks or memory walls. Frontier labs use this technique to reach 1T+ parameter runs. Without it, you're stuck sharding inefficiently or buying vastly more hardware than necessary.
example: Meta's 405B Llama 3 model used 3D parallelism across thousands of H100 GPUs. Within each 8-GPU node, tensor parallelism split the attention and feedforward computations. Pipeline parallelism spanned multiple nodes. Data parallelism replicated this stack across clusters. This composition allowed training to scale to 4M+ tokens per second without hitting memory or bandwidth walls.
pitfalls: Unbalanced pipeline stages cause idle GPUs waiting for slow layers—tune micro-batch size and stage granularity carefully.
InfiniBand becomes a bottleneck if you over-rely on pipeline parallelism without sufficient tensor parallelism within nodes.
Debugging deadlocks across three axes requires distributed tracing—single-GPU debugging intuition fails entirely.
Checkpoint activation patterns interact poorly with all three axes; incorrect ordering causes redundant computation or memory spills.
when_use: Use 3D parallelism when training models larger than 100B parameters where single-node tensor parallelism cannot fit the model and data parallelism alone causes prohibitive communication overhead.
when_avoid: Avoid 3D parallelism for models under 50B parameters, single-machine training, or when you lack uniform, low-latency interconnect across nodes—the coordination overhead outweighs the benefit.

faq.q: What is the difference between 3D parallelism and data parallelism?
faq.a: Data parallelism replicates the entire model and trains separate batches on each GPU, then averages gradients. 3D parallelism shards the model itself (tensor and pipeline) while also using data parallelism on top, enabling much larger models across more GPUs without replicating the full model on each.

faq.q: Does 3D parallelism work with GPUs from different manufacturers?
faq.a: In theory yes, but in practice it requires NVLink or equivalent high-bandwidth intra-node interconnect and low-latency inter-node networks like InfiniBand. Mixing vendor-specific optimizations (NVIDIA NCCL, AMD RCCL) adds complexity; homogeneous clusters are strongly preferred.

faq.q: How much slower is 3D parallelism than single-GPU training?
faq.a: Communication overhead ranges from 10–30% of total training time on well-tuned clusters. Pipeline bubbles add 5–15% depending on micro-batch size. Poorly tuned setups can see 50%+ overhead. The trade-off is worth it because you could not train the model at all on a single GPU.

faq.q: Can I use 3D parallelism with quantization or low-rank adapters?
faq.a: 3D parallelism and quantization (QAT, post-training) are orthogonal—you can combine them. LoRA and other adapter methods reduce memory, but you still need all three axes for trillion-parameter models. Mixed approaches (e.g., tensor parallelism + LoRA) are uncommon in practice because the setup is already complex.

faq.q: What happens if one GPU fails during 3D parallel training?
faq.a: The entire training job fails because distributed checkpointing across three axes is slow and expensive. Most practitioners restart from the last checkpoint rather than attempt live recovery. Fault tolerance remains an open problem at this scale.

meta_title: 3D Parallelism — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: 3D Parallelism

title: Activation Steering
tldr: Add computed vectors to an LLM's activations at inference to shift behavior without retraining.
plain_english: Activation steering is like adjusting the volume knob on specific parts of a model's internal computation mid-conversation. Instead of retraining the whole model, you compute the difference in how a model 'thinks' when it behaves one way versus another, then inject that difference during inference. It works immediately and requires no gradient updates.
how_it_works: First, you run a model on contrasting prompts (truthful vs. deceptive, safe vs. jailbreak-prone) and record the activation values at a chosen layer. You compute the mean activation difference—your steering vector. At inference, before generating output, you add that vector to the residual stream. The model then produces text biased toward the target behavior. Effects are immediate but layer-specific and can degrade with strong scaling.
why_it_matters: Activation steering lets you control model behavior in production without fine-tuning, avoiding retraining costs and data collection. It's the fastest safety or alignment intervention available once you've identified the right vector and layer.
example: Anthropic used activation steering to increase Claude's truthfulness on factual questions. A researcher computed the activation difference between Claude's honest and evasive responses on a calibration set, then added that vector at the 15th transformer layer during inference on held-out questions. Truthfulness improved 10–15 percentage points with no model updates or prompt engineering.
pitfalls: Strong steering vectors corrupt unrelated outputs—maximizing one behavior often degrades coherence or factuality in other domains.
Layer and vector choice are not portable across model sizes or architectures; a vector for Claude won't work for Llama.
Steering effects diminish or flip on adversarial or out-of-distribution inputs; the vector captures a statistical tendency, not a rule.
Composing multiple steering vectors is unpredictable—adding vectors for truthfulness + safety may interfere with each other.
when_use: Use activation steering when you need fast, inference-time behavior modification on a deployed model and you have budget to compute and test steering vectors offline.
when_avoid: Avoid activation steering if you need persistent, generalizable behavior change across all input domains, or if you lack access to model internals (API-only models).

faq.q: How is activation steering different from fine-tuning or prompt engineering?
faq.a: Fine-tuning updates model weights (slow, expensive, permanent). Prompt engineering changes the input text (limited control, no internal steering). Activation steering modifies activations mid-computation (fast, reversible, surgical).

faq.q: Can I use the same steering vector across different models?
faq.a: No. Steering vectors are architecture and size-specific. A vector computed for a 70B model won't work reliably on a 7B model or a different family entirely.

faq.q: What layer should I steer at?
faq.a: Earlier layers (5–10) often capture broad behavioral patterns; middle layers (15–25) target specific semantics; later layers affect output formatting. Empirical testing on your use case is necessary.

faq.q: Does activation steering work on open-source models?
faq.a: Yes, if you have access to model weights and can run inference with activation hooks. It's easier on models like Llama or Mistral where you control the full pipeline.

faq.q: How do I know if my steering vector is working?
faq.a: Run the steered model on a held-out test set measuring your target behavior (truthfulness, safety, tone) and compare against unsteered baseline. Track for unintended side effects on unrelated tasks.

meta_title: Activation Steering — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Add computed vectors to an LLM's activations at inference to shift behavior without retraining.

schema_jsonld.name: Activation Steering

title: Adapter (PEFT Adapter)
tldr: Small trainable modules inserted into frozen model layers; only adapter weights update during fine-tuning.
plain_english: Think of an adapter as a small, removable attachment you bolt onto a pre-trained model. Instead of retraining the entire model (which costs money and time), you freeze the base and only train these tiny add-ons. Different adapters can be swapped in and out at runtime, letting you run multiple specialized versions of the same base model.
how_it_works: Adapters are bottleneck MLPs inserted after attention or feed-forward blocks in each transformer layer. Each adapter compresses activations down through a smaller dimension, applies a nonlinearity, then expands back up—typically 0.5–5% of total model parameters. During training, only adapter weights update via backpropagation; the base model stays frozen. At inference, you load the base once and swap adapters by changing a single weight matrix, avoiding the full model reload required for separate fine-tunes.
why_it_matters: Adapters let you serve thousands of task-specific models on a single base instance without memory explosion or per-request load penalties. This is how providers like Hugging Face and modal host multi-tenant fine-tune fleets. Adapters also predate LoRA—they're the conceptual foundation for all PEFT methods that minimize trainable parameters.
example: A support chatbot platform hosts one BERT-base instance and 200 adapters—one per customer account. When a query arrives, the router identifies the customer, loads that 2MB adapter into GPU memory, and runs inference. Training a new adapter takes 30 minutes on a single GPU versus 4 hours for a full retrain. Without adapters, the platform would need 200 separate model replicas.
pitfalls: Adapter inference still incurs the overhead of the forward pass through each adapter module—not free, just cheaper than full retraining.
Combining multiple adapters (e.g., composition or stacking) degrades performance compared to single-adapter inference and requires careful tuning.
Adapters can suffer from catastrophic forgetting if the base model's pre-training distribution drifts far from your task.
Parameter count is deceiving: 3% of model parameters can still require 15–20% more compute per forward pass due to sequential bottleneck layers.
when_use: Use adapters when you need to fine-tune a large model on limited compute, serve multiple task-specific variants from one base, or iterate quickly on domain adaptation without full retraining.
when_avoid: Avoid adapters if you need to fundamentally change model architecture, if latency is critical and you cannot tolerate the adapter-layer overhead, or if the task requires retraining the base model itself.

faq.q: Is an adapter the same as LoRA?
faq.a: No. Adapters are MLPs with bottleneck structure (down-project, activate, up-project). LoRA is a simpler technique that applies low-rank updates directly to weight matrices. LoRA came later and is now more popular, but adapters predate and inspired the entire PEFT family.

faq.q: How much faster is adapter training than full fine-tuning?
faq.a: Typically 2–4× faster with 3–5× fewer parameters to train. Actual speedup depends on your adapter size, batch size, and hardware; larger adapters approach full fine-tuning cost.

faq.q: Can I use adapters with any pre-trained model?
faq.a: Yes, but they work best with transformer-based models. You insert them after attention or feed-forward blocks. Non-transformer architectures require manual integration.

faq.q: What is the inference latency cost of running an adapter?
faq.a: Expect 5–15% overhead per forward pass due to the bottleneck layers. This is much cheaper than loading a separate fine-tuned model, but not zero-cost.

faq.q: How do I choose between adapters and LoRA?
faq.a: LoRA is simpler, more composable, and faster to train. Adapters are more modular at serving time and slightly more interpretable. Most new projects use LoRA, but adapters shine in high-volume serving scenarios.

title: Attention (Self-Attention)
tldr: Mechanism that weights token relationships by relevance, replacing recurrence in transformers and enabling long-context understanding.
plain_english: Imagine you're reading a sentence and need to understand which earlier words matter most for the current word. Attention does exactly that: for each token, it looks at all previous and surrounding tokens, scores how relevant each is, and blends them together to update the token's meaning. This happens in parallel across multiple "attention heads," each learning to track different types of relationships like grammar, coreference, or topics.
how_it_works: For each token, the model computes three learned vectors: Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V). It calculates attention scores by taking the dot product of Q with every other token's K, divides by the square root of dimension size, then applies softmax to normalize. These scores become weights applied to the V vectors, and the weighted sum becomes the token's updated representation. Multi-head attention runs this computation in parallel with different learned projections (typically 8–64 heads), allowing different heads to specialize in syntax, semantics, coreference, or other patterns.
why_it_matters: Attention replaced recurrence and convolutions as the core mechanism in modern transformers, enabling models to handle thousands of tokens without computational bottlenecks. It's why LLMs can maintain coherent long-range dependencies and why the Transformer architecture (2017) became the foundation for GPT, Claude, and every modern LLM. Without attention's ability to directly connect distant tokens, scaling to current model sizes would be infeasible.
example: When Claude processes a 100k-token document, attention allows the model to weight relevant prior sections when generating an answer, rather than relying on a fixed recurrent state. A 64-head attention layer lets different heads track: head 1 focuses on pronoun-noun relationships, head 2 on semantic similarity, head 3 on sentence structure. After training, ~30–50% of heads often become redundant and can be pruned with negligible quality loss.
pitfalls: Not all trained attention heads are meaningful—many are functionally random. Pruning 30–50% of heads usually causes <1% quality drop.
Attention is quadratic in sequence length without optimization; Flash Attention, KV cache, and sparse variants are required for production at large context windows.
Attention weights alone don't explain model behavior—weight patterns often differ from the linguistic phenomena they supposedly track (mechanistic interpretation is still open).
Head specialization is not guaranteed; some heads learn task-agnostic patterns and some learn task-specific ones without clear separation.
when_use: Use attention whenever you need the model to relate tokens across variable distances; it's the default in all modern transformers for language, vision, and multimodal tasks.
when_avoid: Avoid hand-tuning attention patterns or over-interpreting attention weights as explanations of model decisions—use sparse or linear attention variants only if memory or latency profiling shows attention is the bottleneck.

faq.q: What is the difference between self-attention and cross-attention?
faq.a: Self-attention computes Q, K, V from the same sequence, letting each token attend to all others. Cross-attention uses Q from one sequence and K, V from another—common in encoder-decoder models where the decoder attends to encoder outputs.

faq.q: Why do we divide by the square root of dimension in attention?
faq.a: Dividing by sqrt(d) prevents attention scores from becoming too large (causing vanishing gradients after softmax). It's a scaling trick that keeps gradients stable during backpropagation.

faq.q: Can you remove attention heads without retraining?
faq.a: Yes. Many heads are redundant after training; pruning 30–50% typically causes <1% accuracy loss. Some work (like movement pruning) shows you can remove heads and fine-tune, but direct removal post-hoc often works too.

faq.q: How much does attention cost compared to other layers?
faq.a: In inference, attention is O(n²) in sequence length for exact computation, making it expensive at long contexts. KV caching reduces recomputation per new token, but prefill (initial context processing) remains quadratic without optimizations like Flash Attention or chunked prefill.

faq.q: Do different attention heads learn different things?
faq.a: Partially. Some heads specialize in syntax or coreference, but many learn task-agnostic patterns or overlapping functions. There's no guarantee each head learns a distinct, interpretable role.

meta_title: Attention (Self-Attention) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization)
tldr: 4-bit weight quantization that protects important channels by scaling based on activation magnitudes.
plain_english: AWQ treats all weight channels the same way during quantization, except it first identifies which channels are actually important by looking at how much signal flows through them (their activation magnitudes). It scales up the important channels before compressing everything to 4 bits, then scales the incoming activations down to compensate. Think of it like protecting highway lanes during roadwork—you keep the busiest lanes wider.
how_it_works: AWQ observes that only a small fraction of weight channels matter for model output quality—specifically those that receive large activation values. Before quantization, it scales these high-activation channels up (and their incoming activations down) to keep them numerically stable when compressed to 4 bits. The rest of the weights compress normally. After quantization, the scaling factors are absorbed into the model, requiring no runtime overhead. TensorRT-LLM and vLLM both execute AWQ kernels efficiently on GPUs.
why_it_matters: AWQ is the standard 4-bit serving technique because it combines near-zero quality loss with production-ready latency. It often outperforms GPTQ on instruction-tuned models and integrates directly into modern serving frameworks, making it the default choice for resource-constrained inference.
example: A team running Llama 2 70B on consumer GPUs needs 4-bit quantization to fit the model. Using AWQ instead of GPTQ reduces perplexity loss by 0.5 points on their benchmark, and vLLM's AWQ kernels hit 180 tokens/sec (vs. 140 with GPTQ). They deploy with confidence that the 4-bit version remains instruction-following enough for their chatbot.
pitfalls: AWQ scaling assumes importance is stable across batches; fine-tuning or domain shift can reduce its accuracy gains.
Implementing AWQ kernels is non-trivial; relying on pre-quantized weights from llm-awq avoids reimplementation bugs.
AWQ per-channel scaling adds memory overhead during quantization (not serving); budget extra RAM for the conversion step.
Mixing AWQ with other compression techniques (e.g., pruning) requires careful experimentation—gains don't always stack.
when_use: Use AWQ when you need 4-bit LLM inference on GPUs, the model is instruction-tuned or vision-language, and you want to integrate with vLLM or TensorRT-LLM without custom kernels.
when_avoid: Avoid AWQ if you require sub-4-bit precision, must deploy on CPUs only, or need strict per-token activation statistics that change per batch.

faq.q: What is the difference between AWQ and GPTQ?
faq.a: GPTQ quantizes weights using Hessian information about loss curvature; AWQ scales weights by activation magnitude before quantization. AWQ is simpler, faster to compute, and often wins on instruct models, while GPTQ can be more accurate on base models.

faq.q: Can I use AWQ with any model?
faq.a: AWQ works best on transformer models with clear per-channel importance patterns. It has been validated on LLMs and vision-language models; porting to new architectures requires engineering effort to extract accurate activation statistics.

faq.q: Does AWQ add latency during inference?
faq.a: No. The scaling factors are baked into the quantized weights during conversion. At serving time, AWQ inference is identical in speed to standard 4-bit quantization, assuming your framework (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM) has optimized kernels.

faq.q: How much GPU memory does AWQ save compared to FP16?
faq.a: AWQ reduces model weights to 4 bits, cutting size by 75% vs. 16-bit (e.g., 70B model goes from ~140GB to ~35GB). Full savings depend on batch size, KV cache, and activations.

faq.q: Is AWQ safe for production?
faq.a: Yes, if you validate perplexity and task performance on your data. Pre-quantized AWQ weights from reputable sources (HuggingFace, Ollama) are production-ready; always benchmark before deploying to users.

meta_title: AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization) — Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: 4-bit weight quantization that protects important channels by scaling based on activation magnitudes.

schema_jsonld.name: AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization)

title: Best-of-N Sampling
tldr: Generate N candidates at inference time, score each with a reward model, return the highest-scoring one.
plain_english: Instead of accepting the first completion your model generates, you ask it to produce multiple candidate answers, then pick the best one using a separate scoring system. It's like asking five people the same question and trusting a judge to pick the best answer—you get better quality without retraining the model.
how_it_works: You set temperature above zero and generate N different completions from the same prompt. Run each completion through a reward model or verifier (a smaller classifier, test harness, or semantic scorer). Return the completion with the highest score. Quality typically improves logarithmically with N—gains saturate around N=16 to 64 depending on task and reward signal quality. Works best when the reward model is fast and cheap compared to generation.
why_it_matters: Best-of-N is the cheapest inference-time quality win available. It requires no model retraining and scales predictably. It underpins modern test-time compute strategies used in reasoning models and is the foundation for techniques like rejection sampling and speculative decoding.
example: A math tutoring system generates 16 different solutions to a calculus problem at temperature 0.8. A symbolic math verifier scores each by checking the final answer against a CAS. The system returns the solution with the highest verification score. With 16 samples, solve rate climbs from 72% to 84% without touching the base model.
pitfalls: Reward model quality matters more than N—a weak scorer makes more samples worse, not better.
Diminishing returns kick in fast; doubling N beyond 32 rarely justifies the compute cost.
Assumes your reward signal is reliable; proxy scores (like length or confidence) often fail on edge cases.
Doesn't work well on tasks with no clear checkable criterion (creative writing, open-ended reasoning).
when_use: Use Best-of-N when you have a cheap, reliable way to score completions (tests, math verifiers, semantic similarity), the base model is expensive relative to scoring, and N ≤ 64 fits your latency budget.
when_avoid: Avoid when your only scorer is another LLM (defeats the cost advantage), the task has no objective ground truth, or you need sub-second latency at scale.

faq.q: How does Best-of-N differ from beam search?
faq.a: Beam search generates candidates deterministically and narrows them at each decoding step; Best-of-N samples at full temperature and scores only at the end. Best-of-N is simpler and often better for quality when you have a reliable reward model.

faq.q: What's a good reward model for Best-of-N?
faq.a: Task-specific: unit tests for code, symbolic verifiers for math, semantic similarity for retrieval, or a trained preference model. The score must be fast (milliseconds) and strongly correlated with actual task success.

faq.q: How many samples N should I generate?
faq.a: Start with N=4 to 8 and measure quality vs. latency trade-off. Gains typically plateau around N=16 to 32. Only push higher if your scorer is very cheap and latency budget allows.

faq.q: Can I use Best-of-N with a vision or multimodal model?
faq.a: Yes, if you have a scoring mechanism. Common: generating multiple image captions and ranking by semantic similarity, or sampling code with vision and ranking by test pass rate.

faq.q: Does Best-of-N work for open-ended tasks like creative writing?
faq.a: Poorly, because scoring requires either expensive LLM-as-judge calls or weak proxies. Better suited to tasks with measurable outputs (code, math, factual QA, retrieval).

meta_title: Best-of-N Sampling — Inference-Time Quality Technique | OSS AI Hub

title: Chain of Thought
tldr: Prompt an LLM to show reasoning step-by-step before answering, improving accuracy on complex tasks.
plain_english: Chain of Thought is like asking someone to talk through their work before giving you an answer. Instead of jumping straight to a conclusion, you ask the model to explain each step of its reasoning. This simple change—adding 'Let's think step by step' or showing examples with working shown—makes the model much better at math, logic, and multi-part problems.
how_it_works: You include reasoning steps in your prompt, either as instructions ('Let's think step by step') or as examples showing a problem solved with intermediate steps. The model then generates token-by-token reasoning before producing a final answer. Internally, this forces the transformer to distribute its computation across more tokens, reducing the chance of errors on reasoning-heavy tasks. Models like Claude and OpenAI's o1 automate this process; you still get the benefit without writing the steps yourself.
why_it_matters: On reasoning tasks—math, logic puzzles, code debugging, multi-hop retrieval—CoT can double or triple accuracy with no cost beyond longer output. It's one of the highest-ROI prompt changes you can make. For production systems, longer reasoning chains do increase latency and token cost, so you trade speed for correctness.
example: A customer support team uses CoT to debug API errors. Instead of 'Why did the request fail?', they prompt 'Walk through what happens when a user sends this request, step by step.' The model now traces through authentication, rate limits, and payload validation before saying 'Rate limit exceeded on line 4.' Accuracy jumps from 60% to 85% on error classification.
pitfalls: CoT helps reasoning tasks but wastes tokens on simple retrieval or classification—use only when the task genuinely needs multi-step thinking.
Showing working doesn't help if the intermediate steps are wrong; garbage logic chains reinforce errors rather than catching them.
Longer outputs increase latency and cost per query; batch offline work when possible, or use a fast model for real-time tasks.
Models can invent plausible-sounding steps even when they're hallucinating the final answer; CoT alone doesn't guarantee correctness.
when_use: Use CoT when you need multi-step reasoning: math word problems, logic puzzles, code debugging, complex analysis, or any task where mistakes come from rushed thinking rather than knowledge gaps.
when_avoid: Avoid CoT for simple lookup, classification with no reasoning needed, or real-time user-facing responses where latency is critical.

faq.q: What's the difference between Chain of Thought and Tree of Thought?
faq.a: CoT generates one linear reasoning path. Tree of Thought explores multiple reasoning branches and picks the best one, giving higher accuracy at the cost of many more forward passes.

faq.q: Does Chain of Thought work for all tasks?
faq.a: No. It helps tasks requiring multi-step reasoning (math, logic, debugging). For pure recall or classification, it wastes tokens with little benefit.

faq.q: How much longer does output take with Chain of Thought?
faq.a: Output length typically doubles or triples, so latency and token cost scale proportionally. On an A100, expect 50–200ms extra per request depending on reasoning length.

faq.q: Can I use Chain of Thought with smaller models?
faq.a: Yes, but smaller models produce lower-quality reasoning chains. A 7B model with CoT often underperforms a 70B model without it on hard reasoning tasks.

faq.q: Is Chain of Thought the same as showing your work?
faq.a: Functionally yes—both mean explaining intermediate steps. CoT is the technical term; 'show your work' is how you prompt for it.

meta_title: Chain of Thought — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: Constitutional AI
tldr: AI system that critiques and revises its own outputs against a written constitution of principles, scaling safety without human labelers.
plain_english: Think of it as a self-editing system with a rulebook. The model generates a response, then reads it against principles like 'be honest' or 'refuse harmful requests,' and rewrites it. This creates training data for preference learning without hiring humans to judge every bad output.
how_it_works: The process runs in two stages. First, the model generates a candidate response and then critiques it against a written constitution—a set of principles defining desired behavior. The model revises its response based on the critique, producing a preferred version. Second, these synthetic preference pairs feed into RLAIF (reinforcement learning from AI feedback), where an AI preference model trained on the constitution ranks outputs. This replaces the human labeler bottleneck in RLHF pipelines.
why_it_matters: Safety training typically scales with human annotation cost—labelers must review thousands of harmful outputs. Constitutional AI offloads this to the model itself, reducing labor bottlenecks while maintaining alignment to explicit principles. This makes scaling safety training economically viable and lets you audit exactly what principles shaped the model.
example: Anthropic used Constitutional AI to train Claude. Instead of hiring thousands of labelers to judge responses about violence or misinformation, Claude critiques its own drafts against principles like 'be helpful, harmless, and honest.' The self-critiques generate preference pairs used in RL training. This approach cut annotation costs while keeping Claude's refusals principled and auditable.
pitfalls: The constitution's wording matters enormously—vague principles ('be safe') produce vague behavior; the model mirrors ambiguity back.
AI feedback can amplify its own blindspots; if the critique model misses a harmful edge case, the RL training will too.
Over-reliance on constitution can cause rigid refusal patterns that harm legitimate use cases if principles aren't carefully balanced.
The synthetic data loop creates no external validity check—you're training on self-generated preferences with no human ground truth.
when_use: Use Constitutional AI when you need to scale safety training cost-effectively, have clear behavioral principles you can articulate, and want auditability of alignment decisions.
when_avoid: Avoid it if your principles are ambiguous, you lack a strong base model for self-critique, or you need human judgment on novel harms the model hasn't seen.

faq.q: How is Constitutional AI different from RLHF?
faq.a: RLHF requires humans to label which responses are better; Constitutional AI uses the model's own critiques against a rulebook. Constitutional AI scales labeling; RLHF requires human judgment.

faq.q: Can the model cheat by critiquing itself favorably?
faq.a: Yes, if the constitution is vague or the model learns that praising itself is rewarded. Strong, specific principles and diverse critique prompts reduce this, but it remains a real risk.

faq.q: Do I need a constitution to use this?
faq.a: Yes. The constitution is the core—a written set of principles the model uses to judge itself. Without clear, consistent principles, the technique fails.

faq.q: Can Constitutional AI catch all harmful outputs?
faq.a: No. The model can only critique what it recognizes as violating the constitution. Novel harms, edge cases, or principle contradictions often slip through.

faq.q: Is Constitutional AI used in production systems?
faq.a: Yes. Anthropic uses it to train Claude. Other labs experiment with variants, but RLHF with human feedback remains the industry standard for high-stakes alignment.

meta_title: Constitutional AI — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: Continued Pretraining
tldr: Extend a base model's pretraining on domain-specific unlabeled text to inject specialized knowledge into weights.
plain_english: Imagine you have a general-purpose student (a base model) and you want them to become an expert in law. Instead of just giving them legal documents to retrieve from, you let them read through years of legal texts the same way they learned language initially. Continued pretraining does exactly this: it runs language modeling on raw domain text atop an existing model, moving expertise from retrieval systems into the model's actual parameters.
how_it_works: You take a pretrained base model and resume causal language modeling on unlabeled domain data (medical records, financial reports, internal codebases, proprietary documentation). Token counts typically range from hundreds of millions to tens of billions. You use low learning rates and often replay some original pretraining data to prevent catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. The model learns domain-specific patterns, vocabulary, and reasoning directly into its weights without task-specific instruction formatting.
why_it_matters: When RAG retrieval hits accuracy limits or domain jargon is too dense to retrieve reliably, continued pretraining moves knowledge from external systems into the model itself. This is far more effective than fine-tuning for absorbing large volumes of specialized text and avoids the performance cliff that comes from relying solely on retrieval for rare or technical domains.
example: A financial institution training a model on 10 billion tokens of earnings calls, regulatory filings, and internal trading documentation. After continued pretraining on this domain corpus, the model understands financial terminology, regulatory context, and historical trends so deeply that it no longer needs to retrieve every fact; it can reason directly. Inference stays fast while domain accuracy improves dramatically.
pitfalls: Confusing continued pretraining with instruction fine-tuning: pretraining uses raw text; fine-tuning uses task-formatted label pairs.
Setting learning rates too high, causing the model to overfit to domain data and lose general-purpose capabilities irreversibly.
Underestimating compute cost: pretraining billions of tokens on full model parameters is 10–100× more expensive than LoRA fine-tuning.
Skipping data replay from the original pretraining distribution, risking catastrophic forgetting of English, reasoning, and common knowledge.
when_use: Use continued pretraining when you have large volumes of unlabeled domain text (billions+ tokens), retrieval alone cannot cover your task, and you can afford the compute cost.
when_avoid: Avoid continued pretraining if you have small domain datasets (< 100M tokens), need results in days, or can achieve your goal with fine-tuning, RAG, or prompt engineering.

faq.q: How is continued pretraining different from fine-tuning?
faq.a: Continued pretraining uses raw unlabeled text and objective (causal language modeling), while fine-tuning uses labeled task examples and task-specific loss. Pretraining is slower but injects domain knowledge at scale; fine-tuning is faster but works best with structured task data.

faq.q: How many tokens do I need for continued pretraining to work?
faq.a: Practical impact typically emerges after 500M–2B tokens. Optimal results often require 5B–20B domain tokens, depending on domain specificity and model size. Below 100M tokens, fine-tuning or retrieval is usually more cost-effective.

faq.q: Will continued pretraining make my model forget general knowledge?
faq.a: Yes, if you train too long or with too high a learning rate on pure domain data. Mitigate this by using low learning rates (0.5–5× lower than original pretraining), replaying 10–30% original pretraining data, and monitoring eval sets for language and reasoning tasks.

faq.q: Can I do continued pretraining with limited GPU?
faq.a: Not practically. Full-parameter pretraining requires distributed training across multiple A100s or H100s for weeks. If budget is constrained, use LoRA fine-tuning, prompt engineering, or RAG instead.

faq.q: Should I do continued pretraining before or after instruction tuning?
faq.a: Continued pretraining first, then instruction tuning. Pretraining on domain text injects specialized knowledge, then supervised fine-tuning teaches the model to use that knowledge in task-specific formats.

meta_title: Continued Pretraining — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: Continued Pretraining

title: DARE (Drop And REscale)
tldr: Randomly zero out 90% of task vector parameters and rescale survivors to produce sparse, stable model merges.
plain_english: When you fine-tune a model, most of the changes you make are redundant—you don't actually need 90% of them. DARE exploits this by randomly deleting most parameter changes and boosting the remaining ones to compensate. The result: you can merge multiple fine-tuned models without them interfering with each other.
how_it_works: For each fine-tuned model's task vector (the difference between the fine-tuned and base weights), randomly set a large fraction (typically 90%) of parameters to zero. Scale the remaining 10% by 1/(1−drop_rate) = 10× to restore the original expected magnitude. Combine these sparsified task vectors using any merge strategy—TIES works especially well because it now has cleaner parameter overlap to resolve. The mechanism works because fine-tuning redundancy means you lose almost no task performance by dropping 90% of changes.
why_it_matters: DARE reveals that task learning is fundamentally sparse—most parameter updates are wasted effort. Practically, sparsified merges remain stable as you add more models; dense merges degrade with each addition. This makes scaling to many task-specific models feasible without conflicts.
example: A team maintaining separate medical, legal, and coding fine-tunes of Llama 2 uses DARE to sparsify each task vector (dropping 90%), then merges all three with TIES. The merged model retains 95%+ performance on each specialist task while accepting inputs in any domain. A dense merge would cause catastrophic forgetting; DARE prevents it.
pitfalls: Dropping too little (e.g., 50%) defeats the purpose and leaves redundancy; dropping too much (e.g., 95%) can harm performance if your task vector genuinely needs those parameters.
DARE assumes task vectors are truly redundant—if a single model is fine-tuned on very different objectives, sparsity assumptions break down.
Rescaling by 1/(1−drop_rate) works only if you're using a fixed drop rate across all models; inconsistent drop rates across merges produce magnitude mismatches.
DARE alone doesn't handle parameter conflicts when task vectors point in opposite directions—you still need TIES or another merge strategy afterward.
when_use: Use DARE when merging multiple fine-tuned models and you want stable, scalable combinations; especially effective before TIES-based merging.
when_avoid: Avoid DARE if your task vectors are already sparse, if you're merging models trained on the same task (no redundancy to exploit), or if you need to preserve every parameter change.

faq.q: What is DARE merging?
faq.a: DARE randomly zeros 90% of task vector parameters and rescales the rest, creating sparse models that merge without interference. It's based on the finding that fine-tuning redundancy is extreme.

faq.q: How does DARE differ from TIES?
faq.a: DARE sparsifies task vectors before merging; TIES resolves conflicts between dense task vectors. Use DARE first to densify, then TIES to pick winners where vectors overlap.

faq.q: What drop rate should I use?
faq.a: The original paper uses 90%. Start there; lower drop rates (70–80%) preserve more information if your tasks are very different, higher rates (95%+) work only if redundancy is extreme.

faq.q: Does DARE work with LoRA?
faq.a: Yes. Extract the LoRA-derived task vectors, apply DARE, then merge. The sparsity mechanism is model-agnostic.

faq.q: Can I use DARE without TIES?
faq.a: Yes, you can merge sparsified vectors with any strategy (slerp, linear interpolation). TIES is just a natural downstream step because it handles remaining conflicts cleanly.

meta_title: DARE (Drop And REscale) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Randomly zero out 90% of task vector parameters and rescale survivors to produce sparse, stable model merges.

schema_jsonld.name: DARE (Drop And REscale)

title: Data Parallelism
tldr: Each GPU holds a full model copy, processes different batch shards, then averages gradients across devices.
plain_english: Imagine you're grading 1000 essays. Instead of one person reading all of them, you give 8 people 125 essays each, they grade independently, then you average their scoring rubrics. Data parallelism works the same way: your model stays the same on every GPU, but each GPU processes different training examples, then the gradient updates get averaged back together.
how_it_works: Every GPU receives a full copy of the model and a different slice of the training batch. Each GPU runs a forward pass on its batch shard, computes gradients during backpropagation, then uses all-reduce (a collective communication operation) to sum and average gradients across all devices. Each GPU then updates its model with the same averaged gradient. This repeats for every batch. Scaling is linear with GPU count until network bandwidth becomes the bottleneck—typically around 8–64 GPUs on standard Ethernet, higher on InfiniBand.
why_it_matters: Data parallelism is the default for most fine-tuning and training workflows because it's simple to implement, requires no model refactoring, and works on any model size that fits on a single GPU. Understanding when it hits its limits tells you when to switch to model parallelism or fully sharded data parallel (FSDP) for larger models.
example: A team fine-tuning a 7B-parameter LLaMA model on 8 H100 GPUs uses PyTorch DistributedDataParallel. They run a batch of 256 examples per iteration: each GPU processes 32 examples, computes 32 loss values and their gradients, then NCCL synchronizes gradients across the 8 GPUs before each model updates. Training throughput nearly 8x compared to single-GPU baseline, with negligible communication overhead.
pitfalls: Gradient averaging assumes all GPUs process the same batch size; uneven batch splits cause slow synchronization and wasted compute.
Network bandwidth becomes the bottleneck before GPU memory; 64+ GPU clusters over Ethernet often see 30–50% efficiency loss.
Model must fit entirely in each GPU's VRAM; models larger than single-GPU memory require FSDP or tensor parallelism instead.
All-reduce is a blocking operation; asynchronous gradient updates or gradient accumulation can hide some latency but complicate debugging.
when_use: Use data parallelism when your model fits on a single GPU, you have 2–32 GPUs on a low-latency network, and you want the simplest distributed training setup.
when_avoid: Avoid data parallelism when your model exceeds single-GPU VRAM, you need >64 GPUs, or you are training across geographically distributed sites with high latency.

faq.q: What's the difference between data parallelism and model parallelism?
faq.a: Data parallelism replicates the entire model on each GPU and splits the batch; model parallelism splits the model across GPUs and keeps the batch intact. Data parallelism is simpler but requires the model to fit on one GPU; model parallelism handles larger models but adds complexity.

faq.q: How much faster is training with 8 GPUs using data parallelism?
faq.a: Ideally, ~7.5–7.8× faster (near-linear scaling), because each GPU processes 1/8 of the batch and communication overhead is low. Real-world speedup drops below 7× if network bandwidth or GPU utilization becomes uneven.

faq.q: Does PyTorch DistributedDataParallel (DDP) require special code changes?
faq.a: Minimal changes: wrap your model in `DistributedDataParallel`, set up process groups (usually with `torch.distributed.init_process_group`), and create a `DistributedSampler` for your dataset. Accelerate library automates most of this.

faq.q: When should I switch from data parallelism to FSDP?
faq.a: Switch when your model no longer fits in a single GPU's VRAM, or when you have >64 GPUs and need to reduce per-GPU communication overhead. FSDP shards both model parameters and gradients across devices.

faq.q: Does data parallelism work across multiple machines?
faq.a: Yes, but communication latency between machines is 100–1000× higher than within a node. Cross-machine data parallelism works best on high-bandwidth interconnects like InfiniBand; Ethernet typically causes 20–40% efficiency loss.

meta_title: Data Parallelism — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Each GPU holds a full model copy, processes different batch shards, then averages gradients across devices.

schema_jsonld.name: Data Parallelism

title: DeepSpeed ZeRO
tldr: Memory optimization that partitions optimizer state, gradients, and parameters across GPUs to train larger models on modest hardware.
plain_english: ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) solves a practical problem: when you train huge models, each GPU holds redundant copies of optimizer state, gradients, and weights, wasting memory. ZeRO cuts that waste by splitting these pieces across your GPU cluster so each device stores only what it needs. Think of it like distributing chapters of a book across readers instead of each reader holding a full copy.
how_it_works: ZeRO has three stages, each more aggressive than the last. ZeRO-1 shards optimizer state (momentum buffers, variance estimates) across devices—you only store your slice. ZeRO-2 adds gradient sharding during backward pass; each GPU computes gradients but only keeps its partition. ZeRO-3 shards parameters themselves during forward pass, gathering them only when needed. ZeRO-Infinity extends this to CPU RAM and NVMe disk, enabling trillion-parameter training on clusters with modest GPU memory. The tradeoff: more communication overhead between devices.
why_it_matters: Without ZeRO, a 70B-parameter model requires massive GPUs or distributed training with severe communication bottlenecks. ZeRO made it practical to train models at scale on commodity A100/H100 clusters, reducing infrastructure cost and enabling research labs without unlimited NVIDIA inventory to participate in frontier model work.
example: A research team training a 175B-parameter model on 32 A100 GPUs with ZeRO-3 + NVMe offload reduces per-GPU memory from ~80GB to ~6GB by partitioning weights and spilling rarely-accessed parameters to disk. They sacrifice 20% throughput (longer training time) but stay within a $500K budget instead of needing 256 GPUs or custom TPU clusters.
pitfalls: ZeRO-3 requires all-reduce communication after every forward pass to gather parameters; on slow interconnect (e.g., 1Gbps Ethernet), throughput drops sharply—use only with NVLink or InfiniBand.
CPU/NVMe offload (ZeRO-Infinity) trades memory for latency; training is 2–3× slower and suits long-term pretraining, not iterative research with wall-clock limits.
ZeRO-2 and ZeRO-3 break some gradient accumulation workflows and require careful batch-size tuning; gradient synchronization points differ from single-GPU training.
Most practitioners now use FSDP (PyTorch's native equivalent) over DeepSpeed ZeRO for better integration with PyTorch ecosystem and simpler debugging.
when_use: Use ZeRO when training models larger than your GPU memory allows, you have multi-node clusters with fast interconnect, and you prioritize memory efficiency over maximum throughput.
when_avoid: Avoid ZeRO on single-GPU setups, slow network (non-NVLink), or if you need absolute peak throughput for time-bound training (e.g., real-time model serving research).

faq.q: What is the difference between ZeRO-1, ZeRO-2, and ZeRO-3?
faq.a: ZeRO-1 partitions optimizer state (smallest memory gain). ZeRO-2 adds gradient partitioning (moderate gain). ZeRO-3 partitions parameters too (maximum memory reduction but highest communication cost). Each is a superset of the previous.

faq.q: Is DeepSpeed ZeRO the same as FSDP?
faq.a: Both use similar sharding strategies, but FSDP is PyTorch's native implementation while DeepSpeed ZeRO is a separate library. FSDP integrates better with modern PyTorch; ZeRO has more tuning options and longer history in production.

faq.q: Does ZeRO work with single GPU?
faq.a: No. ZeRO requires distributed training across multiple devices. On a single GPU, it offers no benefit and adds overhead.

faq.q: How much slower is ZeRO-3 compared to single-GPU training?
faq.a: On fast interconnect (NVLink), overhead is 10–20%. On Ethernet, it can be 2–5×. CPU/NVMe offload (ZeRO-Infinity) is 2–3× slower due to disk I/O.

faq.q: Can I use ZeRO for inference?
faq.a: ZeRO is designed for training. For inference, use model parallelism or quantization instead.

meta_title: DeepSpeed ZeRO — Memory Optimization for Distributed Training | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Memory optimization that partitions optimizer state, gradients, and parameters across GPUs to train larger models on modest hardware.

schema_jsonld.name: DeepSpeed ZeRO

title: Distillation (Knowledge Distillation)
tldr: Train a small model to mimic a large model's outputs, trading minor quality loss for 5–50x inference cost reduction.
plain_english: Imagine you have an expert consultant (the teacher) and you want to teach a junior analyst (the student) to make decisions as good as the expert's. Instead of teaching the junior how to think, you show them thousands of the expert's decisions and let them learn the patterns directly. Knowledge distillation works the same way: you train a small model to copy the output behavior of a large model, without needing access to the large model's internal weights.
how_it_works: Start with a trained teacher model (e.g., GPT-4). Generate predictions from it—either full logit distributions (classical distillation) or natural language responses (response distillation). Train the student model on these soft targets instead of hard labels. The student never sees the teacher's weights, only its outputs. During inference, you deploy only the student, which is 10–100x smaller and faster. Common variants: logit distillation matches probability distributions at each token; response distillation uses high-quality text generations; off-policy distillation trains the student on teacher-generated rollouts.
why_it_matters: You get 85–95% of a large model's quality at 1–5% of its inference cost. For high-traffic endpoints where GPT-4 is prohibitively expensive, distilling an open-source model against GPT-4 traces is the standard playbook. Teams ship faster and hit budget targets while maintaining acceptable accuracy.
example: Zephyr (7B) was distilled from Mistral 7B by training it on GPT-4 responses using supervised fine-tuning and DPO. Anthropic's Claude Haiku and OpenAI's gpt-4o-mini are widely believed to be distilled from their larger siblings. A startup with 10M daily requests can reduce GPT-4 costs by 80% by distilling a fine-tuned 7B model, then A/B testing quality on 10% of traffic before full rollout.
pitfalls: Distillation doesn't preserve everything—the student often loses long-context ability, rare-skill performance, and safety guardrails the teacher has.
Teacher quality matters more than teacher size; a bad teacher produces a bad student, no matter how much you distill.
Distilled models sometimes overfit to the teacher's specific output style, reducing diversity and failing on out-of-distribution tasks.
Soft targets help, but if the teacher's logit distribution is overconfident or miscalibrated, the student inherits those errors.
when_use: Use distillation when you have a high-traffic production endpoint, a large teacher model you can afford in offline batch runs, and acceptable accuracy loss of 5–15%.
when_avoid: Avoid distillation if you need exact teacher behavior (e.g., safety-critical reasoning), have minimal traffic (inference cost doesn't matter), or lack a reliable teacher baseline.

faq.q: Is distillation the same as copying a model?
faq.a: No. Distillation trains a new small model on the teacher's outputs; it never accesses or copies the teacher's weights. The student learns to mimic behavior, not steal parameters.

faq.q: How much quality do you lose when distilling?
faq.a: Typical loss is 5–15% in benchmark scores (MMLU, GSM8K), depending on student size and teacher quality. Real-world perception loss is often smaller because users tolerate minor accuracy drops if latency halves.

faq.q: Can you distill an open-source model into a smaller open-source model?
faq.a: Yes. Zephyr distilled Mistral 7B against GPT-4 responses. You can distill any teacher (closed or open) into any student (closed or open), provided you can afford the teacher's inference cost during data generation.

faq.q: What's the difference between distillation and fine-tuning?
faq.a: Fine-tuning trains on labeled data or SFT samples. Distillation trains on a teacher's predictions (soft targets). Distillation preserves the teacher's reasoning; fine-tuning teaches new tasks or styles.

faq.q: Does distillation work for multimodal models?
faq.a: Yes, but it's less common. You can distill LLaVA or CLIP models by training smaller students on teacher embeddings or logits, though multimodal distillation is still research-heavy.

meta_title: Distillation (Knowledge Distillation) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Train a small model to mimic a large model's outputs, trading minor quality loss for 5–50x inference cost reduction.

schema_jsonld.name: Distillation (Knowledge Distillation)
