title: DoRA (Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation)
tldr: LoRA variant splitting weight updates into magnitude and direction, closing gap to full fine-tuning.
plain_english: DoRA treats weight changes the way the math actually works: some weights shift a lot (magnitude), others shift slightly (direction). Instead of LoRA touching the entire weight matrix, DoRA learns magnitude separately and applies LoRA only to direction. You get better quality than LoRA without paying extra at inference time.
how_it_works: Start with a pretrained weight matrix W. Decompose it into a magnitude vector m and a direction matrix V, so W = m · V. During training, freeze m and V initially, then apply LoRA's low-rank update only to V while learning m independently through gradient descent. At the end, merge magnitude and direction back together. The split mirrors how full fine-tuning actually redistributes weight: magnitudes change more variably, directions stay relatively stable. No extra parameters at inference—you merge everything back into the original weight shape.
why_it_matters: DoRA consistently matches or beats LoRA on benchmarks (GLUE, SuperGLUE, question answering) with minimal training overhead. The quality gap to full fine-tuning shrinks without requiring more VRAM or slowing inference, making it a practical upgrade for teams already using LoRA.
example: A team fine-tuning Llama 2 for domain-specific customer support swaps LoRA for DoRA: same 8 GPU training setup, same merging step into a single weight file for deployment, but accuracy on out-of-domain questions improves by 2–3% because the method respects how real weight updates behave.
pitfalls: Decomposition adds a hyperparameter: initializing magnitude relative to original weights—poor init kills convergence.
Training cost is not free; magnitude learning adds per-step gradient updates, partially offsetting LoRA's speed advantage.
Merging is mandatory for inference; you cannot easily swap LoRA adapters at runtime like you can with unmerged LoRA.
Empirical gains vary by task and scale; very small models or very short sequences may not show consistent improvement over LoRA.
when_use: Use DoRA when you're already committed to LoRA, have a clear quality metric, and can tolerate slightly longer training to close the fine-tuning gap.
when_avoid: Avoid DoRA if you need adapter swapping at runtime, are training tiny models under 1B parameters, or must optimize for absolute minimum training wall-clock time.

faq.q: How is DoRA different from LoRA?
faq.a: LoRA updates a weight matrix W via a low-rank product. DoRA first decomposes W into magnitude and direction, then applies LoRA only to direction while learning magnitude separately. This mirrors how full fine-tuning redistributes weights and typically closes 50–70% of the LoRA-to-full-finetune quality gap.

faq.q: Does DoRA add inference latency?
faq.a: No. After training, you merge magnitude and direction back into a single weight matrix, identical to LoRA's merge step. Inference cost is unchanged.

faq.q: Can I use DoRA with existing LoRA tools?
faq.a: DoRA is supported in the PEFT library (Hugging Face) as of late 2024. You swap the method flag from 'lora' to 'dora' in config; merging and inference pipelines remain the same.

faq.q: What if my training is already slow?
faq.a: DoRA's magnitude learning adds ~5–15% wall-clock overhead compared to LoRA. If training speed is your bottleneck, test on a dev set first; quality gain may not justify the cost for your task.

faq.q: Does DoRA work better on all model sizes?
faq.a: DoRA shows consistent gains on medium-to-large models (7B+) and benchmarks like GLUE. On very small models (<1B), LoRA may be sufficient; gains are less clear.

meta_title: DoRA (Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: LoRA variant splitting weight updates into magnitude and direction, closing gap to full fine-tuning.

schema_jsonld.name: DoRA (Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation)
schema_jsonld.description: LoRA variant splitting weight updates into magnitude and direction, closing gap to full fine-tuning.

title: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)
tldr: Train language models on preference pairs without a separate reward model or PPO.
plain_english: DPO is a shortcut for aligning language models to human preferences. Instead of training a separate reward model and then using complex reinforcement learning, you directly teach the model to prefer one response over another by comparing them side-by-side. Think of it like grading papers by saying "this essay is better than that one" rather than assigning numerical scores first.
how_it_works: You provide training examples as (prompt, preferred_response, rejected_response) triplets. DPO derives a closed-form loss function that directly optimizes the language model's likelihood ratio: it increases probability for the chosen response and decreases it for the rejected one, while a KL regularization term (controlled by β) keeps the model from drifting too far from its original behavior. The math avoids training a separate reward model, storing rollout buffers, or running PPO updates—just gradient descent on your preference dataset.
why_it_matters: DPO reduced the engineering and compute cost of alignment by 10–100x. Teams without RLHF infrastructure can now ship preference-tuned models in days instead of weeks. Most modern open-weight instruct models (Llama 3, Mistral, Zephyr) use DPO or variants, making it the practical standard for post-training.
example: A team fine-tunes Mistral 7B on 10,000 (prompt, chosen, rejected) pairs sourced from ChatGPT comparisons using Axolotl. They set β=0.1, train for one epoch on 8 A100s, and ship the result in 6 hours. The resulting model matches or beats the original on MMLU and AlpacaEval while rejecting jailbreak attempts more reliably.
pitfalls: DPO requires high-quality preference data; garbage pairs degrade the model worse than no fine-tuning.
Setting β too high (>1.0) collapses response diversity—the model becomes repetitive and boring.
DPO is sensitive to label noise; disagreement between annotators on 'chosen vs rejected' compounds quickly.
The closed-form derivation assumes binary preferences; multi-way comparisons need variants like KTO or IPO.
when_use: Use DPO when you have thousands of preference pairs, want faster iteration than RLHF, lack a reward model, or need reproducible alignment without RL instability.
when_avoid: Avoid DPO if you have weak preference data, need to optimize for multiple competing objectives, or depend on a pre-trained reward model as ground truth.

faq.q: How is DPO different from RLHF?
faq.a: RLHF trains a reward model first, then uses PPO to optimize the policy against it. DPO skips the reward model and reward function entirely, deriving a loss that directly optimizes preference pairs. DPO is simpler, faster, and more stable; RLHF can optimize for arbitrary reward functions but requires more infrastructure.

faq.q: Do I need a reward model to use DPO?
faq.a: No. DPO's core advantage is that it works without a separate reward model. You only need prompt–chosen–rejected triplets.

faq.q: What is the beta parameter in DPO?
faq.a: β controls the strength of KL regularization—how much the model is penalized for diverging from its original behavior. Lower β (0.05–0.1) allows bigger shifts in preference; higher β (>1.0) keeps the model closer to baseline but can reduce diversity.

faq.q: Can DPO work with noisy preference labels?
faq.a: Some; it's more forgiving than supervised learning but still degrades with high disagreement rates. If >30% of your pairs are mislabeled, consider filtering or relabeling before training.

faq.q: Is DPO better than SFT on the same data?
faq.a: Yes, if the data is structured as preferences (chosen vs rejected). DPO teaches the model to discriminate; SFT just memorizes the chosen response. On standard benchmarks, DPO typically outperforms SFT by 2–5 percentage points.

meta_title: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Train language models on preference pairs without a separate reward model or PPO.

schema_jsonld.name: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)

title: Evol-Instruct
tldr: Iteratively transform simple instructions into harder ones, then generate responses to build complex synthetic training data.
plain_english: Evol-Instruct takes basic instructions and rewrites them progressively—adding constraints, requiring deeper reasoning, complicating the input—until they become multi-step tasks. You then generate high-quality answers to these harder instructions. It's like taking a simple recipe and gradually turning it into a challenging one by adding dietary restrictions, precision requirements, and ingredient substitutions.
how_it_works: Start with seed instructions (often simple). Feed each to an LLM with explicit evolution operators: add constraints, increase reasoning depth, complicate input description, expand scope, or concretize abstract terms. Each generation produces a harder instruction from the previous one. Once you have a chain of evolved instructions, generate responses to the hardest ones in each chain. This creates a dataset skewed toward complex, multi-step tasks rather than the simple Q&A pairs that basic synthetic methods produce. The method was introduced in WizardLM (Xu et al. 2023) and later applied in WizardCoder and Orca-style distillation.
why_it_matters: Quality and diversity of training data matter as much as quantity. Evol-Instruct showed that instruction-tuned models trained on progressively harder synthetic examples outperform those trained on flat, simple instruction sets. This shifts the focus from data size to data complexity as a lever for model capability.
example: Start with: 'Explain photosynthesis.' Evolve to: 'Explain photosynthesis, comparing it to cellular respiration, then describe how C3 and C4 plants differ in this process, accounting for water scarcity.' Generate a detailed response. Repeat with other operators (add a constraint: 'for a 10-year-old'; add breadth: 'include industrial applications'). This chain produces a training example that teaches nuance and multi-step reasoning rather than surface recall.
pitfalls: Evolved instructions can become artificially convoluted—adding constraints that don't reflect real-world tasks wastes generation budget.
LLMs may degrade instruction quality after multiple evolution steps; quality drift compounds across generations.
Generating responses to all evolved instructions (not just the hardest) inflates dataset size without proportional benefit.
Overreliance on evolution operators that your base LLM handles poorly (e.g., reasoning depth if the model is weak) can produce unhelpful examples.
when_use: Use Evol-Instruct when you need instruction-tuned models to handle complex, multi-constraint tasks and have a small seed set of simple instructions you can iteratively transform.
when_avoid: Avoid when your target tasks are simple or domain-specific in ways that generic evolution operators don't capture, or when you already have high-quality human-labeled data.

faq.q: Is Evol-Instruct the same as Self-Instruct?
faq.a: No. Self-Instruct generates new instructions from scratch using templates and diversity criteria. Evol-Instruct transforms existing instructions via explicit hardening operators. Evol-Instruct assumes you start with simple seeds and evolve them; Self-Instruct bootstraps from nothing.

faq.q: Do I need to use all evolved instructions or just the hardest ones?
faq.a: Research shows generating responses only to the hardest instructions in each chain (the final evolution step) is most efficient. Using intermediate steps adds volume but not proportional quality gains.

faq.q: What evolution operators work best?
faq.a: The five main operators (add constraints, deepen reasoning, complicate input, increase breadth, concretize) all help, but your results depend on whether your base LLM can reliably follow and answer the evolved instructions. Test and prune operators that degrade quality.

faq.q: Can Evol-Instruct replace human annotation?
faq.a: For broad instruction-tuning, it reduces annotation cost significantly. But if you need domain-specific correctness (medicine, law) or creative tasks, human review of evolved instructions is still recommended.

faq.q: How many evolution steps should I use?
faq.a: Typically 3–5 steps per seed. Beyond that, instructions often become nonsensical or require too much computational budget. Quality tends to plateau after 4–5 iterations.

title: Few-Shot Learning
tldr: Teaching a model to perform a task by including 2–10 examples in your prompt instead of retraining it.
plain_english: Few-shot learning means showing the model a handful of input-output pairs in your prompt and letting it infer the pattern. It's like showing someone 3 examples of how you format dates, then they apply the same style to new dates—no instruction manual needed. The model adapts its behavior on the fly using context alone, without any weight updates.
how_it_works: You embed 2–10 labeled examples (input-output pairs) directly in your prompt before asking the model to handle a new input. The model attends to these examples during inference and recognizes the implicit task structure. For classification, you might show 3 examples of spam vs. non-spam emails. For extraction, you show 4 instances of pulling dates from text. The model then applies that learned pattern to your actual query. This works because large transformer models can shift their behavior based on in-context examples without modifying any parameters.
why_it_matters: Few-shot prompting is the fastest iteration path for task-specific output. Instead of spending weeks fine-tuning (expensive, requires labeled data, slows deployment), you add a few examples to your prompt and see immediate improvement. Works especially well for classification, extraction, and formatting tasks where patterns are obvious from examples.
example: A customer support team using Claude routes 100 tickets daily by intent. Instead of fine-tuning, they add 5 examples of classified tickets to their system prompt: 'Example: "My password won't reset" → billing-issue. Example: "My card declined" → billing-issue.' Claude now routes 92% correctly. When the intent list changes, they swap examples in seconds.
pitfalls: Adding too many examples (>10) can confuse the model or waste context window; quality and diversity of examples matter more than quantity.
Examples that don't reflect your actual data distribution will teach the model the wrong pattern.
Putting examples in the wrong order or using inconsistent formatting breaks the model's ability to recognize the task structure.
Assuming few-shot will work equally well for all tasks—it fails on novel reasoning tasks where the pattern isn't obvious from examples alone.
when_use: Use few-shot learning when you need quick iteration on a classification, extraction, or formatting task and adding 3–5 good examples improves accuracy noticeably.
when_avoid: Avoid few-shot when the task requires reasoning steps not evident from examples, when you have massive labeled datasets and want to optimize further, or when latency is critical and you need to minimize context.

faq.q: What's the difference between few-shot and zero-shot learning?
faq.a: Zero-shot gives the model only instructions (no examples) and relies on pretraining knowledge. Few-shot adds 2–10 concrete examples so the model learns the task pattern from context. Few-shot generally produces better results but costs a bit more in tokens.

faq.q: How many examples do I actually need?
faq.a: Start with 3–5 high-quality examples. More isn't always better; beyond 10 examples you usually see diminishing returns and waste context. Quality matters more than quantity—pick diverse, representative examples.

faq.q: Does few-shot learning work for complex reasoning tasks?
faq.a: It struggles with multi-step reasoning where the logic isn't obvious from examples alone. For those tasks, combine few-shot with chain-of-thought prompting (show step-by-step reasoning in your examples).

faq.q: Is few-shot learning cheaper than fine-tuning?
faq.a: Yes. Few-shot costs only the tokens for your examples in each request. Fine-tuning requires labeled data collection, training compute, and storage. If a few examples fix your accuracy, few-shot wins.

faq.q: Can I use few-shot with any LLM?
faq.a: Yes, any modern LLM (GPT, Claude, Llama, Mistral) supports in-context learning. Larger models generally perform better with fewer examples.

meta_title: Few-Shot Learning — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Teaching a model to perform a task by including 2–10 examples in your prompt instead of retraining it.

schema_jsonld.name: Few-Shot Learning

title: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)
tldr: Training objective that predicts missing text between prefix and suffix, enabling code completion inside files.
plain_english: FIM teaches a model to complete code in the middle of an existing file, not just at the end. Instead of predicting what comes next, the model learns to fill gaps—like when you're editing a function and need suggestions between existing lines. This is why modern code assistants work inside your editor, not just at the end of your document.
how_it_works: During training, documents are split into [prefix, middle, suffix] and rearranged as [prefix, <suffix_token>, suffix, <middle_token>, middle]. The model learns to predict the middle section given both what came before and what comes after. At inference, an IDE sends the prefix (code above cursor), suffix (code below cursor), and special tokens; the model generates the missing middle. This rearrangement trains a causal language model—which normally only sees past tokens—to handle bidirectional context within the same forward pass.
why_it_matters: FIM is the core enabling technique for Copilot-style in-file code completion. Without it, models can only predict at document boundaries. Every production code model (CodeLlama, StarCoder, DeepSeek-Coder, Qwen-Coder) implements FIM because it's essential for IDE integration and developer velocity.
example: A developer in VS Code writes a Python function with the cursor in the middle. The extension sends context: [def calculate_total(items):, <SUFFIX>, return total, <MIDDLE>]. The FIM-trained model sees both the function signature above and the return statement below, then generates the missing loop logic correctly—something impossible with left-to-right-only models.
pitfalls: FIM training requires careful token rearrangement during preprocessing; off-by-one errors in suffix ordering cause the model to ignore context entirely.
Models undertrained on FIM may generate context-oblivious code when suffix tokens are present, treating them as noise instead of constraints.
FIM works best when prefix and suffix are both substantial; single-line suffix context often produces lower-quality completions than left-to-right models.
Inference latency increases because special tokens expand the prompt, reducing effective batch size in systems with fixed token budgets.
when_use: Use FIM when building IDE plugins, in-editor completion, or any system where users expect suggestions in the middle of existing code—not just at boundaries.
when_avoid: Avoid FIM for left-to-right generation tasks (text summarization, chat) or when you lack bidirectional context; a standard causal model is simpler and faster.

faq.q: What is fill-in-the-middle training?
faq.a: FIM is a training objective where documents are rearranged as [prefix, <suffix_token>, suffix, <middle_token>, middle] so the model learns to predict missing text between existing code. This trains a causal model to act as if it sees both past and future context.

faq.q: How is FIM different from normal code completion?
faq.a: Normal code completion predicts the next token after the cursor (left-to-right only). FIM predicts tokens in the middle of existing code by conditioning on both the prefix above and suffix below, enabling in-file completions.

faq.q: Do all code models support FIM?
faq.a: Most production code models do: CodeLlama, StarCoder, DeepSeek-Coder, and Qwen-Coder all support FIM. Some smaller or older models may not, so check the model card.

faq.q: Why is FIM necessary for IDE plugins?
faq.a: IDEs have code both above and below the cursor. FIM lets the model use that full context, producing relevant completions in the middle of functions—something left-to-right models cannot do well.

faq.q: Can I use FIM with a base model that wasn't trained on FIM?
faq.a: Not directly—the model won't understand the special tokens and will treat the suffix as noise. You'd need to fine-tune or use a model explicitly trained with FIM tokens.

meta_title: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) — Code Completion Training Technique | OSS AI Hub

schema_jsonld.name: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)

title: Fine-tuning
tldr: Training an existing model on your specific data to optimize it for your particular task.
plain_english: Fine-tuning takes a pre-trained model (like Llama or Mistral) and continues training it on your own dataset instead of starting from scratch. Think of it like taking a general-purpose car and customizing the engine for a specific race. The model's weights adapt to your domain—medical data, your codebase, customer service tickets—making it faster and cheaper than training from zero.
how_it_works: You start with a base model that already understands language or code. You prepare a task-specific dataset (hundreds to thousands of examples). During fine-tuning, the optimizer adjusts the model's weights using backpropagation on your data, typically with a lower learning rate than pretraining. The model learns patterns specific to your domain. After training completes, the updated weights are saved and deployed. This process takes hours to days on a single GPU, not months on clusters.
why_it_matters: Fine-tuned models outperform general models on your specific task without the millions of dollars and compute needed for pretraining. Companies use fine-tuning to build proprietary models that understand their domain, reduce hallucinations for specialized use cases, and achieve better latency and cost efficiency than prompt-only approaches.
example: A healthcare startup fine-tunes Mistral on 5,000 anonymized clinical notes to build a medical diagnosis assistant. The base model saw general text; the fine-tuned version learns to recognize symptoms, drug interactions, and clinical terminology. It achieves 89% accuracy on internal benchmarks versus 64% for the base model, and costs 3x less per inference because it's smaller and more focused.
pitfalls: Catastrophic forgetting: fine-tuning on a narrow dataset can degrade the model's general knowledge; use techniques like LoRA or blend training data.
Overfitting with small datasets: models memorize your data instead of learning patterns; collect at least 500–1000 quality examples.
Ignoring data distribution: if your fine-tuning set differs greatly from deployment data, performance drops; validate on held-out test sets.
Underestimating compute cost: full fine-tuning of large models requires significant VRAM; use quantization or parameter-efficient methods like LoRA if resources are constrained.
when_use: Use fine-tuning when you have 500+ task-specific examples, need domain-specific accuracy gains, or want to reduce inference latency by using a smaller customized model.
when_avoid: Avoid fine-tuning if you have <100 examples (use in-context learning instead), need real-time adaptation (use retrieval or prompt engineering), or cannot afford to store multiple model copies.

faq.q: What's the difference between fine-tuning and in-context learning?
faq.a: In-context learning passes examples in the prompt each time you query; fine-tuning updates the model's weights once and uses them for all future queries. Fine-tuning is more permanent and efficient for repetitive tasks; in-context learning is faster to set up but slower and more expensive per query.

faq.q: How much data do I need to fine-tune a model?
faq.a: Effective fine-tuning typically requires 500–5,000 quality examples for good results. With fewer than 100 examples, prompt engineering or retrieval usually outperforms fine-tuning. More examples (10,000+) help with generalization, but diminishing returns apply.

faq.q: Can I fine-tune without a GPU?
faq.a: Yes, but slowly. Methods like LoRA and quantization (QLoRA) reduce memory requirements significantly. A single GPU (A100 or H100) fine-tunes most models in hours; CPU training takes days. Cloud platforms like Lambda or RunPod offer hourly GPU rental.

faq.q: Does fine-tuning permanently change the model?
faq.a: Yes. Fine-tuning updates the model's weights and saves them. You can keep both the original and fine-tuned versions, or use techniques like LoRA adapters to store only the differences, reducing storage overhead.

faq.q: Will fine-tuning make my model forget general knowledge?
faq.a: Possibly, if your training data is very narrow or you train too long. Catastrophic forgetting is real. Mitigate it by mixing domain-specific data with general examples, using early stopping, or applying techniques like LoRA that constrain weight changes.

meta_title: Fine-tuning — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Training an existing model on your specific data to optimize it for your particular task.

schema_jsonld.name: Fine-tuning
schema_jsonld.description: Training an existing model on your specific data to optimize it for your particular task.

title: FP8 Training
tldr: Train neural networks using 8-bit floating-point precision on H100+ hardware, roughly doubling throughput over bf16 with per-tensor scaling.
plain_english: FP8 uses fewer bits than standard 16-bit precision (bf16), fitting more data in memory and moving it faster between chips. Think of it like storing numbers in a smaller suitcase—you fit more, but you have to be careful which numbers go where. The catch: you need special hardware (Nvidia H100 or newer) and must choose which operations use FP8 versus staying in bf16.
how_it_works: FP8 defines two formats: E4M3 (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa bits) for forward activations and weights, and E5M2 (5 exponent, 2 mantissa) for gradients during backprop. You assign a per-tensor scaling factor computed from statistics of activations and weights, then quantize before compute and dequantize after. Most layers run in FP8; critical layers (like layer norms or softmax) typically stay in bf16. Transformer Engine and MS-AMP automate this hybrid approach. The 2× throughput gain comes from faster tensor ops and reduced memory bandwidth.
why_it_matters: Frontier model training budgets are dominated by GPU time. FP8 cuts that cost roughly in half when it works, directly reducing wall-clock time and GPU hours for 2024–2026-era 100B+ parameter models. Quality loss is negligible if scaling factors are chosen correctly, making FP8 a standard practice for large-scale pretraining.
example: A team training a 70B-parameter model on 8 H100s finds that switching matmul operations to FP8 via Transformer Engine reduces training time from 100 to ~55 hours per epoch, while final checkpoint MMLU scores remain within 0.1%. They keep layer norms and attention softmax in bf16 to preserve numerical stability.
pitfalls: Scaling factor staleness: if you compute scales once per epoch, activations can drift and lose precision; recompute scales every few steps.
Layer norm instability: FP8 quantization in softmax or layer norm can explode loss; keep these in bf16 or use higher-precision intermediate buffers.
Gradient underflow: E5M2's narrow range for small gradients can cause zeros; monitor gradient statistics and adjust scales if needed.
Hardware lock-in: FP8 training requires H100, H200, B100, or newer; if you switch to older GPUs, you lose the speed gains.
when_use: Use FP8 when training >50B parameter models on H100+ hardware for ≥7 days, or when you need to reduce training GPU hours and can afford a small engineering effort to tune per-layer precision.
when_avoid: Avoid FP8 if you're training on older hardware (A100, V100), if your model uses custom operations not optimized for FP8, or if convergence is already fragile (e.g., very high learning rate or unstable optimizer config).

faq.q: What is the difference between FP8 E4M3 and E5M2?
faq.a: E4M3 (4 exponent, 3 mantissa bits) stores numbers with more precision but narrower range; use it for weights and activations. E5M2 (5 exponent, 2 mantissa) has more range but less precision; use it for gradients which span larger magnitudes during backprop.

faq.q: Can you train with FP8 on A100 GPUs?
faq.a: No. FP8 tensor ops require Nvidia Hopper (H100) or newer. A100s don't have FP8 hardware support, so the quantization overhead outweighs any bandwidth gain.

faq.q: Does FP8 training give the same final model quality as bf16?
faq.a: Yes, when scaling factors are tuned correctly. Studies show negligible difference in final MMLU, perplexity, or downstream task performance, though convergence curves may be slightly noisier.

faq.q: How do you choose which layers to keep in bf16 vs FP8?
faq.a: Layer norms, softmax, and embeddings typically stay in bf16 because their narrow numerical range and normalization steps are sensitive to quantization. Transformer Engine makes this choice automatically; manual tuning is rarely needed.

faq.q: How often should you update FP8 scaling factors?
faq.a: Every few training steps (5–50, depending on dynamics). Stale scales lead to underflow or overflow; Transformer Engine recomputes them automatically based on activation statistics.

meta_title: FP8 Training — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Train neural networks using 8-bit floating-point precision on H100+ hardware, roughly doubling throughput over bf16 with per-tensor scaling.

schema_jsonld.name: FP8 Training

title: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel)
tldr: PyTorch technique that splits model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across GPUs to train models larger than one device's memory.
plain_english: Think of FSDP like distributing chapters of a textbook across students: each GPU owns different pieces of the model, gathers only what it needs during computation, then discards it. This lets you train massive models (70B+) on clusters without buying a single giant GPU. PyTorch's built-in version mimics what frameworks like DeepSpeed do, but stays within the standard PyTorch API.
how_it_works: FSDP divides model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state into shards, assigning each shard to a different GPU. During the forward pass, each GPU gathers the parameter shards it needs for its layers, computes, then frees them. Gradients are computed locally and reduced across workers. FSDP-2 refines this with per-parameter sharding for finer control and better composability with tensor parallelism. Communication overhead is reduced by overlapping computation and all-gather operations.
why_it_matters: FSDP is PyTorch's default distributed-training API for large-scale training. Without it, 70B+ models require custom frameworks or manual sharding logic. It lets teams use standard PyTorch tools and debugging practices while scaling across clusters, cutting development time and reducing engineering complexity.
example: A team training a 70B parameter model on 8 H100 GPUs uses FSDP to split parameters across devices. Each GPU holds ~8.75B parameters. During forward/backward, GPUs coordinate to gather needed shards, compute, and free memory. This fits where a single GPU (80GB) would run out of space. Training runs in weeks instead of months because communication is pipelined with computation.
pitfalls: All-gather communication can become a bottleneck if sharding rank is too high or inter-GPU bandwidth is limited (e.g., PCIe vs. NVLink).
FSDP-2's per-parameter sharding adds overhead for small models; full-model sharding works better below 10B parameters.
Incorrect wrapping of submodules causes redundant parameter duplication; FSDP requires explicit module boundaries.
Mixing FSDP with ZeRO-optimized AdamW or custom optimizers may cause gradient synchronization bugs; test carefully.
when_use: Use FSDP when training models larger than a single GPU's memory, you're already using PyTorch, and you want built-in distributed training without external frameworks.
when_avoid: Avoid FSDP on small models (<1B parameters) or single-GPU setups where the communication overhead exceeds the memory savings; use gradient checkpointing or LoRA instead.

faq.q: How is FSDP different from DeepSpeed ZeRO?
faq.a: FSDP is PyTorch's native implementation of the same concept. Both shard parameters, gradients, and optimizer state, but FSDP integrates directly into PyTorch's autograd and device management, avoiding external dependencies.

faq.q: Does FSDP work with mixed precision training?
faq.a: Yes. FSDP supports FP32, FP16, and BF16 mixed precision. You combine it with torch.amp or manual scaling; precision is controlled independently of sharding strategy.

faq.q: What's the difference between FSDP and FSDP-2?
faq.a: FSDP-2 introduces per-parameter sharding instead of full-module sharding, allowing finer granularity and better composability with tensor parallelism. It's the newer API but requires PyTorch 2.4+.

faq.q: Can I use FSDP with 3D parallelism?
faq.a: Yes. FSDP (data parallelism) is typically combined with tensor parallelism (within-layer sharding) and pipeline parallelism (layer-stage sharding) for massive models.

faq.q: How much faster is FSDP than gradient accumulation alone?
faq.a: FSDP is memory-efficient, not faster. It trades communication for memory; you fit larger batches or models, which can speed training by letting you use larger learning rates, but communication overhead may add 10–30% latency per iteration.

meta_title: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: PyTorch technique that splits model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across GPUs to train models larger than one device's memory.

schema_jsonld.name: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel)

title: Full Fine-Tuning
tldr: Update every parameter in a pretrained model during training — maximum quality at maximum compute cost.
plain_english: Full fine-tuning means running standard gradient descent on every weight and neuron in your base model, not just a small adapter on top. Think of it like retraining the entire foundation of a house rather than just redecorating one room. You get the most expressive results, but you pay dearly in memory and GPU time.
how_it_works: During training, backpropagation computes gradients for all parameters. An optimizer like Adam tracks momentum and variance for each parameter — for a 70B model this requires ~500GB of optimizer state alone, forcing you to use multi-GPU setups with gradient accumulation or tensor parallelism. Each training step updates every weight. The result is a fully specialized model that can adapt to tasks requiring broad behavioral shifts, but the cost scales linearly with model size and dataset size.
why_it_matters: Full fine-tuning is the baseline you measure other methods against. Since LoRA and QLoRA achieve within 1% of full fine-tuning quality on most tasks while using a fraction of the compute, justifying full fine-tuning requires either unusually large datasets, extreme task divergence from pretraining, or specific performance requirements that ruled out cheaper alternatives.
example: A research team fine-tuning a 70B model on a proprietary 500M-token finance corpus might use full fine-tuning if they have an 8-GPU A100 cluster and need the model to reason about domain-specific accounting rules that lightweight adapters can't capture. After 3 weeks of training, they measure a 4% quality lift over LoRA. The cost: $50K in compute and engineering time spent on infrastructure rather than on LoRA, which would have cost $5K.
pitfalls: Overfitting and catastrophic forgetting happen faster with full fine-tuning — all parameters can shift, eroding pretraining knowledge; aggressive regularization (low learning rate, early stopping) becomes critical.
Memory scaling defeats most setups: a 70B model with Adam optimizer state and gradients consumes 1.5TB+ on a single GPU, forcing complex multi-GPU orchestration or aggressive quantization trade-offs.
Marginal gains don't justify cost: on standard benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K), full fine-tuning often improves only 0–2% over LoRA, too small to offset the infrastructure burden.
Hyperparameter tuning becomes expensive: learning rate, weight decay, and batch size interact differently across all parameters, requiring more trial runs than adapter-based methods.
when_use: Use full fine-tuning when you have a very large, domain-specific dataset (500M+ tokens), the task requires the model to internalize entirely new reasoning patterns, and you have budget and infrastructure for multi-GPU training.
when_avoid: Avoid full fine-tuning for small datasets (<10M tokens), when you must train on a single GPU, when inference cost matters more than training cost, or when LoRA/QLoRA has already met your quality bar.

faq.q: Is full fine-tuning always better than LoRA?
faq.a: No. On most tasks with standard datasets, full fine-tuning improves quality by 0–2% over LoRA while costing 10–50× more compute. Full fine-tuning wins only when your dataset is very large or your task requires wholesale behavior change.

faq.q: How much memory does full fine-tuning need?
faq.a: A 70B model with Adam optimizer state, gradients, and activations requires ~1.5TB on a single GPU. Most teams use 8-GPU A100s with gradient accumulation or tensor parallelism to fit within HBM limits.

faq.q: Can I do full fine-tuning on a single GPU?
faq.a: Only on small models (<7B) and typically with aggressive quantization (8-bit or lower) and severe batch-size restrictions. Most practitioners use multi-GPU setups or accept the quality/cost trade-off of LoRA.

faq.q: Does full fine-tuning destroy the pretrained model?
faq.a: Yes, if not regularized carefully. All parameters can shift, erasing useful pretraining knowledge. Use low learning rates (1e-5 to 5e-5), early stopping, and weight decay to preserve base model capability.

faq.q: What's the difference between full fine-tuning and continued pretraining?
faq.a: Continued pretraining trains on raw unlabeled text; full fine-tuning trains on labeled task data. Continued pretraining updates parameters gradually and is safer for knowledge; full fine-tuning optimizes for task performance and risks overfitting.

meta_title: Full Fine-Tuning — Definition, Costs & When to Use | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Update every parameter in a pretrained model during training — maximum quality at maximum compute cost.

schema_jsonld.name: Full Fine-Tuning
schema_jsonld.description: Update every parameter in a pretrained model during training — maximum quality at maximum compute cost.

title: GPTQ (Post-Training Quantization)
tldr: Layer-wise post-training quantization that compresses LLM weights to 3–4 bits with minimal quality loss.
plain_english: GPTQ shrinks a trained model's weight values from full precision (32 bits each) down to 3–4 bits per weight, cutting model size by 8–10×. It does this one layer at a time, solving a math problem that minimizes how much the layer's output changes when you round the weights. Think of it like photographing a painting at lower resolution—you lose detail, but the scene stays recognizable.
how_it_works: GPTQ processes one transformer layer sequentially. For each layer, it finds the rounded weights that keep the layer's output closest to the original using a small calibration dataset (128–256 examples). It handles outlier weights—unusually large values that round poorly—by treating them separately or with adaptive thresholds. This greedy, layer-by-layer approach is faster than full-model optimization and works well in practice because later layers adapt to earlier quantization.
why_it_matters: A 70B-parameter model quantized with GPTQ fits on a single consumer GPU (24 GB VRAM), dropping inference cost and latency. Most open-source 4-bit checkpoints published on Hugging Face use GPTQ or AWQ, making it the de facto standard for production quantization in open-source workflows.
example: A team running Llama 2 70B for customer support inference pays $0.80 per 1M tokens unquantized. After GPTQ quantization to 4-bit, the same model fits on an L40S GPU and costs $0.15 per 1M tokens. Quality drops <2% on typical chat tasks, measured by human eval.
pitfalls: Outlier activation values can cause quantization error to spike; some GPTQ implementations use per-channel scaling, others use row-wise quantization—check your implementation.
Calibration data distribution matters: using random examples from the training set works, but domain-specific data (e.g., code for code models) can reduce drift.
GPTQ is compute-heavy at quantization time (hours for 70B models), but inference is fast; don't re-quantize repeatedly.
Mixing GPTQ with other optimizations (distillation, pruning) can compound error; quantize last in your pipeline.
when_use: Use GPTQ when you need sub-4-bit compression on a tight deadline, have a GPU with 20–40 GB VRAM, and care about generation quality over exact task metrics.
when_avoid: Avoid GPTQ if you need sub-3-bit compression (use vector quantization), require zero retraining (use round-to-nearest), or must preserve exact numerical outputs (e.g., scientific computing).

faq.q: What's the difference between GPTQ and AWQ?
faq.a: Both produce 4-bit models with similar quality. GPTQ minimizes weight-space error; AWQ minimizes activation-space error. AWQ is slightly faster to quantize; GPTQ has broader hardware support. Most teams pick based on available checkpoints.

faq.q: Can I use GPTQ on a model I fine-tuned myself?
faq.a: Yes. Run GPTQ on your fine-tuned checkpoint using a representative calibration set. Fine-tuning after quantization is harder because gradients are discrete; quantize after training.

faq.q: How much faster is a GPTQ model at inference?
faq.a: 2–4× speedup on GPU with optimized kernels (vLLM, ExLLaMA). Speed gain depends on your hardware; older GPUs see less benefit. Batch size also matters: larger batches hide quantization overhead.

faq.q: Does GPTQ work for vision or multimodal models?
faq.a: Yes, technically. But most production use cases are LLMs. Vision models have different outlier patterns; check if your framework supports them (some do, some don't).

faq.q: How do I choose the number of bits for GPTQ?
faq.a: 4-bit is standard (good quality–speed tradeoff). 3-bit works for some tasks but hurts quality noticeably. Test on your eval set; don't assume published numbers transfer to your domain.

meta_description: Layer-wise post-training quantization that compresses LLM weights to 3–4 bits with minimal quality loss.

title: Gradient Accumulation
tldr: Sum gradients over multiple small batches before updating weights, simulating larger batch sizes on limited GPU memory.
plain_english: You have a GPU that fits 16 examples but want to train as if you had 256 at once. Instead of updating weights after each small batch, you run 16 forward-backward passes without updating, add all the gradients together, then update once. This tricks the optimizer into behaving like it saw a bigger picture before deciding where to step.
how_it_works: Process N micro-batches without calling optimizer.step(). Scale each loss by 1/N before backprop so gradients accumulate correctly. After N passes, call optimizer.step() to apply the summed gradient, then zero_grad() to reset. Loss scaling matters: if you forget to divide, you'll apply N times larger updates than intended. Frameworks like Accelerate and DeepSpeed expose an accumulation_steps parameter to automate this.
why_it_matters: Teams with modest GPUs can now match the batch sizes in published papers without buying enterprise hardware. Batch size affects generalization, and many results assume large global batches. Gradient accumulation closes that gap for reproducibility and competitive model quality.
example: A researcher training a 7B LLaMA variant has one A100 (40 GB VRAM) that fits 8 examples per pass. The paper used batch size 512. She sets accumulation_steps=64, runs 64 micro-batches of 8 before each optimizer step, matching the paper's 512-example effective batch. After 2 days of training, her validation loss aligns with the published results.
pitfalls: Batch normalization and layer norm statistics are computed per micro-batch, not the accumulated size; accumulated training won't exactly replicate single-batch training.
Learning-rate schedules keyed to step count fire 64× fewer times with 64-step accumulation, potentially underwarming the model or missing peak learning rates.
Forgetting to scale loss by 1/accumulation_steps causes gradients to be N times too large, destabilizing training or requiring learning-rate cuts to compensate.
Accumulated weight decay or other per-step regularization is applied less often, subtly altering model regularization.
when_use: Use gradient accumulation when your global batch size target exceeds your GPU memory capacity, or when you need to match published results that assume larger batches.
when_avoid: Avoid it if you can fit your full batch in memory, since accumulation adds no benefit and extra complexity; also reconsider if you need to match batch norm or schedule timing exactly.

faq.q: Does gradient accumulation give the same result as training with a large batch?
faq.a: Not exactly. Batch norm and layer norm operate on micro-batch statistics, not the accumulated batch, and learning-rate schedules may differ. Results are close but not identical.

faq.q: How do I scale loss when using gradient accumulation?
faq.a: Divide each micro-batch loss by the accumulation steps before calling backward(). If you accumulate 8 steps, scale by 1/8. This keeps gradient magnitudes consistent.

faq.q: Does gradient accumulation slow down training?
faq.a: Yes, slightly. You're doing more forward-backward passes before each weight update. The tradeoff is fitting larger effective batch sizes on smaller GPUs.

faq.q: Can I use gradient accumulation with distributed training?
faq.a: Yes. Combine it with data parallelism, DeepSpeed ZeRO, or FSDP. Global batch = (local_batch_size × num_gpus × accumulation_steps).

faq.q: What happens if I forget to zero_grad() after optimizer.step()?
faq.a: Gradients keep accumulating across accumulation cycles, so every cycle adds on top of the previous. This breaks training—always zero after stepping.

meta_title: Gradient Accumulation — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: Gradient Checkpointing
tldr: Trade compute for VRAM by recomputing activations during backprop instead of storing them.
plain_english: Normally your GPU stores every intermediate calculation (activation) from the forward pass so backprop can use them. Gradient checkpointing only saves a few checkpoints and recalculates the rest on-demand during backward. It's like keeping mile-marker photos instead of every step—you walk backward retracing each segment, but you need far less storage.
how_it_works: During forward pass, you mark N layers as checkpoints and store only their activations; discard the rest. When backprop runs, you recompute intermediate activations on-the-fly between checkpoints. Memory footprint drops roughly by √N (for N layers). Backward pass takes ~30% longer per step because recomputation adds compute. Enable via `model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()` in Transformers, or configure per-layer in custom training loops. Works with mixed precision and distributed training (FSDP, DeepSpeed ZeRO) to multiply the gains.
why_it_matters: GPU memory is the bottleneck when training large models on consumer or enterprise GPUs. Gradient checkpointing often means the difference between an out-of-memory crash and a successful training run. The compute overhead is usually acceptable because GPU utilization stays high during backward recomputation.
example: A team training a 7B-parameter model on H100 GPUs would hit OOM without checkpointing at batch size 16. Enabling gradient checkpointing lets them fit batch size 32—40% improvement in throughput—because memory use drops by ~√7000≈84×. The 30% per-step slowdown is offset by fewer reductions in batch size and better hardware utilization.
pitfalls: Checkpointing every layer defeats the purpose (memory ≈ full cost); checkpoint sparsely (every 2–4 layers).
Combining with aggressive gradient accumulation masks the memory win—measure actual max-batch-size gains, not raw peak memory.
Recomputation stalls forward progress on GPUs with weak compute relative to memory bandwidth; not always beneficial on older hardware.
Checkpoint selection is problem-dependent; no one stride works for all architectures and batch sizes.
when_use: Use gradient checkpointing when your model hits OOM at your target batch size, or when you want to fit larger models on fixed hardware without buying more GPUs.
when_avoid: Avoid it if you're memory-constrained on compute (e.g., old GPUs with slow arithmetic) or if your bottleneck is already training wall-clock time, not VRAM.

faq.q: What's the difference between gradient checkpointing and gradient accumulation?
faq.a: Gradient accumulation splits batches across steps and sums gradients—it saves memory by reducing batch size. Checkpointing keeps batch size constant but recomputes activations instead of storing them. They're orthogonal: combine both for maximum memory savings.

faq.q: How much slower does checkpointing make training?
faq.a: Roughly 20–40% per-step slowdown depending on hardware, model depth, and checkpoint stride. The net wall-clock impact is often neutral or positive because you can use larger batches, which improves GPU utilization and reduces communication overhead.

faq.q: Can I use gradient checkpointing with LoRA or other adapters?
faq.a: Yes. Checkpointing applies to the base model; adapters (LoRA, prefix tuning) store their own small activations anyway. Enable checkpointing on the frozen base and you get the memory win without affecting adapter training.

faq.q: Does gradient checkpointing work with inference?
faq.a: No. Checkpointing only saves memory during training (backprop). Inference uses the forward pass once and discards activations naturally, so checkpointing adds overhead without benefit.

faq.q: Which layers should I checkpoint?
faq.a: Start by checkpointing every 2–4 transformer layers (not embeddings or final head). Tune the stride upward if memory is still tight, or downward if compute overhead dominates. Empirical tuning on your hardware is fastest.

title: IA3 (Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Inner Activations)
tldr: PEFT method that scales transformer activations with learned per-channel vectors—0.01% trainable parameters.
plain_english: IA3 trains three tiny multiplier vectors per transformer block instead of retraining the whole model. One vector scales keys, one scales values, one scales MLP outputs. You start them at 1 (no change) and adjust them during fine-tuning. It's like adding volume knobs to specific parts of the network rather than rewiring the whole thing.
how_it_works: During fine-tuning, IA3 introduces exactly three learned vectors per transformer block. Each vector has one scalar per activation channel. Vectors initialize to 1.0 (identity transformation). During backprop, only these vectors update—the base model weights stay frozen. Keys, values, and MLP intermediate activations are element-wise multiplied by their corresponding vectors. Total trainable parameters: ~0.01% of model size. Inference speed and latency are identical to the base model.
why_it_matters: IA3 is the smallest PEFT method on disk. A 7B-parameter model adds ~700KB per specialized variant instead of gigabytes. This lets you serve many task-specific adapters with minimal storage overhead—critical when deploying dozens of fine-tuned versions from one base model.
example: A team running a customer support platform fine-tunes Llama 2 once for FAQ answering, complaint routing, and order tracking. With IA3, each adapter is <1MB. They load the base model once and swap 3 vectors per request, keeping memory fixed. With full fine-tuning or LoRA, they'd need 4–10× more disk and RAM per variant.
pitfalls: IA3 assumes the base model already learns useful representations—it only reweights them. On very distant tasks (e.g., chemistry to poetry), it underperforms LoRA.
The three-vector-per-block architecture is rigid. If you need to adjust attention heads differently, IA3 can't—LoRA's rank dimension gives more flexibility.
Initialization to 1.0 means the first few training steps do nothing. Use higher learning rates or longer warmup to compensate.
IA3 vectors are brittle to distribution shift. A vector trained on customer emails may fail on internal docs from the same domain.
when_use: Use IA3 when disk space or deployment footprint is the constraint, the task is close to the base model's training distribution, and you need to serve 10+ specialized variants of one model.
when_avoid: Avoid IA3 if you need state-of-the-art accuracy on out-of-distribution tasks, require per-head control during attention, or are fine-tuning a small model where parameter count isn't a bottleneck.

faq.q: How does IA3 compare to LoRA?
faq.a: LoRA learns low-rank weight updates across the entire model. IA3 learns only channel-wise scalar multipliers for three activation types. IA3 is 10–100× smaller on disk but less expressive for complex task shifts. LoRA offers better accuracy; IA3 offers better storage.

faq.q: Can I use IA3 for instruction tuning?
faq.a: Yes, if your base model already follows instructions well. IA3 reweights existing knowledge rather than adding new reasoning patterns. For base models with poor instruction-following, LoRA or full fine-tuning will be more effective.

faq.q: What is the training overhead of IA3?
faq.a: IA3 training is faster than full fine-tuning but comparable to LoRA—speed depends on your batch size and sequence length, not adapter type. Inference is identical to the base model; no overhead.

faq.q: Do I need to retrain IA3 vectors if I change the base model?
faq.a: Yes. The vectors are tied to the specific architecture and layer structure. Swapping to a different model size or variant requires retraining.

faq.q: Is IA3 suitable for multimodal models?
faq.a: IA3 works on any transformer-based model with distinct key, value, and MLP blocks. Vision transformers and CLIP-style encoders are fair game, though empirical results vary by architecture.

title: Knowledge Editing
tldr: Update specific facts in a model's weights without retraining the entire network.
plain_english: Knowledge editing lets you change what a model knows about a single fact—like swapping out a capital city or a person's job title—by surgically modifying tiny sections of its weights instead of retraining everything. Think of it like correcting one entry in an encyclopedia without reprinting the whole book. The model updates that fact locally, though related knowledge can sometimes shift unexpectedly.
how_it_works: Methods like ROME and MEMIT identify which MLP layers store factual knowledge, then compute small rank-one or rank-batch weight updates targeting a specific (subject, relation, object) triple—for example, (France, capital, Paris). You feed in the fact you want to change, the method locates its neural "address," and updates only those parameters. The edited model outputs the new fact while leaving most other behavior unchanged. Edits work best at small scale; beyond a few thousand edits, changes can ripple into adjacent facts or cause contradictions.
why_it_matters: When retraining a model is too expensive and retrieval-augmented generation is too slow, knowledge editing offers a middle ground: deploy a single model that reflects current facts without full fine-tuning cycles. This is especially useful for LLMs serving time-sensitive domains (news, compliance, product catalogs) where facts change monthly or weekly.
example: A company maintaining a customer-facing chatbot learns that a client's job title changed from VP Engineering to SVP Engineering. Rather than retrain or add a retrieval system, they use a knowledge editing library to update the model's weights in minutes. The change costs nearly nothing and the model immediately reflects the new title in responses about that person.
pitfalls: Edits don't persist across retraining or fine-tuning—the updated weights revert to the original model.
Changing one fact can break adjacent facts: editing 'Paris is the capital of France' may cause hallucination about French geography.
Scaling fails predictably—hundreds of edits usually work; thousands often cause cascading contradictions.
The model can still retrieve old facts through paraphrasing or related questions; edits are not bulletproof.
when_use: Use knowledge editing when you need frequent, small factual updates, retraining is prohibitively expensive, and facts are isolated (no heavy interdependency).
when_avoid: Avoid when you need permanent, bulletproof updates; when facts are deeply interconnected; or when you can afford periodic retraining or lightweight RAG.

faq.q: What is the difference between knowledge editing and fine-tuning?
faq.a: Fine-tuning retrains the entire model on new data; knowledge editing surgically updates a few weights to change one fact. Editing is faster and cheaper but doesn't scale to thousands of changes.

faq.q: Can knowledge editing be combined with RAG?
faq.a: Yes. RAG fetches fresh facts; editing updates the model's internal knowledge. Together they cover both up-to-date retrieval and cases where retrieval is too slow.

faq.q: Do knowledge edits persist after I save the model?
faq.a: Yes, edits are saved in the model weights. But if you later fine-tune or retrain the model on other data, the edits may revert or be overwritten.

faq.q: How many facts can I edit before the model breaks?
faq.a: Roughly hundreds to low thousands, depending on how interconnected facts are. Beyond that, edits reliably cause contradictions and hallucinations.

faq.q: Does knowledge editing work on all model architectures?
faq.a: It works best on dense transformer models with clear MLP layer structure. It is less reliable on mixture-of-experts models or models with unusual architectures.

title: KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization)
tldr: Preference optimization using binary feedback (good/bad) instead of paired comparisons, inspired by prospect theory.
plain_english: You have thumbs-up and thumbs-down signals from users, but not explicit pairs of "this response was better than that one." KTO treats each signal as independent: desirable means optimize toward it, undesirable means optimize away from it. The math is grounded in how humans actually weight losses (they hurt more than gains feel good), so the loss function mirrors real decision-making.
how_it_works: KTO takes a labeled dataset where each example is marked as desirable or undesirable. For desirable examples, it learns to increase their likelihood; for undesirable examples, it learns to decrease theirs. The loss function applies asymmetric weighting inspired by prospect theory—penalizing model drift toward bad outputs more heavily than rewarding drift toward good ones. This works because binary signals (like user ratings) accumulate naturally in production systems without requiring the expensive step of explicitly pairing and ranking responses.
why_it_matters: Preference pairs (DPO, IPO) require curating chosen/rejected samples, which is labor-intensive at scale. KTO accepts unstructured binary feedback—the kind that naturally flows from production systems—and achieves comparable or better performance. This cuts annotation cost and deployment friction when you have volume but not perfect pairwise labels.
example: A team running a customer-facing chat service collects 50,000 thumbs-up/down ratings per week. Instead of hiring annotators to construct preference pairs from these signals, they use KTO to fine-tune directly on each binary label. The model converges in fewer steps than DPO would require on synthetically paired data, and performance matches a DPO baseline trained on hand-curated pairs.
pitfalls: Unbalanced binary labels (90% positive, 10% negative) can cause KTO to overfit toward the majority class; weight or resample your dataset.
Prospect theory's asymmetric weighting assumes loss aversion, but not all domains reflect human decision-making equally—verify it suits your feedback distribution.
Using KTO with noisy or adversarial binary labels (e.g., from users gaming the system) can train the model to optimize for gaming rather than quality.
Confusing binary feedback with preference information; KTO works best when labels reflect absolute quality, not relative rank.
when_use: Use KTO when you have high-volume binary feedback (thumbs/ratings) from production, cannot afford to construct preference pairs, and want faster convergence than DPO on synthetic comparisons.
when_avoid: Avoid KTO if you already have curated preference pairs, need fine-grained ranking signals beyond binary, or suspect your binary labels are heavily corrupted by user bias or adversarial behavior.

faq.q: How is KTO different from DPO?
faq.a: DPO requires paired (chosen, rejected) examples; KTO works with unpaired binary labels. KTO is faster to set up in production and matches DPO performance on comparable tasks.

faq.q: Can I use KTO with imbalanced feedback (mostly positive or mostly negative)?
faq.a: Yes, but you should reweight or resample to avoid the model ignoring the minority class. Check your loss curves and validation metrics to confirm balance.

faq.q: What does prospect theory have to do with training language models?
faq.a: KTO's loss function uses asymmetric weighting (losses matter more than gains) because the Kahneman-Tversky paper showed humans make decisions that way. This weighting empirically improves model alignment when training on human feedback.

faq.q: Do I need to pair my thumbs-up and thumbs-down signals?
faq.a: No. KTO treats each signal independently. If response A gets a thumbs-up and response B gets a thumbs-down, KTO learns from both without requiring you to explicitly link them.

faq.q: Is KTO production-ready?
faq.a: Yes. It's implemented in the TRL library and designed specifically for real-world feedback pipelines. Several teams use it to fine-tune models on live user signals.

title: Lookahead Decoding
tldr: Inference-time decoding speedup using Jacobi iteration to predict and verify multiple tokens in parallel without extra training.
plain_english: Lookahead decoding predicts several candidate token sequences in parallel, then checks which ones the actual model would produce. Think of it like sketching multiple possible next moves in chess simultaneously, then validating the most promising ones against the official rules. No new model weights, no training—pure inference speedup.
how_it_works: You maintain an N-gram pool of candidate continuations at each decoding step. Jacobi iteration (fixed-point parallel updates) refines these candidates without waiting for sequential verification. The original model validates predictions in batch, discarding wrong branches and keeping valid tokens. This parallelization compresses what would normally be sequential decode steps into fewer actual model calls. Speedups compound when combined with other optimizations like batching or KV-cache reuse.
why_it_matters: You get 1.5–2× faster generation for free—no architecture changes, no retraining, no draft model to maintain. Every inference endpoint benefits immediately, making response times tighter without adding infrastructure cost.
example: A chatbot serving 100 concurrent users processes user queries through lookahead decoding. Instead of waiting for the model to generate each token one at a time (100+ model calls per response), lookahead predicts 4 candidate branches in parallel and validates them in 2–3 model calls. Same output quality, 30–40% less wall-clock time per response.
pitfalls: Jacobi iteration can diverge if the N-gram pool is too large or candidates stray too far from true continuations—tune pool size carefully.
Speedups vary significantly by model size and batch size; small models or batch-size-1 see minimal gains.
Interaction with other decode-time optimizations (beam search, constrained decoding) is poorly documented; test empirically.
Memory overhead for maintaining candidate pools can offset speed gains on memory-constrained hardware.
when_use: Use lookahead decoding when you need latency reduction and have room to tune candidate pool size for your workload—especially effective for high-throughput batch inference or when serving many concurrent requests.
when_avoid: Avoid when the model output is highly constrained (tool use, JSON schema) or when token prediction variance is very high—validation overhead can eliminate speedup.

faq.q: How is lookahead decoding different from speculative decoding?
faq.a: Speculative decoding uses a separate draft model to propose tokens; lookahead uses Jacobi iteration on the same model. Lookahead requires no training or draft model, making it simpler to deploy. Both verify proposals in parallel, but lookahead's mechanics are purely mathematical rather than model-based.

faq.q: Do I need to retrain my model for lookahead decoding?
faq.a: No. Lookahead is a pure inference-time technique that works with any pretrained model—no fine-tuning, no new weights, no adapter layers needed.

faq.q: Why isn't lookahead decoding faster than 2× speedup?
faq.a: Jacobi iteration still requires multiple validation passes through the model. The parallel candidate checking is bounded by how many tokens you can reasonably predict ahead without divergence, typically 4–8 tokens per branch.

faq.q: Does lookahead decoding work with constrained decoding or tool use?
faq.a: Compatibility is mixed. Hard constraints (JSON schemas, tool signatures) can invalidate parallel predictions, requiring expensive revalidation. Test on your specific constraints before deploying.

faq.q: What's the memory cost of lookahead decoding?
faq.a: Maintaining N-gram candidate pools adds overhead proportional to pool size and token sequence depth. On GPUs with high VRAM, this is negligible; on constrained devices, it can exceed the latency savings.

meta_title: Lookahead Decoding — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub

title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
tldr: Fine-tune large language models by training only small adapter matrices instead of all weights, using 10–100x less memory.
plain_english: LoRA lets you adapt a pretrained model without retraining it from scratch. Instead of changing billions of parameters, you add tiny trainable matrices to attention layers and only update those. Think of it like adjusting a few control knobs on a massive machine rather than rewiring the whole thing. The original model stays frozen, and at inference time the adapter merges in with zero slowdown.
how_it_works: LoRA inserts low-rank decomposition matrices (typically rank 8–64) into each attention layer of your base model. During training, only these adapter matrices update via backpropagation; the original weights stay frozen. At inference, you merge the adapter weights back into the base model in a single forward pass—no runtime cost. A 70B-parameter model that needs 500GB of VRAM for full fine-tuning requires only 80GB with LoRA rank 16. Quality loss is typically under 1 percentage point on benchmarks.
why_it_matters: LoRA made fine-tuning accessible to individual developers on consumer GPUs. Before LoRA, adapting a 7B+ model required a data center. Now every major open-source fine-tune uses LoRA or variants. This unlocked rapid iteration on custom tasks—customer support bots, domain-specific assistants, instruction-following variants—without prohibitive infrastructure costs.
example: A team fine-tunes Llama 3.2 (3B) on 10,000 customer support conversations using an RTX 4090 in 4 hours with Unsloth. They set rank=16, train only query/key/value projections, and merge the adapter back into the base model. Total VRAM: 24GB. Cost: ~$0.30 in compute. Inference speed: identical to the base model. Without LoRA, they'd need a multi-GPU setup or cloud cluster.
pitfalls: LoRA does not reduce inference latency once merged—people confuse adapter size with speed; merged adapters run at base-model speed.
Rank is not 'more is better'—rank 16 or 32 works for most tasks; higher ranks increase memory and don't always improve quality.
Adapter placement matters—targeting only q_proj and v_proj saves memory but loses expressiveness compared to targeting all four projections.
LoRA assumes your base model is already strong; if the base model can't do the task, LoRA won't fix it, only specialize what's there.
when_use: Use LoRA when you have a strong pretrained model, limited VRAM or budget, and want to adapt it for a specific task or domain in hours rather than days.
when_avoid: Avoid LoRA if you need to fundamentally change model behavior (e.g., making a base model multilingual from scratch), or if your base model already underperforms on your task.

faq.q: What is LoRA?
faq.a: LoRA is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that trains only small adapter matrices instead of all model weights, reducing memory by 10–100x with minimal quality loss.

faq.q: Does LoRA slow down inference?
faq.a: No. Once the adapter is merged into the base model, inference runs at the same speed as the original model with zero latency overhead.

faq.q: What rank should I use for LoRA?
faq.a: Rank 16–32 works for most tasks. Lower rank saves memory but limits expressiveness; higher rank rarely improves quality enough to justify the cost.

faq.q: Can I use LoRA on any model?
faq.a: Yes, but LoRA works best on models with attention layers (transformers). The base model must already be reasonably capable at your target task.

faq.q: How much memory does LoRA actually save?
faq.a: For a 70B model, full fine-tuning needs ~500GB VRAM; LoRA with rank 16 needs ~80GB. Exact savings depend on rank, target modules, and batch size.

meta_title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Fine-tune large language models by training only small adapter matrices instead of all weights, using 10–100x less memory.

schema_jsonld.name: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
schema_jsonld.description: Fine-tune large language models by training only small adapter matrices instead of all weights, using 10–100x less memory.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability
tldr: Reverse-engineering neural networks into human-readable algorithms by identifying specific circuits that perform identifiable tasks.
plain_english: Mechanistic interpretability treats a trained language model like disassembled software—instead of treating it as a black box, you trace which neurons and attention heads perform which operations. It's detective work: you find the circuits that handle copying tokens, suppressing outputs, or finding indirect objects, mapping the model's actual computation rather than guessing from external behavior.
how_it_works: You start with a trained model and apply techniques like activation patching (zeroing or replacing neuron outputs to see what breaks), causal tracing (working backward from outputs to inputs), and sparse autoencoders (decomposing activations into interpretable features). By systematically disabling parts and measuring the effect on outputs, you identify functional units—a head that copies preceding tokens, neurons that detect repeated patterns, feature combinations that solve specific tasks. These circuits are usually local and probabilistic: one pathway among many the model uses, not the sole mechanism.
why_it_matters: This is the most concrete path to auditing what LLMs actually do at the algorithm level, not just predicting their outputs. It's essential for safety certification, debugging unintended behaviors, and understanding failure modes before deployment.
example: Anthropic's Circuits research identified specific attention heads in GPT-2 that implement 'induction heads'—they detect when a token sequence repeats and predict the next token in the pattern. By patching these heads out, they confirmed these circuits causally drive in-context learning. This kind of finding lets you understand whether a model's learning ability comes from genuine pattern recognition or spurious correlation.
pitfalls: Mech interp findings are usually local: identifying a circuit that solves a task does not prove the model relies on only that circuit for that task.
Circuits are probabilistic and context-dependent—a head that 'does' something in one setup may be dormant or redundant in another.
Scaling from toy models (GPT-2) to billion-parameter LLMs remains unsolved; interpretability claims don't automatically transfer.
Sparse autoencoders trade off interpretability for coverage—they may miss rare but important features or over-decompose behavior into noise.
when_use: Use mech interp when you need to debug specific misbehaviors, audit model safety properties, or validate claims about what a model computes before deployment.
when_avoid: Avoid mech interp as a general model understanding tool for pre-deployment go/no-go decisions; it's too slow and uncertain for rapid iteration or black-box performance optimization.

faq.q: What is the difference between mechanistic interpretability and probing?
faq.a: Probing trains a classifier on internal activations to measure what information is present; mech interp directly traces causal circuits to show how the model computes outputs. Probing asks 'is this feature there?' Mech interp asks 'does this circuit cause this behavior?'

faq.q: Can mechanistic interpretability work on large language models?
faq.a: Partially. It scales to mid-size models (GPT-2, small Transformers). For billion-parameter models, bottlenecks include compute cost, noise in sparse autoencoders, and redundancy in circuits. Research is ongoing but it's not yet a standard audit tool for frontier models.

faq.q: How is activation patching different from causal tracing?
faq.a: Activation patching directly edits neuron outputs to measure causal impact. Causal tracing attributes changes in outputs back to changes in earlier layers using gradients. Patching is more direct; tracing is more efficient for large models but less precise.

faq.q: Does finding a circuit mean the model always uses it?
faq.a: No. A circuit that solves a task in one context may be dormant or replaced by alternative pathways in other contexts. Models learn redundant solutions; identifying one does not exclude others.

faq.q: What tools do researchers use for mechanistic interpretability?
faq.a: TransformerLens is the standard for PyTorch models—it lets you hook activations and run causal interventions. Sparse autoencoders use SAE-Lens. Some teams build custom patching scripts; the field is pre-standardized.

meta_title: Mechanistic Interpretability — Plain-English Definition & Expert Guide | OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Reverse-engineer neural networks into human-readable algorithms by identifying specific circuits that perform identifiable tasks.

schema_jsonld.name: Mechanistic Interpretability
schema_jsonld.description: Reverse-engineering neural networks into human-readable algorithms by identifying specific circuits that perform identifiable tasks.

title: Medusa Decoding
tldr: Self-speculative decoding that adds prediction heads to one model, achieving 2–3× speedup without a draft model.
plain_english: Medusa attaches extra token-prediction heads directly to your main LLM. Instead of running two models in parallel (one fast drafter, one slow verifier), you train a lightweight adapter on the existing model that predicts multiple future tokens at once. These predictions are checked in parallel, cutting inference time without the operational overhead of managing two separate models.
how_it_works: During training, you add K decoding heads to the target model's hidden states using LoRA-style fine-tuning (typically a few hours on a single GPU). At inference, a single forward pass produces predictions for positions t+1, t+2, ..., t+K from each head. These predictions form a tree-structured candidate set verified in parallel using tree attention, accepting multiple correct tokens per iteration. Only rejected branches trigger additional forward passes, reducing total compute.
why_it_matters: Speculative decoding normally requires running two models—a small drafter and large verifier—adding operational complexity, memory overhead, and serving latency. Medusa collapses this into one model, making acceleration accessible in production without managing multiple inference pipelines or model checkpoints.
example: A team serving Llama 2 70B via vLLM adds Medusa heads trained for 3 hours on A100s. At inference, each token request gets 4-token lookahead predictions verified in one tree-attention pass. Median time-to-first-token stays unchanged; throughput on longer sequences improves 2.5×. No second model required, no scheduling complexity.
pitfalls: Training heads on a frozen base model transfers poorly to quantized or LoRA-adapted versions; retrain heads after any model changes.
Tree attention verification is still overhead—gains shrink on very short sequences (fewer than 10 tokens) where speculative decoding adds latency.
Head predictions quality degrades significantly if base model has been heavily fine-tuned on out-of-distribution tasks; validation on your actual use case is critical.
when_use: Use Medusa when you need speculative-decoding gains, already control the model serving infrastructure, and want to avoid running dual models.
when_avoid: Avoid Medusa if you depend on third-party inference APIs (no way to train heads), need single-token latency guarantees, or are serving models smaller than 7B parameters where overhead dominates.

faq.q: How is Medusa different from speculative decoding?
faq.a: Speculative decoding runs two models (drafter + verifier). Medusa adds lightweight heads to one model, eliminating the second model entirely while delivering similar speedups.

faq.q: How long does it take to train Medusa heads?
faq.a: Typically 2–4 hours on a single A100 using LoRA. Training cost is a small fraction of fine-tuning the base model.

faq.q: Will Medusa work if I quantize my model?
faq.a: No—quantization changes activations, so heads trained on full-precision activations will give bad predictions. Retrain heads after quantization.

faq.q: What speedup should I expect?
faq.a: 2–3× on typical long sequences, but gains depend on task and model. Short sequences and very high-quality base models see smaller improvements.

faq.q: Does Medusa work with prompt caching?
faq.a: Yes, they're complementary. Prompt caching reduces prefill cost; Medusa speeds up the decode phase.

meta_title: Medusa Decoding — Self-Speculative Inference Without Draft Models | OSS AI Hub
